This placement ensures that after users review info and configure options, their eye naturally lands on this action area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains a standard, non-sticky block within the right column. It expands vertically with content but is designed to stay relatively compact. On narrower screens, it will stack under the options form, preserving sequence and spacing. It interacts with the add-to-cart-feedback-banner logically: submitting the form may trigger the banner above the grid without changing the form’s position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-feedback-banner appears as a slim, full-width message bar at the top of the centered main content container, immediately below the white page-title band and above the breadcrumb and two-column grid. It aligns with the content container edges rather than the full browser width, giving the impression of a controlled, in-page notification. Horizontally, it spans 100% of the container; vertically, it is relatively shallow—about 40–64px tall depending on message length, with 12–16px padding above and below the text. The banner contrasts with the surrounding dark background—often using a tinted or slightly lighter dark strip—so that success or error messages are clearly visible but not visually heavier than the page title band. A 16–24px vertical gap sits below the banner before the breadcrumb nav begins, preserving the page’s generous vertical rhythm. When inactive, the banner’s space collapses entirely, allowing the breadcrumb to sit closer to the title band and keeping the layout clean.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears conditionally as a block element within the document flow. When visible, it pushes the breadcrumb and main grid downward; when hidden, it relinquishes its vertical space without leaving gaps. It is not sticky; it scrolls off-screen as the user moves down the page, acting as a transient but noticeable feedback element in the upper portion of the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-loading-error-state occupies the central area of the page where the main two-column content grid would normally appear. When triggered (e.g., product failed to load), it replaces or overlays the media viewer, tracklist, and the entire right-column stack, while leaving the global header, white page-title band, and possibly the breadcrumb intact to preserve orientation. Visually, it appears as a centered, medium-width panel (roughly 50–60% of the content container, centered horizontally) floating within the dark background of the main content area. Generous vertical margins (48–64px) above and below keep it surrounded by empty space, emphasizing its importance. The panel itself uses 24–32px of internal padding, with an icon or headline at the top and explanatory text and a retry/back action beneath, arranged in a simple vertical stack. This creates a clear focal point in the middle of the screen when something goes wrong, without the distraction of the normal product layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: When active, it either fully replaces the main content grid in the document flow or appears as an overlay above it with a higher stacking order, while the underlying grid is hidden or visually dimmed. The panel is vertically scrollable only if its content exceeds viewport height, but is intended to remain relatively compact. It is static relative to scroll; the user can scroll past it only if the page height demands it, but at initial load it is centered and dominant.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Shows all items the user is preparing to purchase, allows editing quantities or removing items, and provides totals and actions to continue shopping or proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load and display the current cart contents and monetary totals using getCartSummary when the page is opened or refreshed.
Render each cart item (products and tickets) with name, unit price, quantity, line total, and any selected options such as size or format.
Allow users to change the quantity of any cart item and persist the change via updateCartItemQuantity, then refresh the cart summary.
Allow users to remove individual cart items via removeCartItem, then refresh the cart summary and handle the empty-cart state.
Display subtotal, taxTotal (if provided), discountTotal (if provided), and total using data from getCartSummary so users can verify their spend.
Provide navigation controls to continue shopping (store.html) or proceed to checkout (checkout.html), and a contextual link from hoodie items to store.html with categoryId=apparel&subtype=hoodie.
user_workflows: View cart contents: 1) User navigates to cart.html from the header or a product/event page. 2) On page load, the cart page calls getCartSummary with no parameters. 3) The page renders the list of items and totals, or an empty-cart state if getCartSummary returns no items.

Update an item quantity: 1) User adjusts the quantity control on a specific cart item. 2) The cart page validates the new quantity on the client (e.g., positive integer) and calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and the new quantity. 3) On success, the controller component triggers getCartSummary to refresh all items and totals. 4) The updated cart item row and totals are re-rendered; an optional highlight is applied if the affected item matches the highlightItemId URL parameter.

Remove an item from the cart: 1) User clicks the remove control on a cart item row. 2) The cart page calls removeCartItem with the cartItemId of that line. 3) If success is true, the controller calls getCartSummary to retrieve the updated cart contents. 4) If remainingItemsCount is zero (or getCartSummary returns no items), the page switches to an empty-cart state. 5) The URL may be updated to set lastAction=removed and lastItemId=<cartItemId> so a feedback banner can show a contextual message on reload.

Navigate to checkout: 1) After reviewing the cart items and totals, the user clicks the 'Proceed to Checkout' button. 2) The button triggers a navigation to checkout.html without additional API calls from the cart page. 3) Optional: if a highlightItemId URL parameter is present, it is not propagated by default; checkout.html receives a clean URL unless business rules specify otherwise.

Continue shopping from cart: 1) User clicks the generic 'Continue Shopping' link in the cart actions bar. 2) The link navigates to store.html with no query parameters by default. 3) Optional: if the application has set a from=<value> parameter on cart.html, the cart page can decide whether to append additional query parameters when constructing the continue-shopping URL; otherwise, it uses store.html.

Add another hoodie from a hoodie line item: 1) For a cart line identified by business rules as a hoodie product, the item row displays a contextual link 'Add another hoodie'. 2) The user clicks this link. 3) The browser navigates to store.html?categoryId=apparel&subtype=hoodie; no APIs are called from the cart page as part of this navigation.
interactions: Page load interaction: When cart.html loads, a controller component triggers getCartSummary; the response is bound to the items list and totals components. If a highlightItemId URL parameter is present, that item is flagged for visual emphasis by the items list component.

Quantity change via stepper buttons: When the user clicks plus or minus controls on a cart item row, the item row component computes the prospective quantity, validates it, and emits an onItemQuantityUpdate event with cartItemId and the new quantity; the controller then calls updateCartItemQuantity and, on success, calls getCartSummary to refresh the UI.

Quantity change via direct input: When the user types a number into the quantity field and blurs the input or presses Enter, the item row validates the value (e.g., integer greater than zero). If valid and different from the current quantity, it emits onItemQuantityUpdate; invalid values cause the field to revert to the last known valid quantity without calling updateCartItemQuantity.

Remove item: When the user clicks the remove control for a cart item, the item row emits onItemRemove with the cartItemId. The controller calls removeCartItem and, if success is true, calls getCartSummary to update the items list and totals. If the API call fails or success is false, a feedback component shows an error message and the cart list is not modified on the client.

Proceed to checkout: When the user clicks the 'Proceed to Checkout' button in the actions bar, a navigation event is triggered to checkout.html. No cart APIs are called as part of this interaction; it assumes the cart state is already up to date.

Continue shopping: When the user clicks the 'Continue Shopping' link, the browser navigates to store.html. Optional URL parameters can be included based on existing cart.html query parameters if business rules require, but by default no additional parameters are added.

Add another hoodie: When the contextual 'Add another hoodie' link is present on a hoodie cart item and the user clicks it, the page performs a navigation to store.html?categoryId=apparel&subtype=hoodie. No cart updates or API calls occur as part of this interaction.

Feedback banner dismissal: After a quantity update or removal, the controller may update the URL to include lastAction and lastItemId. A feedback component reads these parameters on load and shows a message; when the user dismisses the banner, the component updates the URL (e.g., via history.replaceState) to remove lastAction and lastItemId without reloading the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The cart.html page does not require URL parameters to function but can use them to manage UI state instead of relying on localStorage. Supported patterns include: 1) highlightItemId=<cartItemId>: When present, the cart items list highlights the matching item after getCartSummary returns. This parameter can be set by other pages when redirecting to cart.html after adding an item. 2) lastAction=<value> and lastItemId=<cartItemId>: After a successful updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem call, the controller may optionally update the URL query string to record the last action (e.g., 'updated' or 'removed') and the affected cartItemId. On subsequent page loads, the feedback banner component reads these parameters to show contextual messages; when the banner is dismissed, it clears them via a URL update. 3) from=<source>: External pages may append a from parameter when linking to cart.html (for example, from=store or from=events). The cart actions bar can inspect this parameter to decide how to configure the 'Continue Shopping' link; if from is absent or unrecognized, it defaults to store.html without additional parameters. No cart data (items or prices) is persisted in the URL; all cart content comes from getCartSummary, and any change in cart contents via updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem is followed by a getCartSummary call to keep the UI synchronized with server state.

components.functionality: On page load and on explicit refresh requests, this component calls getCartSummary, transforms the response into the cart page view-model, and passes the data to the items list, totals panel, and actions bar. It also reads URL parameters such as highlightItemId, lastAction, lastItemId, and from to seed UI state for other components.

Renders the collection of cart items returned by getCartSummary, handles the empty-cart state when no items are present, and coordinates highlighting based on the highlightItemId URL parameter. It delegates per-item interactions (quantity changes, removal, contextual navigation) to child item row components and aggregates their events for the controller.

Represents a single line item in the cart, whether a product or a ticket. It displays the item name, any ticketTypeName and eventName for ticket items, unitPrice, quantity, lineTotal, and selected options such as selectedSize and selectedFormat. It provides quantity controls (stepper and/or numeric input), a remove action, and, when business rules determine the item is a hoodie product, a contextual 'Add another hoodie' link that navigates to store.html with categoryId=apparel and subtype=hoodie.

Listens for quantity update and remove item events from the cart-items-list and cart-item-row components. For quantity changes, it calls updateCartItemQuantity with the provided cartItemId and quantity, evaluates the success flag, and then calls getCartSummary to refresh the UI with updated items and totals. For remove actions, it calls removeCartItem, checks the success flag and remainingItemsCount, and calls getCartSummary to re-render the list and totals or switch to an empty state. It optionally updates URL parameters such as lastAction and lastItemId to drive the feedback banner.

Displays monetary totals based on data from getCartSummary, including subtotal, taxTotal (if provided), discountTotal (if provided), and total. After any cart modification via updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem, the controller triggers a fresh getCartSummary call and passes the updated totals to this component.

Provides primary navigation actions from the cart: a 'Continue Shopping' link that navigates to store.html and a 'Proceed to Checkout' button that navigates to checkout.html. It may inspect the from URL parameter to adjust the destination of the 'Continue Shopping' link if required by business rules; otherwise, it defaults to store.html. These actions do not invoke cart APIs and rely on the cart state already managed by other components.

Reads URL parameters such as lastAction and lastItemId to show contextual feedback messages about the most recent cart action (e.g., item removed or quantity updated) after getCartSummary has rendered the current state. It can also display error messages when the cart-update-controller reports API failures. When the user dismisses the banner, it updates the URL (without reloading) to remove feedback-related parameters.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: full-width dark header (logo + nav) at the very top, followed by a full-width white page-title band with the word 'CART'. Below that, place a centered main content container on the dark, textured background. Inside this container, use a two-column layout: a wide left column for the cart items list and a narrower right column for the cart totals panel. Global feedback (e.g., cart status messages) sits just inside the main container, above the two columns and spanning their combined width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: full-width dark header (logo + nav) at the very top, followed by a full-width white page-title band with the word 'CART'. Below that, place a centered main content container on the dark, textured background. Inside this container, use a two-column layout: a wide left column for the cart items list and a narrower right column for the cart totals panel. Global feedback (e.g., cart status messages) sits just inside the main container, above the two columns and spanning their combined width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a relatively shallow header and a generously tall white title band (roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height). The main content container beneath should occupy the bulk of the page height. Inside the container, allocate about 65–70% of the width to the left column (cart items) and 30–35% to the right column (totals). Internal padding within modules follows the 16–24px standard, with 32–48px vertical gaps between major bands (header → title band → main content, and title band → main container).
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the header and page-title band spacious and minimal, with lots of breathing room. The main content area is more content-dense but still clearly structured: the cart items list uses strong horizontal alignment and consistent row spacing, while the totals panel is compact but well-padded like a sidebar widget. Vertical spacing of 16–24px separates logical blocks: between the feedback banner and the columns, between the items list and the actions bar, and between the totals panel and any secondary text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the F-pattern: the eye starts at the top-left logo and sweeps across the header nav, then drops to the centered 'CART' title in the white band. From there, the left column (cart items list) captures primary attention; the user scans down the list of items row by row. Once the list is understood, attention shifts right to the cart totals panel, which functions like the sidebar modules in the mockup. Finally, the gaze returns to the lower-left for the cart actions bar, where primary decision buttons (continue shopping / checkout) are located.

layout.overall_description:
The page sits on a dark, slightly textured background with a gritty, indie-band feel. A full-width, relatively low-height dark header anchors the top, with the hand-drawn logo on the left and navigation links aligned horizontally to the right, spaced with tight 8–12px padding. Immediately below, a bright white page-title band spans the width of the viewport, creating strong contrast against the dark header and background. The word 'CART' appears in bold, uppercase text, centered vertically within this band, with generous top and bottom padding so it feels airy and important.

Below the title band, a centered fixed-width main content container (roughly 960–1200px wide) sits on the dark textured background, separated from the title band by about 32–48px of vertical space. At the very top of this container, a feedback banner—when present—runs across its full width. Beneath it, the container splits into two columns: a wide left column occupying about two-thirds of the width, and a narrower right column taking the remaining third. The left column holds a vertically stacked cart items list, with each row aligned in a clean horizontal grid: product thumbnail on the far left, details next, quantity/update controls toward the right, and prices right-aligned. The right column mirrors the sidebar pattern from the mockup, housing a single, prominent cart totals panel styled like a dark, padded card. Below the items list in the left column, a horizontal cart actions bar spans the column, placing primary and secondary action buttons side-by-side. The overall effect is a modern, minimalist, band-branded cart page that feels both polished and raw, guiding the user from title to items, then to totals and actions in a clear F-shaped reading pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart initializer appears only during data loading and overlays the main content container that sits below the white 'CART' title band. It covers both the left and right columns, stretching from the top edge of the container (just below the feedback banner area) down to at least the fold of the screen, effectively dimming or skeletonizing the cart items list and totals panel beneath. A central loading indicator or skeleton block is positioned slightly left-of-center, aligned with the main left column, reinforcing that the primary content being prepared is the items list. Padding to the container edges follows the standard 16–24px so the overlay respects the same content margins as the rest of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-overlay layer within the main content container, positioned above all other cart components with a semi-transparent dark background; disappears entirely once the cart data has loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart items list sits in the left column of the main content container, beginning just below the feedback banner (or, if no banner, about 32px below the bottom of the white title band). It occupies approximately 65–70% of the container’s width and runs from the top of the column downwards, forming a vertical stack of cart-item-rows. The list content is inset from the column edges with 16–24px of internal padding, giving each row breathing room while still feeling dense enough for a cart. Each row is separated by 16px vertical spacing, and the list can extend beyond the initial viewport height, scrolling naturally while maintaining a fixed relationship to the totals panel on the right. The left edge of the list aligns vertically with the page title above, reinforcing the strong left-hand reading line from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as part of the left column; height is flexible to fit any number of items, with internal rows flowing vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart item row is a horizontally structured band inside the cart items list, spanning nearly the full width of the left column’s content area. From left to right, the row is divided into logical segments: a small product thumbnail bloc on the far left occupying roughly 15–20% of the row width; next to it, a product information block (title, variant, perhaps small meta) taking about 35–45%; then a quantity and update control segment (containing the cart-update-controller) occupying around 20–25%; and finally, a price/subtotal segment on the far right, right-aligned, occupying the remaining width. Vertical padding of about 12–16px above and below the row content keeps each item distinct without feeling bulky. The row background remains dark, with subtle separators (thin lines or slight tonal differences) between rows to echo the minimalist, high-contrast aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically as a repeated element inside the cart items list; width adapts to the left column, with internal segments using a responsive horizontal grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart update controller sits within each cart item row, in the quantity/control segment toward the right side of the row. It is horizontally centered in that segment, with a numeric quantity field or display in the middle and small increment/decrement controls to its left and right, or directly above/below in a compact stack depending on viewport width. The controller’s total footprint is modest—about 20–25% of the row width—leaving space to its right for the price/subtotal. Vertical alignment is balanced so that the controller sits on the same baseline as the main product info, giving the row a cohesive horizontal rhythm. There is tight internal padding (8–12px) around the control elements to keep it compact and visually aligned with the band’s minimal, utilitarian aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays anchored to the right-center portion of each cart item row; does not float independently and reflows with the row’s responsive grid when the viewport width changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart totals panel occupies the right column of the main content container, mirroring the sidebar module structure from the mockup. It begins near the top of the right column, horizontally aligned with the top of the cart items list in the left column. The panel takes up roughly 30–35% of the container’s width and is inset with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, forming a dark, card-like block that stands off from the textured background with subtle contrast. Inside, totals rows (subtotal, shipping, taxes, grand total) are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing, and the grand total is visually emphasized at the bottom of the panel. The panel’s width is fixed relative to the container, so as the viewport widens, it stays visually compact and sidebar-like, reinforcing the secondary but important nature of the order summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; scrolls vertically with the page alongside the cart items list, maintaining its position relative to the top of the main content container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart actions bar is positioned directly below the cart items list in the left column, separated from the last item row by about 24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the left column’s content area, aligning with the left and right edges of the cart items list. Inside the bar, the primary action (e.g., 'Proceed to Checkout') is aligned to the right, while secondary actions (e.g., 'Continue Shopping', 'Update Cart') are aligned to the left or center, depending on the number of buttons. The bar itself can be a subtle, slightly elevated strip or simply a horizontally arranged cluster of buttons with 8–16px spacing between them. Vertical padding of 12–16px ensures the actions feel substantial and clickable while remaining consistent with the minimalist, band-branded style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored at the bottom of the left column’s content; moves down the page as the cart items list grows and scrolls with the page, maintaining its relative position under the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart feedback banner sits at the top of the main content container, immediately below the white 'CART' title band and above the split into left and right columns. It spans the full width of the centered container (matching the combined width of both columns), creating a horizontal strip that is visually separate from both the title band above and the main content below. The banner has a modest height, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding, enough to make messages like 'Item removed from cart' or error notices clearly legible. Its background color contrasts against the dark main background (for example, a slightly lighter dark or a muted accent color), but remains in line with the edgy, minimalist aesthetic. There is about 16px of vertical space below the banner before the two-column layout begins, maintaining clear separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally; when present, it pushes the two-column content (cart items and totals panel) downward but remains fixed in its zone at the top of the main content container, scrolling away with the page like any other content block.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Review the final order, enter billing and delivery information, and place the order for the current cart contents.

core_features: Load and display the current cart’s checkout summary using getCheckoutSummary, including all items and monetary totals.
Show a structured list of checkout items (merch and tickets) with quantities and line totals, read-only at this stage.
Display an order totals block (subtotal, tax, discounts, total) bound to the checkout summary data.
Provide a checkout form to capture billingName, billingEmail, billingAddress, and deliveryDetails required by placeOrder.
Validate required billing and delivery fields on the client before submitting the order.
Create an order using placeOrder and display the returned orderId, status, total, and currency as confirmation on this page.
Allow users to navigate back to cart.html for edits via a dedicated 'Back to Cart' control.
Handle loading and error states for both getCheckoutSummary and placeOrder and expose them to child components.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load checkout summary and review items
1. User navigates from cart.html to checkout.html via a link or button.
2. On initial page load, the checkout-page-controller component invokes getCheckoutSummary with no parameters.
3. While waiting for the response, the controller exposes a loading state so the order-summary-panel can show a loading indicator instead of item data.
4. Once getCheckoutSummary returns, the controller stores the returned cartId, currency, items, subtotal, taxTotal, discountTotal, and total in in-memory state.
5. The order-summary-panel component renders the items and totals based on the bound summary data.
6. If getCheckoutSummary fails, the controller sets an error state that is displayed by an error-display section within the order-summary-panel.

Workflow: Enter billing and delivery details
1. After reviewing the summary, the user scrolls to the checkout-form component.
2. The user fills in billingName, billingEmail, billingAddress, and deliveryDetails in the form fields.
3. On each change, the checkout-form updates its local in-memory form state (not persisted to localStorage) and clears field-level validation errors for that field.
4. When the user clicks the form submit button, the checkout-form triggers onSubmit, which passes the form values to the checkout-page-controller.
5. The controller validates that the required fields are present and properly formatted (for example, non-empty name and a syntactically valid email). If validation fails, the controller returns a validation result and the checkout-form displays inline error messages without calling placeOrder.
6. If validation passes, the controller proceeds to the order placement workflow.

Workflow: Place order and view confirmation
1. After client-side validation succeeds, the checkout-page-controller calls placeOrder with the collected billingName, billingEmail, billingAddress, and deliveryDetails values from the checkout-form.
2. While waiting for the response, the controller sets an orderSubmissionInProgress state so the checkout-form can disable the submit button and show a submission indicator.
3. When placeOrder returns successfully, the controller stores the returned orderId, status, total, and currency in an orderResult state object.
4. The order-status-banner component reads orderResult and displays a confirmation section including the orderId, status, and payment summary.
5. Following a successful order, the controller can optionally adjust internal flags (for example, mark the cart as no longer editable on this page) so the checkout-form can hide or disable the submit button to prevent duplicate orders.
6. If placeOrder fails, the controller sets an orderError state. The checkout-form and/or a shared error-display component reads this state and shows an error message, allowing the user to retry submission.

Workflow: Navigate back to cart
1. At any time before a successful order submission, the user can click the 'Back to Cart' control rendered by the navigation-controls component.
2. The navigation-controls component performs a navigation to cart.html, optionally preserving the current URL query parameters if relevant (for example, to maintain a return context).
3. The user then edits quantities or removes items in cart.html.
4. When the user again chooses to proceed to checkout from cart.html, they are redirected back to checkout.html, which re-runs getCheckoutSummary to reflect the updated cart.

Workflow: Manage UI step state via URL parameters
1. The checkout page reads an optional "step" query parameter from the URL (for example, checkout.html?step=details or checkout.html?step=confirm) as soon as the controller initializes.
2. If step is not provided or has an unexpected value, the controller defaults to a combined view where both order summary and checkout form are visible.
3. If step=details, the checkout-page-controller exposes a viewMode state that instructs child components to emphasize the billing/delivery form (for example, show the order-summary-panel in a collapsed or secondary mode).
4. If step=confirm, the controller configures the view so the order-summary-panel and a read-only view of the entered form data are emphasized; the checkout-form component can render in a review-only state if it already has valid data.
5. When the user clicks internal controls such as "Review & Confirm" or "Back to Details" within the checkout-form or navigation-controls, the controller updates the browser URL query parameter "step" via standard navigation or history APIs, without storing any state in localStorage.
6. All components derive their visibility and mode (for example, editable vs. read-only) from the current viewMode, which in turn is derived only from the URL parameter and in-memory state.
interactions: On page load, the checkout-page-controller automatically calls getCheckoutSummary with no arguments; this interaction is not user-initiated but is essential to populate the page.

Clicking the 'Back to Cart' button or link in the navigation-controls component triggers navigation to cart.html (optionally preserving query parameters such as step for contextual return if desired by the broader application).

Editing any billing or delivery input field in the checkout-form component triggers an onChange handler that updates the form’s in-memory state and clears any existing validation error for that field.

Submitting the checkout form via the 'Place Order' button triggers the checkout-form onSubmit handler, which delegates to the checkout-page-controller for validation and, upon success, for calling placeOrder.

If client-side validation fails on submit, the controller returns a validation summary to the checkout-form, which highlights invalid fields and prevents calling placeOrder until corrections are made.
While the order submission is in progress, the checkout-form disables its submit button and may disable its fields based on a bound isSubmitting state exposed by the controller.

If placeOrder succeeds, the checkout-page-controller updates the orderResult state, which the order-status-banner listens to; this causes the banner to render confirmation information without additional user interaction.
If placeOrder fails, a shared error state is set; the checkout-form and/or a dedicated error-display section reactively show the error, and the submit button is re-enabled so the user can retry.

Clicking any internal control that changes the logical step (such as a 'Go to Confirm Step' control inside the form) triggers an event handler in the controller that updates the URL query parameter step and adjusts the viewMode that children consume.
page_functionality.state_logic: The checkout page relies on in-memory state and URL query parameters; it does not use localStorage. Data state: When checkout.html is loaded, the checkout-page-controller requests the checkout summary via getCheckoutSummary. The returned object is stored as the canonical summary state (including cartId, currency, items, subtotal, taxTotal, discountTotal, and total). This summary state is passed to the order-summary-panel and other components needing pricing or currency information. Form state: The checkout-form maintains current values for billingName, billingEmail, billingAddress, and deliveryDetails in its own in-memory state, initialized as empty when the page loads. These values are only propagated to the controller during validation and submission. Order result state: After placeOrder is called, its response (orderId, status, total, currency) is stored in an orderResult object in the controller. Components such as order-status-banner bind to this orderResult and re-render when it changes. Error and loading state: The controller maintains separate flags and messages for summaryLoading, summaryError, orderSubmitting, and orderError; child components render differently based on these flags. URL parameter state: The page reads an optional step query parameter from the URL (for example, checkout.html?step=details or checkout.html?step=confirm). This parameter is mapped to a viewMode flag in the controller, which child components use to decide which sections to show or emphasize (for example, whether the form is editable or in confirmation mode). If the step parameter is absent or unknown, the controller defaults to a standard combined view mode. When the user changes steps via UI interactions, the controller updates the browser URL to set step to the appropriate value, ensuring that page state is reflected in the URL and can be restored on refresh without using localStorage. No business data (such as billingName or totals) is encoded in the URL; only view configuration like the current step is derived from query parameters.

components.functionality: Central controller for the checkout page. It initializes the page, calls getCheckoutSummary on load, maintains shared loading/error/order state, interprets URL parameters, and coordinates placeOrder calls triggered by the checkout form.

Displays a read-only list of items from the checkout summary and the aggregated monetary fields (subtotal, tax, discounts, total). Reacts to loading and error state from the controller and updates when the summary data changes.

Collects billingName, billingEmail, billingAddress, and deliveryDetails from the user. Performs basic client-side validation, then delegates to the controller’s onFormSubmit handler, which calls placeOrder if validation succeeds. It also reacts to order submission state (e.g., disabling inputs while submitting) and can switch between editable and confirmation modes based on the viewMode derived from URL parameters.

Shows the outcome of the order placement. When the controller updates the orderResult state after placeOrder returns, this component renders the orderId, status, and payment summary. It also responds to orderError state by displaying an error message when the order could not be created.

Provides navigation actions for the checkout page, including a control to return to cart.html. It can also include controls to change the logical step of the checkout (details vs. confirm) by updating the step query parameter via the controller.

Displays error messages related to loading the checkout summary or placing the order. It listens to error state provided by the controller and can render contextual messages, along with controls to retry operations such as reloading the summary or resubmitting the order.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s vertical stacking: a full-width dark header (handled globally), then a full-width white page-title band for the Checkout title. Below this, the checkout-page-controller centers a fixed-width main content container using a two-column layout. The left, wider column holds the checkout-form as the primary task flow, with the checkout-error-display anchored to its top. The right, narrower column acts as the sidebar and contains the order-summary-panel. The order-status-banner sits at the top of the main container, spanning both columns. The navigation-controls sit at the bottom of the left column, after the form sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s vertical stacking: a full-width dark header (handled globally), then a full-width white page-title band for the Checkout title. Below this, the checkout-page-controller centers a fixed-width main content container using a two-column layout. The left, wider column holds the checkout-form as the primary task flow, with the checkout-error-display anchored to its top. The right, narrower column acts as the sidebar and contains the order-summary-panel. The order-status-banner sits at the top of the main container, spanning both columns. The navigation-controls sit at the bottom of the left column, after the form sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a centered fixed-width content area (roughly 1120–1200px) inside the browser viewport. The top header and the white title band together occupy the upper 20–25% of the viewport height, with the title band itself taking around 15–20%. The main content container fills the remaining vertical space. Within it, the left main column occupies about 65–70% of the content width; the right sidebar with the order-summary-panel occupies about 30–35%, separated by a 24px gutter. Vertical spacing between major blocks (title band, status banner, main content, and the final navigation-controls cluster) uses 32–48px margins; spacing inside components and between sub-sections uses 16–24px padding/margins.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep the header and title band very spacious, with lots of empty white space around the Checkout title to echo the mockup. Inside the main content, the layout becomes denser around the form fields and order summary, but each block (status banner, errors, form sections, summary sections, and navigation controls) still has clear padding and separation. The form and summary should feel information-rich but not cramped, using 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px spacing between logical groups of fields or line items.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the F-pattern from the mockup: the user’s eye moves from the band logo and navigation in the dark header to the right, then drops to the bold Checkout title in the white band. From there, the eye moves down into the main content, first catching the full-width order-status-banner (when present), then following the left column down through checkout-error-display, form sections, and finally the navigation-controls. Secondary attention then moves horizontally to the right sidebar to review the order-summary-panel. The overall flow prioritizes completing the form, with constant peripheral awareness of the order summary.

layout.overall_description: The Checkout page sits on the same dark, slightly textured background as the rest of the band site, framed by a full-width, compact black header with the hand-drawn logo on the left and navigation on the right. Immediately beneath, a stark white title band stretches full width, with the word “CHECKOUT” set large and left-aligned in dark, high-contrast type, surrounded by generous vertical padding that leaves plenty of white breathing space. Below this band, the main content snaps into a centered, fixed-width container floating on the dark background. At the very top of this container, a full-width order-status-banner appears when needed, like a dark strip with accent color that sits above the two-column layout. Under it, the content splits into a 70/30 two-column grid: the left, wider column holds the entire checkout process—errors at the top, then the stacked form sections—while the right, narrower sidebar column presents a compact, card-like order summary. The left column flows vertically, guiding the user step-by-step down the form and ending with prominent navigation-controls (Back/Place Order) aligned near the bottom. The right order summary remains visually cohesive and slightly inset, echoing the sidebar treatment from the mockup, so users always see what they’re paying for while completing the form. The overall effect is a modern, dark, band-ready checkout that balances edgy aesthetics with a clean, orderly structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-page-controller acts as the structural frame for the entire page’s main content, beginning immediately below the full-width white Checkout title band. It defines a centered fixed-width container (roughly 1120–1200px wide) on the textured black background, with equal left and right margins so the content feels like a single, solid block in the middle of the screen. Inside this container, it first reserves a full-width horizontal strip for the order-status-banner. Beneath that, it divides the remaining width into a two-column grid: the left main column (about 65–70% of the container width) is aligned flush left inside the container and will house the checkout-error-display, checkout-form, and, at the bottom, the navigation-controls. The right sidebar column (about 30–35% of the container width) is aligned to the right edge of the container, separated from the left column by a consistent 24px gutter, and is dedicated to the order-summary-panel. The controller enforces vertical rhythm: a 32–40px gap between the title band and the top of the status banner, 24–32px below the status banner before the columns, and 32–48px bottom padding below the navigation-controls so the content does not touch the viewport edge. It also ensures that both columns share the same top baseline (just below the status banner) and that the page respects the 8px-based spacing system throughout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, full-page layout wrapper for the main content, managing the two-column grid and vertical spacing without scrolling independently of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-status-banner is positioned at the very top of the main content container created by the checkout-page-controller, directly beneath the white Checkout title band and above the two-column layout. It spans the full width of the centered container, visually reading as a horizontal strip that cuts across both the left main column and the right sidebar. Vertically, it has 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, with a 24–32px margin below it before the columns begin. When visible, it should feel like a bold but not overwhelming notification: perhaps a dark charcoal background slightly lighter than the page backdrop, with a high-contrast accent edge or left border in a brand color (e.g., red for errors, green for success). Text within the banner is left-aligned, starting near the left edge of the container, following the F-pattern so users encounter it immediately when they enter the main area. If the banner is not active, the layout simply collapses that vertical space and the two-column layout rises to sit just below the title band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears as needed, always occupying a full-width horizontal band above both columns and pushing content down rather than overlaying it; it scrolls away with the rest of the page (not sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel sits in the right sidebar column of the main content area, aligned with the top of the checkout-form in the left column (i.e., starting just below the order-status-banner, if present). It occupies the full width of the sidebar column, which is roughly 30–35% of the container width, and is inset as a compact card with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. The panel should visually echo the sidebar modules from the mockup: a clear heading like “ORDER SUMMARY” at the top, followed by vertically stacked content—line items, quantities, prices, shipping costs, and the final total—with 8–16px spacing between rows. The card sits on a subtly lighter dark background than the page, separated from the main background with a thin border or soft shadow, and has 16–24px of margin below if additional sidebar content is ever added. Horizontally, it is positioned flush to the right edge of the main container, with a consistent 24px gutter between its left edge and the right edge of the checkout-form column. Its vertical height grows with the order content, but it should maintain at least 24px of bottom padding before the end of the main container so it doesn’t feel cut off.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar column by default, but can optionally become sticky within the viewport on taller pages—remaining visible as the user scrolls through the form—while respecting top and bottom padding so it doesn’t collide with the header or footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-form occupies the left main column of the two-column layout and is the primary vertical content stream of the page. It begins near the top of this column, immediately below the checkout-error-display (when errors exist) and horizontally aligned to the left edge of the container. The column itself spans roughly 65–70% of the container width, with the form filling almost all of that width, minus 16–24px internal padding on the sides to keep fields away from the column edges. The form is organized into clearly separated sections—e.g., Contact Details, Billing Address, Shipping Address, Payment Information—stacked vertically. Each section has a bold, all-caps or high-contrast heading at the top, with 8–16px of space below the heading and 16–24px between individual fields or field groups. Sections are separated from one another with 24–32px of vertical spacing, sometimes with a subtle dividing line or tonal shift to prevent visual clutter. Input fields are full-width within the column, aligned in a clean grid so labels and inputs produce a stable left edge. Above the form’s first section, there is room for a concise line indicating the current step (e.g., “Step 2 of 3”), if needed, using smaller, muted text. At the bottom of the form, after the last section’s fields, there is 24–32px of vertical space before the navigation-controls, which are visually attached but clearly distinct. The entire form area maintains ample breathing room from the bottom of the viewport and feels like the core, work-focused region of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally with the main page scroll within the left column, expanding vertically as needed based on the number of fields; it does not scroll independently but drives overall page height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: DUPLICATE ENTRY PLACEHOLDER
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: static

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-controls cluster is positioned at the bottom of the left main column, directly beneath the final section of the checkout-form. It spans horizontally within the same width as the form fields but occupies only a short band of vertical space. There is a 24–32px gap between the last form inputs and the top of the controls to clearly signal the transition from data entry to action. The controls group typically consists of two buttons: a secondary “Back to Cart” or “Continue Shopping” action on the left and a primary “Place Order” or “Complete Purchase” button on the right. The buttons are aligned along a single baseline, with the primary action visually emphasized through a stronger fill color (e.g., a bold accent) and slightly larger visual weight. On larger screens, the primary button is right-aligned to the column edge, with the secondary button to its left separated by 16–24px. The entire button row is centered vertically within a low-profile band, with 16–24px of bottom padding between the controls and the bottom edge of the main content container so the buttons don’t feel cramped against the viewport or footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the form’s content flow; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not remain fixed, ensuring users encounter it only after completing the necessary fields.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-error-display is anchored at the very top of the left main column, directly above the checkout-form and aligned flush with the left edge of the form content. It occupies the full width of the main column and appears as a compact yet noticeable alert block. Internally, it uses 16–24px of padding, with error text left-aligned and potentially a small icon on the far left. The background is a dark red or otherwise high-contrast error color that stands out against the charcoal form background, framed by either a thin border or a subtle glow to echo the edgy aesthetic. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of the error display and the first form section so the block feels clearly distinct but directly related to the fields below. When multiple errors are present, they are listed vertically with 8–12px spacing between lines. When there are no errors to show, the component collapses entirely, allowing the form’s first section to rise directly beneath the order-status-banner (if present). This placement ensures users see critical blocking issues in the main vertical path, right where their eyes naturally land after the title band and status banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears as needed at the top of the left column, pushing the form content down but not overlaying it; it scrolls with the form as part of the main content flow.

name: Discography / Albums
title: Discography
description: Displays all published band albums in a sortable list, providing navigation to album detail pages for tracklists and playlist building.

core_features: Retrieve and display a list of published albums using the listAlbums(sort) interface.
Support sorting of albums via a sort control that maps to the URL ?sort= parameter and re-invokes listAlbums with the selected mode.
Render albums in a grid or list with key metadata from the Album model (e.g., title, subtitle, coverImageUrl, releaseDate, totalTracks).
Allow users to navigate to an album detail page (album.html?id=ALBUM_ID) by clicking an album item.
Handle empty and error states when listAlbums returns no albums or fails, with a retry mechanism.
Maintain album listing state via the sort URL parameter so that users coming from index.html or returning from album.html see albums in the same order.
user_workflows:
Browse all albums:
1) User navigates to discography.html (from header navigation or a link on index.html).
2) The page controller reads the current URL parameter `sort` (if present).
3) The page controller invokes listAlbums(sort) with the parsed sort value or a default when missing/invalid.
4) When the albums load successfully, the album-grid component renders all Album records in a grid or list.
5) The user scrolls through the albums to review covers and basic information.

Change album ordering:
1) User interacts with the album-sort-control (e.g., selects a sort option from a dropdown).
2) The sort control emits an onChangeSort event with the newly selected sort value.
3) The page controller updates the browser URL’s `sort` query parameter (without adding any other parameters) to the new value.
4) The page controller re-invokes listAlbums(newSort) and updates the album-grid with the returned albums.
5) If the user uses the browser’s Back/Forward buttons, the page controller listens for URL changes and re-applies the `sort` value, reloading albums as needed.

Open an album and return for playlist building:
1) From the discography.html album-grid, the user clicks an album item.
2) The album-grid triggers navigation to album.html?id=ALBUM_ID using the Album.id from the bound item.
3) On album.html, the user can view the tracklist and perform playlist-related actions (handled on album.html).
4) The user returns to discography.html using the browser Back button or the site navigation.
5) When discography.html is shown again, the page controller reads the `sort` parameter from the URL and calls listAlbums(sort) so the album-grid is restored in the same ordering the user previously selected.
interactions: Page load: on initial load of discography.html, the page controller reads the `sort` URL parameter, calls listAlbums(sort), and passes the resulting Album array to the album-grid component.

Sort change: when the user selects a new sort option in the album-sort-control, it triggers an onChangeSort event, which the page controller handles by: updating the `sort` query parameter in the URL and re-calling listAlbums with the new sort value.

Album click: when the user clicks an album in the album-grid, the component triggers an onAlbumClick event that navigates the browser to album.html?id=ALBUM_ID, where ALBUM_ID is the clicked Album.id.

Empty state retry: if listAlbums returns an empty array or a load error occurs, the album-grid-empty-state component is shown with a retry action; when the user clicks retry, an onRetryClick event re-triggers listAlbums with the current `sort` parameter.

Browser navigation: when the user uses Back/Forward in the browser, the page controller listens to URL changes (onPopState), re-reads the `sort` parameter, and reloads albums via listAlbums(sort) to keep the view in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of persistent state for discography.html is the sorting mode, managed exclusively via the `sort` query parameter in the URL. On every page initialization and on any URL change, the page controller reads the current `sort` value from location.search. If `sort` is present and recognized (e.g., matches one of the supported sort modes), that value is passed as the argument to listAlbums(sort). If `sort` is missing or invalid, the controller uses an implementation-defined default sort mode and may normalize the URL by setting `sort` to that default. When the user changes sorting in the album-sort-control, the controller updates the `sort` parameter in the URL (using history.pushState or history.replaceState) and then re-invokes listAlbums with the new sort value, ensuring that the displayed albums and the URL remain consistent. No other state (such as selections or scroll position) is persisted via URL parameters, and no state is stored in localStorage; transient UI state is managed in memory only. Navigation to album.html passes only the album id via album.html?id=ALBUM_ID, and users returning to discography.html rely on the persisted `sort` parameter in the discography URL to restore the album ordering.

components.functionality: Initializes the Discography page, manages URL-driven state (sort), calls listAlbums(sort) to retrieve albums, coordinates rendering of the sort control and album grid, and handles reloads on sort changes or browser navigation events.

Provides user controls (e.g., a dropdown or buttons) to select the album sorting mode. Reflects the current `sort` parameter from the URL on initial render and emits events when the user selects a different sort option.

Displays the list of albums returned from listAlbums(sort) as a grid or list. For each Album, shows core metadata (such as title, subtitle, coverImageUrl, releaseDate, totalTracks) and exposes a clickable region that navigates to the album detail page.
Handles cases where listAlbums(sort) returns an empty array or fails. Shows an informative message about the absence of albums or the load error and offers a retry action to re-fetch albums.

Synchronizes the `sort` query parameter in the URL with the in-memory sorting state. Parses `sort` on load and on browser Back/Forward (popstate), validates it against supported sort modes, and notifies the page controller and album-sort-control of any changes.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: full-width site header at the very top, then a full-width white title band with the page title 'DISCOGRAPHY' / 'ALBUMS'. Below the title band sits a centered main content container on a dark textured background. Inside this container, a two-column layout is used: the left column is the main discography area (where all listed components live), and the right column follows the established sidebar pattern used across the site (search, recent posts, categories, etc.). Within the left column, the discography-page-controller wraps a vertical stack: the album-sort-control at the top, followed by either the album-grid or the album-grid-empty-state occupying the remaining space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: full-width site header at the very top, then a full-width white title band with the page title 'DISCOGRAPHY' / 'ALBUMS'. Below the title band sits a centered main content container on a dark textured background. Inside this container, a two-column layout is used: the left column is the main discography area (where all listed components live), and the right column follows the established sidebar pattern used across the site (search, recent posts, categories, etc.). Within the left column, the discography-page-controller wraps a vertical stack: the album-sort-control at the top, followed by either the album-grid or the album-grid-empty-state occupying the remaining space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header remains shallow but full-width. The white title band beneath it occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, with generous top and bottom padding so that the 'DISCOGRAPHY' title floats with breathing room. The main content container is centered and constrained (e.g., around 1120–1200px max-width) on a 12-column grid. The left discography column consumes about 65–70% of this width; the right sidebar occupies 30–35%. Inside the left column, the album-sort-control is a low-height strip at the top with about 24–32px of vertical padding, while the album-grid consumes most of the vertical space below, with cards arranged in a responsive multi-column grid. Vertical gaps between primary blocks (title band → sort control → grid) follow the 32–48px section margin; gaps between internal elements (grid rows, tile content) follow the 16–24px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The top of the page (header, title band) stays airy and minimal, with large zones of empty space around the logo, nav, and page title. Once in the main content area, density increases but remains controlled: the sort control is compact and efficient, and the album-grid presents many items without feeling cluttered by using consistent gutters and clear card boundaries. Each album tile has sufficient padding and breathing room (around 16–24px internal padding with 16px gutters between tiles), letting album covers remain visually prominent. The empty state follows the spacious treatment from the title band, centering its message and icon with generous padding so the absence of content feels intentional and calm.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye follows the same F-pattern observed in the mockup. First, the eye hits the band logo at the top-left of the header, then scans right across the navigation. It then drops vertically to the high-contrast page title in the white band. From there, attention flows down into the main content container, landing first on the album-sort-control at the top-left of the main column. The sort options then lead the eye downward into the album-grid, where album artwork tiles form the primary visual focus. The right sidebar is visually secondary and is scanned once the main album grid is understood. In the empty-state scenario, the eye moves from title band down to the centered empty-state message in the main column, bypassing the grid entirely.

layout.overall_description: The Discography page sits on a dark, slightly textured background with a full-width, thin header bar at the top containing the hand-drawn band logo on the left and the navigation on the right. Immediately beneath, a bold white title band stretches across the full width of the browser, with the page title 'DISCOGRAPHY' (or 'ALBUMS') set in large, high-contrast type and ample top and bottom padding so the text feels almost suspended in space. Below this band, the content narrows into a centered container that rests on the dark background, divided into a wider left column and a narrower right sidebar. The left column is the functional heart of the page: at its top, a lean strip houses the sorting controls for the discography, aligned with the left edge of the column and framed by comfortable vertical spacing. Directly under this strip, the album-grid fills the column with rows of album tiles—each tile dominated by its album cover, forming a strong visual wall of artwork that echoes the large hero image from the mockup. The spacing between tiles and rows is consistent, ensuring the grid feels organized rather than noisy. If there are no albums for the current filters, the grid area is replaced by a centered empty-state panel that sits in the same left column, with a simple icon and message framed by generous negative space. Throughout, the right sidebar retains its vertical stack of modules, visually lighter and more compact, providing secondary navigation and content. As the user scrolls, the dark, edgy aesthetic and disciplined grid keep the experience coherent and on-brand, while the clear hierarchy directs attention from header to title band to the discography controls and artwork.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discography-page-controller occupies the full height of the main content area within the centered two-column container that starts just below the white title band. Horizontally, it is anchored to the left column of this container, spanning approximately 65–70% of the container’s width from the left edge, with a consistent left and right inner padding of about 24px. There is a generous vertical gap (around 32–48px) between the bottom of the title band and the top of this controller, maintaining the airy transition seen in the mockup. Inside the controller, elements are arranged linearly from top to bottom: the album-sort-control sits at the very top, followed immediately by either the album-grid or the album-grid-empty-state below it. The controller itself scrolls with the page; it does not fix or pin any content. Its right edge aligns with the seam between the main content and the sidebar, reinforcing the underlying grid. Visually, it defines the main reading and interaction column, mirroring the left content column from the mockup where the large post artwork and text live.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level container anchored to the left side of the centered two-column layout; scrolls naturally with the page and defines the vertical stacking context for all discography components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The album-sort-control is the first interactive element within the discography-page-controller and is positioned directly at the top of the left column, just below the white title band. It spans nearly the full width of the left column (approximately 90–100% width of that column, minus the column’s inner padding), with its content aligned to the left. Vertically, there is about 32–40px of space separating it from the bottom of the title band, echoing the generous spacing between major sections in the mockup. The control itself is a low-profile horizontal bar: a label or heading (e.g., 'Sort by') sits flush left, while a dropdown or segmented control sits to its immediate right, with options aligned horizontally. If additional filters are present, they line up to the right, forming a compact horizontal cluster that never feels taller than necessary. The entire control has about 16–24px of vertical padding to give its text and inputs breathing room. Below the sort-control, there is a clear vertical gap of around 24–32px before the first row of album tiles in the album-grid, making the control feel visually separated but still closely associated with the content it affects.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the main column within the page controller; scrolls away with the rest of the content and does not stick or float. It expands horizontally with the left column but maintains its relative position at the top of the discography area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The album-grid sits directly below the album-sort-control in the left main column, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full usable width of the discography-page-controller (approximately 65–70% of the overall content width), minus internal padding, and is visually anchored to the same left edge as the sort control and the page title above. The grid is arranged in a responsive multi-column layout that prioritizes album artwork: on larger screens, it typically displays three album tiles per row (each tile occupying about one-third of the left column width, with 16px horizontal gutters), while on slightly narrower screens it collapses to two columns. Each album tile is almost entirely comprised of the album cover image, which fills most of the tile’s width and sets the dark, edgy tone through imagery—this echoes the large hero artwork treatment from the mockup but repeated as a structured grid. Below each cover, a compact text block for album name and year sits with about 8–16px of top margin and 12–16px internal padding, keeping the overall tile height balanced and not overly dense. Vertical spacing between rows of tiles is consistent (around 24px), creating a clear rhythm as the user scrolls down. The grid extends downward as needed, becoming the primary scrollable content in the main column. The right sidebar remains visible to the side on wider screens, while the album grid maintains its left alignment and dominance in the viewing area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flexible, responsive grid that grows vertically and adjusts the number of columns based on available width; it remains confined to the left main column and scrolls with the page, always sitting directly below the sort control when present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The album-grid-empty-state occupies the same spatial region as the album-grid within the discography-page-controller but appears only when there are no albums to display for the current filters or sort settings. It is positioned directly below the album-sort-control, maintaining the same 24–32px gap that the grid would have. Instead of multiple tiles, it presents a single, centered content block within the left main column. This block is horizontally centered within the column and typically constrained to about 50–60% of the column width, so it feels focused and intentional. Vertically, the block sits with generous padding (around 32–48px inside the panel and at least 64px from the bottom of the viewport on first load), echoing the spacious feel of the white title band. An icon or minimal graphic sits at the top of this block, with a short, high-contrast message beneath (e.g., 'No albums match your selection'), and optional secondary text below that. The surrounding dark background remains visible around the panel, emphasizing the emptiness without feeling harsh. The right sidebar stays in place, but the user’s eye is drawn to this centered empty-state message as the main focus of the content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the album-grid—when active, it replaces the grid in the vertical stack. It is static and scrolls with the page, maintaining its centered alignment within the left column regardless of page length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The discography-url-state-sync component is non-visual and does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it is attached to the discography-page-controller and listens to interactions from the album-sort-control (and any other discography filters) as well as changes in the URL. It synchronizes the sort and filter state with the URL’s query parameters so that the discography view can be shared and restored. From a layout perspective, it occupies no width or height, does not affect spacing, and does not alter the visual alignment of any elements. It simply ensures that the visible components (sort control, album grid, empty state) remain in sync with navigation and browser history.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and layout-neutral; it has no spatial footprint and behaves purely as a logical/behavioral layer tied to the page-controller and sort/grid components.

name: Album Detail
title: Album
description: Displays a specific album’s tracks, lets users play available previews, and add individual tracks to playlists.

core_features: Load album details and full tracklist based on the URL parameter id using getAlbumDetail(albumId).
Display album metadata including title, optional subtitle, description, cover image, release date, and total track count.
Render an ordered tracklist with track number, title, duration (derived from durationSeconds), and explicit indicator when applicable.
Allow playback/preview of individual tracks that have an audioPreviewUrl, using a page-level audio player bound to the selected track.
Provide a per-track options control that opens a playlist selection dialog.
Use listPlaylists() to show available playlists in the dialog so the user can add the selected track.
Allow creation of a new playlist from within the dialog via createPlaylist(name, description, visibility).
After selecting or creating a playlist, add the track using addTrackToPlaylist(trackId, playlistId).
Show a confirmation message after a track is added to a playlist and offer navigation to the playlist page playlist.html?id={playlistId}.
Support navigation back to the discography album list via a "Back to albums" control that goes to discography.html.
Provide navigation to playlists.html for managing playlists from the album page.
user_workflows:
View album details and tracks:
1) User navigates to album.html?id={albumId} from discography.html, index.html, or playlist.html.
2) On page load, the component reads the id URL parameter and calls getAlbumDetail(albumId = id).
3) When the response returns, album metadata and the ordered tracklist are rendered.
4) User can scroll through the tracklist and inspect track information.

Play a track preview:
1) User clicks a play control on a specific track row.
2) The page sets the currently selected track in the audio player component, using the track’s audioPreviewUrl.
3) If audioPreviewUrl exists, playback starts; otherwise, the play control is disabled or shows that preview is unavailable.
4) User can pause, resume, or switch to another track by clicking play on a different track row.

Add a track to an existing playlist:
1) User clicks the options control on a track row (e.g., an options button).
2) The playlist selection dialog opens and triggers listPlaylists() if playlists are not already loaded in this session.
3) The dialog displays the list of playlists returned from listPlaylists().
4) User selects one playlist from the list.
5) The page calls addTrackToPlaylist(trackId = selectedTrack.id, playlistId = selectedPlaylist.id).
6) On success, the dialog closes and a confirmation component appears with a message referencing the playlist name and a link to playlist.html?id={selectedPlaylist.id}.

Create a new playlist and add a track to it:
1) User opens the track options dialog from a track row.
2) In the dialog, user chooses the option to create a new playlist.
3) A playlist creation form is shown inside the dialog, where the user enters playlist name and optional description and chooses a visibility value compatible with the Playlist model.
4) User submits the form, triggering createPlaylist(name, description, visibility).
5) When createPlaylist returns, the dialog updates its playlist list to include the new playlist and automatically proceeds to add the current track by calling addTrackToPlaylist(trackId = selectedTrack.id, playlistId = createdPlaylist.playlistId).
6) On success, the dialog closes and a confirmation component appears with a link to playlist.html?id={createdPlaylist.playlistId}.

Navigate back to discography while assembling playlists:
1) At any time, user clicks the "Back to albums" control in the album navigation component.
2) Browser navigates to discography.html without additional parameters.
3) From there, user can choose another album, which opens a new URL album.html?id={newAlbumId}, continuing the playlist-building process.

Manage playlists from the album page:
1) User clicks a "Manage playlists" control.
2) Browser navigates to playlists.html where the user can reorganize or review playlists (handled on that page).
3) If the user returns to an album detail page (via discography or other navigation), album.html?id={albumId} is loaded and the process repeats.
interactions: On page load: Read the id parameter from the URL query string; if present, call getAlbumDetail(albumId = id).
On missing or invalid id: If id is absent or getAlbumDetail results in an error indicating the album is not found, show an error state with an option to navigate back to discography.html.
Click "Back to albums": Navigate directly to discography.html.
Click "Manage playlists": Navigate directly to playlists.html.
Click track row play control: If the track has an audioPreviewUrl, set the current track for the audio player and start playback; if not, show a non-blocking message that no preview is available.
Click pause/stop in audio player: Pause playback of the current preview without altering track selection.
Click track options control: Open the playlist selection dialog with the context of the selected track (trackId).
Open playlist selection dialog: If playlists have not yet been loaded in this user session, call listPlaylists(); otherwise, reuse the previously loaded playlist list.

Select an existing playlist in dialog: Call addTrackToPlaylist(trackId, playlistId) for the currently selected track and chosen playlist; on success, close the dialog and show a confirmation component.
Choose to create a new playlist in dialog: Switch the dialog view to a playlist creation form without leaving the page.

Submit new playlist form: Validate required fields in the UI, then call createPlaylist(name, description, visibility); on success, add the new playlist to the in-memory playlist list and automatically call addTrackToPlaylist(trackId, playlistId = returned playlistId).
Click confirmation link to view playlist: Navigate to playlist.html?id={playlistId} using the playlistId returned from addTrackToPlaylist or createPlaylist.
Dismiss confirmation message: Hide the confirmation component for the current album session without affecting underlying data.
Click another track’s options or play controls while a preview is playing: Stop current playback and switch context to the newly selected track.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is the album being viewed, determined exclusively by the URL query parameter id. When the page loads, it reads id from album.html?id={albumId} and passes it to getAlbumDetail. If id changes (for example, via in-app navigation that updates the URL), the page must re-run getAlbumDetail with the new albumId and refresh all album- and track-bound components. No album or playlist data is stored in localStorage or other persistent browser storage; the page relies on the assigned interfaces and in-memory state only for the current session. Navigation to related pages uses URL parameters to encode target entities: navigating to playlist.html after adding a track always includes a query parameter id set to the playlistId returned by addTrackToPlaylist or createPlaylist; navigating back to discography.html or to playlists.html does not include additional parameters. UI-specific transient state—such as whether the playlist dialog is open, which track is currently selected for preview, or which playlist was last used—is maintained only in memory within the page components and is not encoded in the URL.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the albumId from the URL parameter id, calling getAlbumDetail(albumId), and providing the resulting album and tracks data to child components. Manages loading and error states for the album detail view.
Provides basic navigation controls for the album page, including going back to the discography and accessing playlist management.

Displays key album metadata retrieved from getAlbumDetail: title, optional subtitle, description, coverImageUrl, releaseDate, and totalTracks. Uses this information to contextualize the tracklist below.

Renders the ordered list of tracks for the album. For each track, shows trackNumber, title, formatted duration derived from durationSeconds, and explicit/content indicators based on isExplicit. Delegates playback and playlist actions to per-row controls.
