Represents a single track within the tracklist. Contains the play/preview button and options control to add the track to a playlist. Sends events with the associated trackId when the user interacts.

Handles playback of audio previews for the currently selected track using the track’s audioPreviewUrl. Exposes controls to play, pause, and switch tracks when a different track is selected from the tracklist.

Opens when the user clicks a track’s options control. Loads available playlists via listPlaylists() if not already loaded in this session. Displays playlists so the user can select one for the current track. Also exposes a path to open the playlist creation form when the user wants a new playlist.

Embedded in the playlist-selection dialog when the user chooses to create a new playlist. Collects playlist name, optional description, and visibility. On submit, calls createPlaylist(name, description, visibility), then uses the returned playlistId to add the current track via addTrackToPlaylist.

Coordinates calls to addTrackToPlaylist when the user selects an existing playlist or after a new playlist is created. Ensures that the correct combination of trackId and playlistId is sent to the interface and that success or error states are propagated to the dialog and confirmation components.

Shows a non-blocking confirmation when a track has been successfully added to a playlist. Displays dynamic content based on the playlist name and provides a link that navigates to playlist.html?id={playlistId}. Can be dismissed by the user.
Displays user-facing error information when getAlbumDetail fails or when the id URL parameter is missing or invalid. Offers a navigation control to return to discography.html.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Use the mockup’s vertical flow: a full-width dark header with logo and album navigation at the very top; directly beneath it, a full-width light (near-white) album title band containing the album header. Below that, a centered fixed-width main content container on a dark textured background, split into a two-column grid: a wide left column (about 2/3) holding the audio preview player and tracklist table, and a narrower right column (about 1/3) reserved for auxiliary album info or future widgets. Global overlays (loader, modal, notifications) sit above this structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the mockup’s vertical flow: a full-width dark header with logo and album navigation at the very top; directly beneath it, a full-width light (near-white) album title band containing the album header. Below that, a centered fixed-width main content container on a dark textured background, split into a two-column grid: a wide left column (about 2/3) holding the audio preview player and tracklist table, and a narrower right column (about 1/3) reserved for auxiliary album info or future widgets. Global overlays (loader, modal, notifications) sit above this structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Header height is compact (about 64–80px) but spans full width. The light album title band below it is taller and airy, taking roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height with generous vertical padding around the album title. The main content container is centered and constrained (e.g., ~1120–1200px wide) with a 65–70% / 30–35% left/right column split. Internal padding follows a 16–24px rhythm for main blocks and 8–12px for compact items like buttons, table cells, and nav links.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and album title band are spacious with plenty of white space and minimal elements. The main content area is more content-dense: the tracklist table and controls pack more information but preserve clear row spacing and padding between elements, avoiding clutter. The right sidebar remains visually lighter and less dense so the left column reads as the primary focus.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts top-left at the band logo and album navigation, moves horizontally along the navigation, then drops to the high-contrast album title in the light band. From there it moves down into the left column’s audio preview player and tracklist table. Secondary attention moves right to the sidebar, following a classic F-pattern. Overlays (notification banner, modal, loader) briefly intercept this path by appearing either below the header (banner) or centered on top (modal/loader).

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark, slightly textured background with a centered fixed-width content column. At the very top, a slim, full-width black header holds the band’s hand-drawn logo on the left and the album navigation links on the right, with tight horizontal spacing. Immediately below, a wide, almost white horizontal band stretches edge to edge, creating a strong contrast; within this band, the album header is centered in the page’s grid and given generous vertical padding so the album title and key metadata sit in a clean, airy field. Beneath this, the page transitions back to dark: a main content container aligned to the same center column is split into two vertical columns. The left, wider column begins with a compact audio preview player bar, followed by a tall tracklist table that nearly fills the column width, creating a strong vertical spine of content. The right, narrower sidebar aligns to the top of this main area and stacks any contextual or future modules, leaving breathing room so it does not compete with the tracklist. When the user triggers actions, a slim notification banner appears directly under the header and atop the light album band, while a dimmed overlay with a centered modal dialog can appear above everything for playlist selection and creation. Loading and error states replace or cover the main content area while preserving the overall header + title band structure, maintaining the same F-pattern and high-contrast, underground aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque dark overlay that covers the entire viewport from just below the album-navigation-bar down to the bottom of the screen, dimming the album-header, main content columns, and sidebar. At its center, aligned both vertically and horizontally within the viewport, sits a minimal spinner or loading glyph with a short status label, sized to roughly the width of a small card (about 240–320px). The overlay maintains a slight transparency so the underlying structure is faintly visible, reinforcing the sense that content is being fetched while keeping the user’s focus locked on the center of the screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport with a high stacking order, spanning 100% width and height; it scrolls with the viewport (effectively preventing underlying scrolling) until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The album-navigation-bar lives inside the full-width top header. It is horizontally centered within a fixed-width inner container (matching the main content width) and aligned vertically within a bar about 64–80px tall. The band’s grunge-style logo sits flush to the left with a small left margin from the viewport edge, and the navigation links (e.g., Home, Albums, Tour, Merch) are arranged in a horizontal row on the right, each separated by 16–24px of space. The nav links use compact vertical padding (about 8–12px) and are aligned to create a crisp, linear scan from left to right across the dark header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the top of the page, spanning full width; it remains at the top of the document flow and scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The album-header is housed within a full-width light band directly beneath the dark header. This band spans the entire viewport width with a near-white background, creating a strong visual break. Inside, a centered content container (aligned to the same maximum width as the main content) places the album’s title and metadata slightly left of center, honoring the F-pattern. The album title appears as a large, bold line near the top-left of this band, with generous top and bottom padding (about 32–48px above and below), and secondary details like artist name, release year, and genre appear beneath it in smaller type with 8–16px vertical spacing. There is ample empty space to the right of the text, preserving the airy feel and making this band read as a distinct page title area before the denser track content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level section sitting directly below the navigation header, pushing the main content area downward; it does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tracklist-table occupies the majority of the left main column within the centered content area below the album-header. The main content container starts around 32–48px below the bottom of the light album band. Within this container, the left column takes roughly 65–70% of the available width; the tracklist-table aligns to the left edge of this column and spans nearly its full width, leaving a small inner margin (about 16px) from the column edges. The table is a vertical stack of rows, optionally with a subtle header row indicating columns (e.g., #, Track, Time, Actions). Each row is clearly separated with 8–16px vertical padding and a faint dividing line or alternating dark background bands, creating a structured but not overly rigid feel. The table extends downward as far as needed, forming the primary vertical reading path of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally in the left column, expanding vertically with the number of tracks; it scrolls with the page, with no fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each track-row is a horizontal strip within the tracklist-table that spans the full width of the left column’s table area. The row height is moderate—about 48–64px—with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside the row. On the far left, a narrow column (about 5–10% of the row width) displays the track number, right-aligned. The central, flexible region (about 60–70% of the row width) holds the track title and any secondary labels (such as explicit tags or guest features) stacked or inline with subtle contrast. On the far right, a compact zone (about 20–25% of the row width) houses the play/preview trigger and the add-to-playlist control, aligned horizontally and right-justified. On hover, the entire row slightly lightens or shows a subtle border, indicating interactivity without breaking the dark, minimalist style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline list items within the tracklist-table that expand or highlight on hover or active states; they remain part of the scrolling table and do not use fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The audio-preview-player appears as a compact media bar at the top of the left main column, directly above the tracklist-table and sharing the same column width. It spans nearly the full width of the left column, with 16–24px of internal padding and a slightly lighter dark background than the page, making it stand out as a control surface. The play/pause, scrubber, and timecode are grouped toward the left-center of the bar, while secondary information (current track title, artist) sits immediately to their right. The bar has a modest height of about 56–64px, leaving 16–24px of vertical space between its bottom edge and the first track-row to maintain separation. When no track is selected, it may show a subtle prompt centered within the bar, such as “Select a track to preview,” maintaining the same footprint so the layout remains stable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it sits in the normal flow above the tracklist-table and scrolls off the screen with the rest of the content as the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-selection-dialog appears as a centered modal card overlaying a darkened, semi-transparent backdrop that covers the entire viewport. The card itself occupies about 30–40% of the viewport width (roughly 400–480px on desktop) and up to 60% of its height, with a minimum internal padding of 24px on all sides for comfortable breathing room. It is centered both horizontally and vertically. At the top of the card, a bold, left-aligned title like “Add to Playlist” appears with 16px of spacing below. Beneath, a vertical list of existing playlists fills most of the modal’s body; each playlist item is a compact row with 8–12px vertical padding and clear touch/click targets. At the bottom of the list, an affordance such as a link or button labeled “Create new playlist” sits aligned to the left, leading into the playlist-creation-form area within the same modal. The overall look is stark and minimal, with high contrast against the dimmed background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport with a dark backdrop that covers the entire page; it locks background scroll while open and sits above all other components via z-index.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-creation-form is contained within the lower portion of the playlist-selection-dialog card, either revealed inline beneath the playlist list or as a second step that uses the same modal footprint. It takes the full internal width of the modal minus 24px side padding, resulting in a clean vertical stack: a form title or label at the top, a single-line text input for the playlist name centered under it, and a compact row of actions (e.g., Cancel, Create) aligned to the right at the bottom. Vertical spacing between the label, input, and actions follows a 12–16px rhythm, keeping the form concise yet readable. The form’s background remains the same as the modal card, so it feels like a natural extension of the dialog rather than a separate element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the playlist-selection-dialog, expanding the modal’s content area; it does not create an additional overlay and scrolls within the modal if content exceeds the available height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The track-to-playlist-adder is expressed visually as an icon button or small text button labeled “Add” or “+ Playlist” positioned at the far right of each track-row’s action area. It sits to the right of or just after the play/preview trigger, aligned along the row’s vertical centerline. The button uses compact horizontal padding (8–12px) and minimal height so it does not overpower the track title, and it maintains a subtle outline or accent color that is visible against the dark row background. The spacing between the play control and this button is about 8–12px, forming a tight, functional cluster that users can quickly scan and interact with on the right side of each track-row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control anchored to the right end of each track-row; it appears and behaves within each row’s layout, optionally becoming more prominent on row hover, but remains part of the normal scrolling content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-add-confirmation appears as a slim, horizontal notification banner that spans the full width of the viewport and sits directly beneath the album-navigation-bar, overlaying the top edge of the light album-header band. Its height is modest—around 40–56px—with 12–16px vertical padding. The banner uses a slightly brighter or tinted background (e.g., a deep green or accent color) that contrasts against the dark header and light title band, while still fitting the edgy aesthetic. Inside, the message text is centered within the same fixed-width inner container used by the rest of the page, aligned slightly left-of-center for readability, with an optional close icon aligned to the right. When shown, it visually slides down from the header area and then retracts after a short time, without permanently shifting the layout below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned overlay anchored to the top of the page, just below the header; it appears and disappears with a slide or fade animation and sits above the album-header and main content without affecting their structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The album-error-state occupies the same visual territory as the main left content column when an error occurs (for example, failure to load album data). The header and light album title band remain in place, but instead of the audio-preview-player and tracklist-table, the left column shows a centered error card starting about 32–48px below the album-header. This card is moderately wide (about 60–70% of the left column’s width), horizontally centered within that column, with 24px padding and a slightly lighter dark background to distinguish it from the page. Inside, an icon or bold error title appears at the top, a short explanatory message sits beneath it with 8–16px spacing, and a retry or back action is placed at the bottom right or centered. The right sidebar may remain empty or show generic messaging, but the user’s visual focus is drawn to the prominent error card in the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block that replaces the main track content in the left column; it sits within the normal document flow and scrolls like any other section.

name: Playlists Overview
title: Playlists
description: Overview page for browsing existing playlists, opening a playlist detail page, and creating new playlists.

core_features: Load and display all playlists available to the user using the listPlaylists interface.
Show key metadata for each playlist such as name, description, visibility, whether it is a system playlist, and timestamps derived from the Playlist model.
Allow the user to open a specific playlist detail page by navigating to playlist.html with the playlist ID in the URL.
Provide an inline form to create a new playlist using the createPlaylist interface.
Allow the user to control the list view via URL-driven parameters such as sorting, visibility filter, and layout mode.
Expose management entry points (e.g., rename, delete) as navigation to playlist-specific management modes, without performing the actions directly on this page.
user_workflows: Browse existing playlists: 1) User opens playlists.html. 2) On initial load, the page reads optional URL parameters (e.g., sort, visibility, view, page). 3) The playlists-data-loader component calls listPlaylists with no parameters. 4) The playlists-list component renders all returned Playlist records, applying client-side sorting and filtering according to URL parameters. 5) User can change sort or filter controls in the toolbar; components update the URL parameters and re-render the list based on current in-memory data.

Open a playlist detail page: 1) User locates a playlist in the list. 2) User clicks anywhere on the playlist tile or row that is bound to that Playlist record. 3) The playlists-list component handles the click and navigates the browser to playlist.html?id=<playlistId>, passing the clicked playlist’s id as the URL parameter.

Create a new playlist: 1) User clicks a control to open the new-playlist form (or navigates to playlists.html?new=1 which opens the form by default). 2) User fills out the playlist name, optional description, and selects visibility (public or private). 3) User submits the form; the playlist-creation-form component calls createPlaylist with the entered values. 4) On success, the component receives the new playlistId and name from the createPlaylist response. 5) The component either navigates immediately to playlist.html?id=<playlistId> or triggers a refresh event so the playlists-data-loader reloads listPlaylists and the new playlist appears in the list.

Adjust list view and filters via URL: 1) User interacts with toolbar controls to change sort order, visibility filter, layout type, or page number. 2) The playlists-toolbar component updates the query string (e.g., playlists.html?sort=updatedAt_desc&visibility=all&view=grid&page=2) without reloading data from the server. 3) The playlists-data-loader and playlists-list components listen for URL changes, re-apply client-side sorting/filtering/pagination to the Playlists already loaded from listPlaylists, and re-render the list.

Access playlist management actions (rename/delete) via navigation: 1) User opens the action menu for a playlist in the list. 2) User selects Rename or Delete. 3) The playlists-list component navigates to playlist.html?id=<playlistId>&mode=edit or playlist.html?id=<playlistId>&mode=delete (or another defined management mode). 4) A dedicated playlist detail/management page (outside the scope of this design) handles the actual rename or delete using its own interfaces.
interactions: Click on playlist tile/row: Triggers navigation to playlist.html?id=<playlistId> using the id of the clicked Playlist.
Click on 'New playlist' control: Toggles visibility of the playlist-creation-form component or updates the URL to include new=1, which the component interprets as 'open on load'.
Submit new playlist form: Triggers createPlaylist(name, description, visibility); on success, either navigates to the new playlist detail page or requests a list refresh.

Change visibility filter (e.g., all/public/private/system/user-created): Updates the visibility query parameter in the URL; the playlists-list component re-applies client-side filtering on the existing Playlist collection.
Change sort order (e.g., by updatedAt, createdAt, name): Updates the sort query parameter; the playlists-list component sorts the existing Playlist collection accordingly.
Change view mode (e.g., list or grid): Updates the view query parameter; the playlists-list component switches its internal rendering mode while using the same Playlist data.
Change pagination (e.g., next/previous page): Updates the page query parameter; the playlists-list component slices the existing Playlist collection to show only the relevant subset.

Click on playlist actions menu (e.g., manage, rename, delete): Opens a contextual menu; choosing an action navigates to playlist.html with the playlistId plus an appropriate mode parameter to delegate the management operation to another page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page does not receive required incoming parameters from other pages but uses optional URL query parameters to control view state. Suggested query parameters: sort (e.g., 'name_asc', 'createdAt_desc', 'updatedAt_desc'), visibility (e.g., 'all', 'public', 'private', 'system', 'user'), view (e.g., 'list', 'grid'), page (integer >= 1 for client-side pagination), pageSize (optional integer to indicate how many playlists to show per page), and new (optional flag, e.g., '1', which indicates that the new playlist form should be expanded on load). On initial load or whenever the URL changes, a central state controller parses these parameters and exposes a derived viewState to child components (toolbar, list, creation form). The listPlaylists interface is called exactly once per full-page load or explicit refresh and is not parameterized; all filtering, sorting, and pagination are applied client-side based on the returned Playlist array and the current viewState derived from URL parameters. No playlist data is persisted in localStorage or other client-side storage; the URL is the single source of truth for view configuration. Navigation to a playlist detail page is handled by constructing playlist.html?id=<playlistId> from the clicked Playlist's id field.

components.functionality: Centralized data-loading component that invokes listPlaylists on page initialization or when a manual refresh is requested, stores the resulting Playlist collection in in-memory state, and exposes it to other components along with loading and error states.

Parses and synchronizes URL query parameters with internal view state (sort, visibility, view, page, pageSize, new). Provides a structured viewState object that other components use to render the playlist list, controls, and creation form consistently.

Provides user controls for creating a new playlist, changing sort order, filtering by visibility or playlist type, switching between list and grid view modes, and controlling client-side pagination. Updates the URL query parameters to drive view state instead of directly altering data.

Allows the user to create a new playlist by entering a name, optional description, and selecting a visibility value. Uses the createPlaylist interface on submit and, depending on configuration, either navigates to the new playlist detail page or triggers a list refresh.

Renders the collection of playlists provided by playlists-data-loader, applying the sorting, filtering, and pagination rules derived from playlists-view-state-controller. Displays Playlist metadata (name, description, visibility, isSystem, systemType, createdAt, updatedAt) and exposes interactions for navigating to playlist details and management modes.

Centralizes construction of navigation URLs for playlist-related actions. When a playlist is selected, it builds playlist.html?id=<playlistId> for detail view or playlist.html?id=<playlistId>&mode=<mode> for management workflows and updates window.location accordingly.
Displays error messages related to playlist loading or creation. Listens for error events from playlists-data-loader and playlist-creation-form and provides retry hooks when applicable.
Shows a functional empty-state when listPlaylists returns no playlists. Offers a control to open the playlist-creation-form and may update the URL to include new=1.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark site header with band logo and navigation (global), then a full-width white page-title band reading something like “PLAYLISTS”. Directly beneath this band, a centered main content container appears on the textured black background, using a two-column layout. The left, wider column is dedicated to playlist-related interactions (toolbar, playlist creation form, playlists list or empty state, error notification), while the right, narrower column is reserved for global sidebar widgets (search, categories, etc.) consistent with the site template but not part of this component set. All playlist components live inside the left main column, except the error notification which visually spans the full content container width to demand attention.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark site header with band logo and navigation (global), then a full-width white page-title band reading something like “PLAYLISTS”. Directly beneath this band, a centered main content container appears on the textured black background, using a two-column layout. The left, wider column is dedicated to playlist-related interactions (toolbar, playlist creation form, playlists list or empty state, error notification), while the right, narrower column is reserved for global sidebar widgets (search, categories, etc.) consistent with the site template but not part of this component set. All playlist components live inside the left main column, except the error notification which visually spans the full content container width to demand attention.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header remains relatively shallow but full-width. The white page-title band beneath it occupies a generous vertical slice—roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height—with large top and bottom padding. Below that, the main content container is centered at a fixed width (approximately 1100–1200px), divided into a left column (about 65–70% of the container width) and a right sidebar (about 30–35%). Within the left column, the playlists-toolbar sits at the top, followed by a possibly expanded playlist-creation-form panel, then the playlists-list (or empty state) taking up the bulk of the vertical space. The data-loader overlays this same content zone when active. The error notification appears just under the title band across the container. The right sidebar stays comparatively compact but tall, stacking its global modules from top to bottom.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and white title band are very spacious, with minimal elements and substantial vertical padding (around 32–48px). The main content area is moderately dense: the playlists-toolbar is compact and functional with 12–16px internal padding, the playlist-creation-form uses comfortable padding (16–24px) and clear separation from surrounding elements, and the playlists-list stacks large, visually strong playlist cards with 16–24px gaps to keep breathing room. The empty state, when shown, is more open and centered with generous whitespace, contrasting with the denser list view. Sidebar modules remain compact but separated by 16–24px vertical margins to avoid clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a classic F-pattern. The user’s eye starts at the top-left band logo in the dark header, travels right across navigation, then drops to the high-contrast white title band with the page title (“PLAYLISTS OVERVIEW” or similar). From there, attention moves down into the main container’s left column, first encountering the playlists-toolbar, then any error notification directly below the title band, then the playlist-creation-form, and finally the playlists-list or empty state as the primary content stack. Once the main column is scanned, attention drifts to the right sidebar modules. When the data-loader is active, a centered overlay indicator temporarily becomes the focus within the main content area. When an error or empty state occurs, those components interrupt the usual flow and become the immediate focal point within the left column or full container band.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark, textured background with an indie, slightly grunge aesthetic. At the very top, a full-width dark header anchors the band’s hand-drawn logo on the left and a minimalist navigation bar on the right. Directly beneath, a stark white page-title band stretches across the screen, with a large, uppercase page title like “PLAYLISTS” centered or left-aligned and surrounded by generous vertical whitespace, creating a strong visual break. Below this, the content pulls inward into a centered, fixed-width container riding on the dark background. Inside this container, the layout splits into a wider left column and a narrower right sidebar. The left column begins with a dark, compact playlists-toolbar aligned under the page title, followed optionally by a slightly elevated playlist-creation-form panel. Beneath that, the main vertical stream of content is formed by large playlist cards in the playlists-list, each nearly filling the width of the column and showcasing artwork, track counts, and metadata, stacked with comfortable gaps. If there are no playlists, a bold but minimal empty state graphic or message occupies this same territory, loosely centered and surrounded by plenty of negative space. A slim, high-contrast error notification bar can appear directly below the white title band, spanning the content width and pushing everything down. A semi-transparent loading overlay can temporarily veil the left column content, with a centered spinner. Throughout, the dark palette, sharp contrast, and clean grid maintain a professional yet underground band aesthetic, guiding the eye from header to title, then down the main playlist content and over to the secondary sidebar modules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the main content container area beneath the white title band and above the footer, but is visually constrained to the left main column where playlist content lives. When active, it renders as a semi-transparent dark overlay that covers from just below the playlists-toolbar (or error notification if present) down to the bottom edge of the visible left column content, spanning the full width of that column (approximately 65–70% of the container width). At the center of this overlay (both horizontally centered in the left column and vertically centered within the viewport portion of the column) sits a minimalist loading indicator—such as a circular spinner or subtle waveform—against the darkened background. The right sidebar remains visible and interactive, emphasizing that loading applies specifically to playlists. When inactive, this component has no visible footprint and does not affect spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay relative to the left main column inside the centered container. It does not scroll independently; instead, it covers the currently visible portion of playlist content while allowing the page itself to scroll. When activated, it temporarily intercepts interaction with underlying left-column components (toolbar, form, list) without shifting their layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The view-state controller is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the left main column, orchestrating which visible component appears in the primary content slot: it toggles between showing the playlists-list, the playlists-empty-state, or surfacing the playlists-error-notification, and triggers the playlists-data-loader overlay when needed. It effectively governs what the user sees in the vertical stack beneath the playlists-toolbar without adding its own visible UI elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Has no direct spatial presence; it is logically bound to the main left column region and dynamically controls which components are rendered there, but does not affect the physical layout beyond causing other components to appear, disappear, or swap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlists-toolbar is positioned at the top of the main content container, aligned directly under the white page-title band and constrained to the left main column. It sits roughly 32px below the bottom edge of the title band to maintain the mockup’s spacious separation between structural bands and content. The toolbar spans the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of the container width) and has a compact height, roughly 56–72px depending on its controls. It uses a dark background slightly lighter than the page background, with 12–16px of internal padding on all sides. Its contents align horizontally: key actions such as filters or sort controls sit on the left, while a prominent “New Playlist” action sits on the right edge of the toolbar, following the top horizontal scan pattern. There is approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the toolbar and the next element in the stack (either the error notification if it’s relocated here, or more typically the playlist-creation-form when expanded, or directly the playlists-list if the form is hidden).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the top of the left column and scrolls with the content by default, but it can optionally become sticky within the viewport once the user scrolls past the white title band, remaining pinned to the top edge of the main content area. This reinforces its role as a persistent control bar without breaking the clean grid-based layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-creation-form sits directly below the playlists-toolbar within the left main column, maintaining a vertical gap of about 16–24px from the toolbar to visually separate control and form. It occupies the full width of the left column and appears as a card-like panel on the dark background: a slightly lighter charcoal rectangle with subtle edges or a thin border. Inside, the form content is wrapped with comfortable padding on all sides (approximately 16–24px, matching other text block modules), giving the fields breathing room while preserving the page’s moderate density. Vertically, the form may vary in height depending on the number of fields, but it should feel like a self-contained block, ending with a clear gap of 24px before the playlists-list begins. When the form is collapsed or not in focus, it can visually compress into a slim header bar—still in this same position—minimizing its height while keeping its left-column alignment; in that state, the playlists-list slides up to fill the freed vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block element in the left column’s vertical flow, pushing content below it down when expanded and allowing content to rise when collapsed. It scrolls naturally with the rest of the page and does not overlay other components. It remains anchored beneath the toolbar regardless of scroll state, forming part of the main vertical reading path.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlists-list forms the core of the left column’s content and appears immediately beneath the playlist-creation-form (or beneath the playlists-toolbar when the form is not visible). A vertical margin of about 24px separates the top of the list from the element above. The list occupies the full width of the left column—about 65–70% of the container—and extends downward, creating a tall, scrollable content stream analogous to the large post artwork column in the mockup. Each playlist appears as a substantial card with its artwork or cover image nearly filling the card’s width, accompanied by title and metadata below or alongside, reinforcing the visually heavy, media-centric feel. Cards are stacked vertically with 16–24px between them, ensuring clear separation without feeling sparse. The first playlist card visually lines up horizontally with the top modules of the right sidebar, reinforcing the two-column grid. The bottom of the list flows naturally into the page’s footer or continues off-screen as the user scrolls, maintaining the linear content flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard, scrollable vertical list within the left column. Its height grows with the number of playlists, pushing the page length downward. It does not overlay or fix itself; instead, it is the main body of content that the user scrolls through. The list is replaced entirely by the playlists-empty-state when no items exist, governed by the view-state controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-navigation-handler is non-visual and conceptually attached to each playlist card within the playlists-list. It does not occupy its own space; rather, it expands the clickable area of each playlist entry to cover the whole card, making the transition to a playlist detail page feel natural and responsive. When a user hovers or focuses on a card, the handler triggers subtle visual feedback on that card (such as a slight glow, border, or brightness shift) within the existing card bounds, reinforcing its interactive nature without altering the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Has no independent spatial footprint; it is bound to the playlists-list items. It does not change the stacking or sizing of cards but adds interactive behavior within their existing rectangles. Navigation transitions occur from the card’s location in the left column, preserving the spatial context when moving to detail views.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlists-error-notification appears as a slim but visually striking banner directly beneath the white page-title band and above the playlists-toolbar, staying within the centered main content container but spanning its full width (covering both the left column and right sidebar, emphasizing its global importance). It sits about 16px below the title band, maintaining the generous band separation. The banner is 40–56px tall, with 12–16px internal vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding, and uses a high-contrast accent color (such as deep red) against the dark background to immediately catch attention. The text is left-aligned, with any close/dismiss icon aligned to the right edge. When present, this notification pushes the playlists-toolbar and all other main content downward, maintaining consistent spacing below it (around 16–24px) so the layout remains airy and structured.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block element in the vertical flow of the centered main container, anchored between the title band and the playlist-specific components. It scrolls away with the page content once the user moves down and does not overlay or fix itself. Its appearance and dismissal smoothly reflow the content below, preserving the grid and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlists-empty-state replaces the playlists-list within the left main column whenever there are no playlists to display. It is positioned in the same slot where the list would begin: directly beneath the playlist-creation-form (or beneath the playlists-toolbar if the form is absent), with a top margin of roughly 24px to maintain consistency. Within that left column, the empty state’s content (icon, headline, and supporting text or call-to-action) is centered horizontally and given generous vertical padding—approximately 48–64px inside a subtle panel or simply as open space—to create a calm, uncluttered feel. The message block itself sits slightly above the vertical center of the visible left column area so it remains within the user’s initial viewport. The right sidebar remains visible and aligned alongside the empty state, reinforcing the two-column layout even when the main content is minimal.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the same spatial area as the playlists-list within the left column and is mutually exclusive with it. The empty state content scrolls with the page, but because it is relatively short, it often fits within a single viewport, leaving extra dark negative space below. It does not overlay other components and simply fills the left-column content slot controlled by the view-state controller.

name: Playlist Detail
title: Playlist
description: Displays the details and contents of a selected playlist, and lets users play, shuffle, reorder, and remove playlist items.

core_features: Read the playlist ID from the URL and load playlist metadata and items using getPlaylistDetail.
Display playlist name, description, and visibility, with clear indication if it is a system playlist.
List all items in the playlist in their current order, showing key metadata such as item type (track or video), title, source album (for tracks), and duration when available.
Provide playlist-level playback actions such as playing the list from the beginning or shuffling playback (emitting events for an external player).
Allow removing individual playlist items using removePlaylistItem and immediately reflecting the change in the list.
Support reordering playlist items (e.g., via drag-and-drop or move up/down controls) and persisting the new order using reorderPlaylistItems.
Offer navigation back to the playlists overview (playlists.html) and onward to related album or video detail pages (album.html, video.html) based on the selected playlist item.
Optionally allow switching between playlists using listPlaylists, by navigating to playlist.html with a different playlistId.
user_workflows:
View a playlist:
1. User navigates to playlist.html with a URL parameter id containing the playlistId.
2. The page reads id from the URL and triggers getPlaylistDetail(playlistId=id).
3. While loading, a loading state is shown instead of the playlist contents.
4. On success, playlist metadata and the ordered list of items are stored in page state and rendered in the header and items list.
5. If getPlaylistDetail fails (e.g., invalid id), an error state is shown with an option to go back to playlists.html.

Play or shuffle the playlist:
1. After the playlist is loaded, the user clicks the primary "Play" control.
2. The page emits an event (e.g., onPlayPlaylist) carrying the ordered list of items and the playlist metadata for an external player to handle playback.
3. Alternatively, the user clicks the "Shuffle" control.
4. The page emits an event (e.g., onShufflePlaylist) carrying the playlist items, leaving the shuffle logic to the external player or controller.
5. The page may highlight the currently playing item when a corresponding event is received from the external player (e.g., onActiveItemChange), without calling additional APIs.

Remove an item from the playlist:
1. In the items list, the user locates an item and clicks its "Remove" action.
2. The item row component optionally shows a confirmation interaction (e.g., confirmation dialog or inline confirmation control).
3. On confirmation, the page calls removePlaylistItem(playlistItemId=item.id).
4. While the call is in progress, the item’s remove control shows a loading/disabled state to prevent duplicate removal actions.
5. On success, the item is removed from the local items list state, and the UI updates without requiring a full page reload.
6. If the removal fails, an error message is shown for that item, and the item remains in the list.

Reorder playlist items and save the new order:
1. The user initiates reordering by dragging an item to a new position or by using move up/down controls provided by the items list.
2. As the user reorders, the list updates locally in real time to show the new sequence.
3. When the user completes the reorder action (e.g., drop ends or a "Save order" action is triggered), the page computes the new ordered list of playlistItem IDs.
4. The page calls reorderPlaylistItems(playlistId=currentPlaylist.id, orderedPlaylistItemIds=[...newOrderIds]).
5. While the call is in progress, the list may show a saving indicator, and reorder controls can be disabled.
6. On success, the local order is confirmed as the persisted order; any temporary indicators are cleared.
7. If the call fails, the items list reverts to the last known persisted order and shows an error message.

Navigate back to playlists overview:
1. The user clicks the "Back to Playlists" navigation control in the header or navigation bar.
2. The page performs a client-side navigation to playlists.html without additional API calls from this page.
3. The playlists overview page then manages its own data loading and display logic.

Open the album for a track in the playlist:
1. In the items list, the user clicks the source album name for a track item.
2. The item row component uses the associated album identifier (resolved from the track) as sourceAlbumId.
3. The browser navigates to album.html?id=sourceAlbumId via URL parameters.
4. The album detail page is responsible for loading and showing the album for that ID.

Open a video in the playlist:
1. In the items list, the user clicks on a video item row or a dedicated "Open Video" action.
2. The item row component uses the associated video identifier as videoId.
3. The browser navigates to video.html?id=videoId via URL parameters.
4. The video detail page is responsible for handling playback and display for that video.

Switch between playlists (optional, using listPlaylists):
1. The user opens a playlist selector control (e.g., dropdown) that calls listPlaylists() to retrieve all available playlists.
2. The playlists are shown in a list, highlighting the currently active playlist based on the id URL parameter.
3. The user selects a different playlist from this control.
4. The page navigates to playlist.html?id=selectedPlaylistId.
5. The new playlist detail page instance reads the new id, calls getPlaylistDetail, and displays the corresponding playlist.
interactions:
Page load / URL change:
- Trigger: Browser loads playlist.html or the id query parameter changes.
- Behavior: Read id from the URL. If present, call getPlaylistDetail(playlistId=id). If missing, show an error state with navigation back to playlists.html.
- State Updates: Set loading=true before the call; on success, store playlist and items; on error, set error state.

Back to Playlists click:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Back to Playlists" control.
- Behavior: Navigate to playlists.html using a standard link or router navigation.
- State Updates: None on this page after navigation.

Playlist switcher interaction (optional):
- Trigger: User opens the playlist switcher control.
- Behavior: On first open (or on component init), call listPlaylists(). Display results and mark the current playlist by comparing each playlist.id with the id URL parameter.
- Trigger: User selects another playlist from the list.
- Behavior: Navigate to playlist.html?id=selectedPlaylist.id.
- State Updates: Current page instance is replaced or reinitialized with the new playlistId.

Play playlist button:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Play" button in the playlist header.
- Behavior: Emit an onPlayPlaylist event containing the current playlist metadata and the ordered list of items. No additional API calls are made from this page.
- State Updates: Optionally track a currentPlayingItemId in page state when the external player confirms which item started playing.

Shuffle playlist button:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Shuffle" button in the playlist header.
- Behavior: Emit an onShufflePlaylist event containing the current playlist metadata and items. Shuffling is performed externally or by a shared playback controller.
- State Updates: Optionally track a shuffled sequence or currently playing item based on external player feedback.

Play single item from the list:
- Trigger: User clicks on an item row or a dedicated "Play" icon for that item.
- Behavior: Emit an onPlayItem event with that specific PlaylistItem and its resolved track/video metadata for an external player.
- State Updates: Mark this item as active in the list (e.g., currentItemId=item.id) based on feedback from the player.

Remove item from playlist:
- Trigger: User clicks the "Remove" icon/button on an item row and confirms.
- Behavior: Call removePlaylistItem(playlistItemId=item.id).
- State Updates: Temporarily mark the item as removing; on success, remove it from the items array in page state. If the playlist becomes empty, show the empty state component instead of the list. On error, clear the removing flag and show an item-level error message.

Reorder items via drag-and-drop:
- Trigger: User drags a list item and drops it at a new position.
- Behavior: The list reorders locally as the user drags. When the drag ends, compute orderedPlaylistItemIds from the new visual order and call reorderPlaylistItems(playlistId=playlist.id, orderedPlaylistItemIds=[...]).
- State Updates: While saving, mark the list as saving (e.g., reorderingInProgress=true) and optionally disable further drag operations. On success, clear the flag and keep the new order; on failure, revert to the previous order stored before the drag.

Reorder items via move controls:
- Trigger: User clicks "Move up" or "Move down" controls on an item row.
- Behavior: Adjust the local array order immediately, then call reorderPlaylistItems with the updated orderedPlaylistItemIds.
- State Updates: Same as drag-and-drop: temporary saving flag, revert on error.

Navigate to album detail from an item:
- Trigger: User clicks an album title within a track item row.
- Behavior: Retrieve the associated album identifier from the track relation (e.g., track.albumId) and navigate to album.html?id=albumId.
- State Updates: None on this page after navigation.

Navigate to video detail from an item:
- Trigger: User clicks a video item row or a specific "Open Video" action.
- Behavior: Retrieve the videoId from the PlaylistItem’s associated Video and navigate to video.html?id=videoId.
- State Updates: None on this page after navigation.

Handle empty playlist:
- Trigger: getPlaylistDetail returns a playlist with no items, or all items have been removed.
- Behavior: Show the empty state component which explains that the playlist has no items and provides navigation back to playlists.html.
- State Updates: items array is empty; list component is not rendered; empty state component is rendered instead.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main state driver for this page is the URL query parameter id. When the page loads, it reads id from playlist.html?id={playlistId}. If id is present, the page calls getPlaylistDetail(playlistId=id) and stores the returned playlist object and items array in page-level state. If id is missing or if getPlaylistDetail fails, the page enters an error state and offers navigation back to playlists.html without trying to operate on undefined playlist data. When the user switches playlists via an internal control (e.g., a playlist switcher), the page does not directly change internal state; instead, it navigates to playlist.html?id=newId so that the new playlist is always reflected in the URL. Any subsequent refresh or share will keep the correct playlist context. After a successful reorder operation using reorderPlaylistItems, the new items order is treated as the canonical order in page state and is assumed to be the persisted server order. If reorderPlaylistItems fails, state is rolled back to the previous order captured before reordering. After a successful removePlaylistItem call, the corresponding item is removed from the local items array; this updated list is used for any subsequent reorder or playback actions. All state needed to re-open a given playlist (which playlist is shown and which items are in it) is derivable from the server via getPlaylistDetail using the id URL parameter; no persistence via localStorage is used.

components.functionality: Reads the playlistId from the URL (id query parameter), calls getPlaylistDetail to fetch playlist metadata and items, and provides loading, success, and error states to child components.
Displays a "Back to Playlists" navigation link and optionally a playlist switcher that uses listPlaylists to allow quick navigation to another playlist detail page.
Shows core playlist information such as name, description, visibility, and whether it is a system playlist, and exposes primary playlist-level actions like Play and Shuffle.

Coordinates playback-related events originating from the playlist (play full playlist, shuffle playlist, play single item) and emits them to a global or shared player without making additional API calls.
Displays the ordered list of playlist items, supports item selection, item playback requests, item removal, and item reordering operations, and coordinates persistence via reorderPlaylistItems.

Represents a single playlist item row, displaying metadata from the associated track or video (title, type, album, duration when available) and providing controls to play, navigate to album/video detail pages, remove the item, and initiate reordering.

Maintains the current ordering of playlist items in page state, computes the orderedPlaylistItemIds array after drag-and-drop or move up/down actions, and calls reorderPlaylistItems to persist the new order.
Handles removePlaylistItem calls for individual items, tracks loading and error state per item, and updates the playlist items array when removals succeed.
Appears when the playlist has no items, explaining that there are no tracks or videos in this playlist and offering navigation back to the playlists overview.
Displays an error message when getPlaylistDetail fails or when the id URL parameter is invalid or missing, and offers a way to navigate back to playlists.html.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom hierarchy: a full-width dark navigation bar at the very top (playlist-navigation-bar), immediately followed by a full-width, high-contrast, light playlist header band (playlist-header). Below this, a centered, fixed-width main content container sits on a textured black background. Inside this container, the layout splits into a two-column structure: a dominant left column (about 65–70% width) dedicated to playlist-specific content (playlist-playback-controller followed by playlist-items-list), and a narrower right sidebar (about 30–35% width) reserved for secondary site widgets and contextual info. Structural and state components (playlist-detail-loader, playlist-empty-state, playlist-error-state) layer over or replace the main column content without breaking this overall grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom hierarchy: a full-width dark navigation bar at the very top (playlist-navigation-bar), immediately followed by a full-width, high-contrast, light playlist header band (playlist-header). Below this, a centered, fixed-width main content container sits on a textured black background. Inside this container, the layout splits into a two-column structure: a dominant left column (about 65–70% width) dedicated to playlist-specific content (playlist-playback-controller followed by playlist-items-list), and a narrower right sidebar (about 30–35% width) reserved for secondary site widgets and contextual info. Structural and state components (playlist-detail-loader, playlist-empty-state, playlist-error-state) layer over or replace the main column content without breaking this overall grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation bar is shallow in height (around 64–80px) but spans the full browser width. The playlist-header band beneath it is taller and spacious, taking roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, with its content centered within a fixed-width container (approx. 1120–1280px) and generous vertical padding (24–48px). The main content container below uses the rest of the viewport and scrolls as needed. Within this container, the left column occupies about two-thirds of the width and hosts the visually dominant playback controller at its top (around 160–220px tall), followed by a vertically expanding list of items. The right column uses the remaining one-third of the width for sidebar modules with stacked blocks and 16–24px vertical spacing between them. Margins between major sections (header band to main content) are around 32px, while spacing between internal components (controller to list, list items, sidebar modules) is in the 16–24px range.
layout_strategies.content_density: The upper portion of the page (navigation bar and playlist-header band) remains visually spacious, with plenty of negative space around logo, nav links, and the playlist title to echo the mockup’s minimalist, high-impact feel. The main content area becomes denser, especially in the left column where the large playback controller and multiple playlist rows live, but each block still maintains clear padding (16–24px) and consistent spacing between items so the layout never feels cramped. List rows are compact yet breathable (roughly 48–64px tall with internal padding), and sidebar content is broken into small, clearly separated modules. States like empty and error are given focused, central space in the main column, replacing list content to avoid visual clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual attention starts at the top-left with the band logo within the playlist-navigation-bar, then sweeps horizontally across the navigation links (F-pattern top bar). The eye then drops straight down to the bold playlist-header band, where the playlist name and key meta are left-aligned within the wide white strip, creating a strong focal anchor. From there, the gaze moves down into the main content container, first landing on the playlist-playback-controller at the top of the left column—the main functional hero. After establishing playback controls and cover art, attention flows down through the playlist-items-list, scanning titles along the left edge and durations on the right. Secondary attention moves to the right sidebar to pick up any contextual or global widgets. When active, the playlist-detail-loader, empty state, or error state temporarily override this flow by becoming the dominant focal region in the main content area while still respecting the overall structure.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a textured, nearly-black background that stretches edge to edge. At the very top, a slim, full-width dark navigation bar anchors the layout, the band’s logo hand-drawn on the left and minimalist nav links running along the right, all within a centered fixed-width container. Directly beneath this, a bold, full-width white playlist header band slices across the screen, creating strong contrast with the dark surroundings. Inside that band, the playlist title is left-aligned in large, confident type, with subtle meta details beneath or beside it, surrounded by generous breathing room. Below, the content tightens into a centered two-column structure: on the left, a wide main column holds a prominent playlist playback controller—cover art, big play/shuffle buttons, and core controls—flush with the column width, followed by a vertical stack of playlist tracks. Each track sits on a dark row with clean alignment, subtle separators, and small interactive icons for drag reordering and removal. On the right, a narrower sidebar stacks secondary site modules and future playlist-related widgets as compact dark blocks separated by consistent vertical spacing. As the user scrolls, the navigation bar remains pinned to the top, the white header band quickly clears, and the user lives mainly in the dark main content area, moving down the left column list while occasionally glancing right. Any loading, empty, or error states appear centered within the main column, dimming or replacing the playlist content but maintaining the same structural rhythm and gritty, minimalist aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-detail-loader exists as an overlay layer that sits above the main content but below the global navigation bar. When active, it covers the area starting just under the playlist-navigation-bar and extending down to the bottom of the viewport, matching the width of the entire content area (including both left main column and right sidebar). The overlay is a semi-transparent dark veil (slightly lighter than the background texture) that subtly mutes the white playlist-header band and the main two-column content beneath. At the center of the viewport, a small spinner or loading glyph appears, aligned horizontally to the center of the fixed-width content container and vertically centered within the visible portion of the page. Padding around the loader indicator is generous (24–32px) to keep it isolated and visually dominant. When inactive, this component is invisible and does not occupy any space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay relative to the viewport below the navigation bar; it scrolls with the page background but remains covering the content area while active. It temporarily blocks interaction with underlying elements until data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-navigation-bar is a full-width horizontal strip pinned at the very top of the page. It sits on a deep black, slightly textured background, approximately 64–80px tall, creating a thin but strong visual anchor. Within this strip, a centered fixed-width container (around 1120–1280px) houses the content: the band’s logo is positioned at the far left, aligned with the left edge of the main content grid, and the navigation links run horizontally to the right with compact 8–12px horizontal padding between each. Vertical padding inside the bar is tight (12–16px), keeping it lean and unobtrusive. The bar’s bottom edge visually separates from the white playlist-header beneath, enhancing the F-pattern: users read from logo leftwards across nav items before moving down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/fixed to the top of the viewport so that it remains visible while scrolling through the playlist content. It does not collapse; the rest of the page scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-header forms a full-width horizontal band directly beneath the navigation bar, stretching from left to right edge of the viewport. Its background is a solid white or very light gray, sharply contrasting with the dark site chrome. Its vertical height is generous—roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height—with 32–48px of vertical padding, creating a calm, spacious field. Inside, the same centered fixed-width container used by the navigation bar aligns content: the playlist title is left-aligned, starting at the same horizontal line as the logo above, set in large, bold type. Additional metadata (such as number of tracks, total duration, creator) sits just below or to the right of the title in smaller, low-contrast text. The right side of this band is mostly empty space, reinforcing the minimalist, gallery-like feel. The lower edge of this white band sits about 24–32px above the top of the main content container, giving a distinct separation between header and body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page, leaving the darker main content as the primary focus.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-playback-controller occupies the top of the left main column within the centered content container, directly below the spacing that follows the playlist-header band. The main content container is divided into two columns: the left column takes roughly 65–70% of the container’s width, and the playback controller spans this entire left column width. Horizontally, it aligns flush with the left column’s grid, leaving a 24px gap between it and the right sidebar. Vertically, it is a distinct dark module about 160–220px tall, with 24px internal padding on all sides. On the left side of the controller, a large square cover image (around 96–128px) sits, aligned vertically center. To its right, text details (playlist name echo, short description) and interactive controls (Play, Shuffle, Repeat, possibly a queue icon) are grouped, arranged in one or two horizontal rows. Primary actions (Play, Shuffle) are larger and positioned towards the left side of this grouping to be immediately in the eye’s path as the user moves down from the header. The entire block visually feels like a hero card: stronger contrast, subtle glow or border against the dark background, making it a focal step between the white header and the track list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the column but optionally configured as sticky within the left column: when the user scrolls, it can pin to just below the navigation bar as long as there are tracks to scroll, keeping playback controls visible while the playlist-items-list moves underneath. In either case, it always remains at the top of the left column content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-items-list sits directly beneath the playlist-playback-controller in the left main column, separated by approximately 16–24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of this left column, aligning with the controller above and maintaining a consistent 24px left and right padding to match the column’s grid. The list is a vertical stack of playlist-item-row components, filling the remaining height of the column and extending downward as needed; the page scrolls to reveal additional rows instead of using a separate scroll area. The background underneath the list is a slightly lighter black or dark gray than the page background, providing a subtle panel effect. Between rows, there may be hairline dividers or a 4–8px gap to visually separate tracks while maintaining a compact rhythm. At the bottom, an extra 32–48px of breathing room ensures the final track doesn’t crash into the viewport edge or footer elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list within the left column that scrolls as part of the main page. If the playback controller is sticky, the list scrolls beneath it, starting just below the controller’s fixed lower edge.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each playlist-item-row is a horizontally oriented strip within the playlist-items-list that occupies the full inner width of the left column. Rows are approximately 48–64px tall, with 16–24px horizontal padding and about 8–12px vertical padding around the text. On the far left, aligned to the column grid, a small drag handle icon (e.g., vertical dots or bars) is positioned, sitting flush to the left padding and vertically centered; this visually hints at reordering. Immediately to the right, the track index number appears in compact, low-contrast type. Next, the primary text area spans the majority of the row: track title in a brighter, higher-contrast label, with artist name and any additional info in smaller, toned-down text beneath or to the right. On the far right edge, aligned with the right padding, the track duration is placed, and just left of this, a subtle remove icon (e.g., an ‘x’ or trash can) appears. When hovered, the row’s background darkens slightly or gains a soft glow, and the remove icon becomes more prominent. Rows are visually separated either by a 1px line or a small vertical space, keeping the list legible and rhythmically consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the list but responds to interactions: on drag, the row lifts slightly with a shadow and moves freely vertically; on removal, it animates (fade/slide) out of the stack and the subsequent rows slide up to close the gap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-reorder-handler is not a visible block on its own but manifests through affordances on each playlist-item-row. Its primary visual cue is the drag handle positioned on the far left of each row, slightly indented from the edge within the row’s left padding. When a user hovers near this handle, the cursor changes to a move icon and the row subtly highlights to indicate it can be grabbed. During a drag action, the selected row lifts by a few pixels, gains a faint shadow, and its background lightens slightly against the dark list. Neighboring rows shift, leaving a clear gap where the dragged row will land, respecting the 8–12px vertical rhythm of the list. The overall list height expands or contracts temporarily as rows move, but the layout preserves the left column width and spacing to maintain the structured feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behavioral and interaction-focused; acts within the existing spatial confines of the playlist-items-list. It dynamically repositions playlist-item-row elements without changing the surrounding grid or column proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-item-removal-handler surfaces visually through a small, right-aligned remove control in each playlist-item-row. This control is positioned just inside the row’s right padding, aligned vertically center with the track duration slightly to its left. In the default state, the remove icon is low-contrast and unobtrusive, preserving the minimalist aesthetic. On row hover, the icon becomes more pronounced—either brightening or gaining a subtle outline—making the delete affordance clear without overwhelming the track information. When activated, the corresponding row animates out of the list: it may slide horizontally to the left and fade, or briefly collapse vertically, while subsequent rows smoothly slide up to close the space, keeping the list density consistent. The handler ensures that removal actions feel contained within the row’s footprint and do not disrupt the two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Interaction-layer behavior bound to each playlist-item-row. It removes rows from the vertical stack while maintaining the left column’s width and overall vertical flow; it does not introduce new spatial regions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the playlist is empty, the playlist-empty-state occupies the main content area of the left column in place of the playlist-items-list (and optionally minimizes the playback controller). The left column still starts beneath the playlist-header with the same horizontal alignment, but instead of a track list, a single centered empty-state card appears. This card spans about 60–70% of the left column’s width and is centered horizontally, with generous 32–48px padding and a slightly lifted dark background panel. It is positioned roughly in the upper-middle of the viewport, leaving ample space above and below, so it becomes the clear focal point. At the top of the card, an icon or abstract mark sits; below it, a concise headline such as “No tracks in this playlist yet” appears in medium weight, followed by a brief explanatory line and a primary call-to-action button (e.g., “Add tracks”) centered below. The surrounding left column and right sidebar remain in place, but the empty card visually dominates the central area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the playlist-items-list within the left column while active. It is static in the scroll flow and keeps the two-column structure intact; as content is added, it disappears and yields space back to the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: On errors (e.g., failing to load playlist data), the playlist-error-state displays a strong but compact alert band within the main content container, just below the playlist-header and above any playlist content. This band spans the full internal width of the content container, bridging across both the left and right columns, and sits with about 24px vertical spacing from the bottom of the header. It is 56–72px tall with 16–24px internal padding, using a dark red or deep maroon background that contrasts against both the white header above and the black main background. Inside, an error icon appears aligned to the left, followed by a concise error message and, if appropriate, a subtle link-style action such as “Retry.” The text aligns with the same left margin as the playlist title above to preserve grid consistency. Playlist content below may be dimmed, replaced, or simply separated by additional vertical space (16–24px), making the error band the first element the eye encounters in the main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element within the main content flow. It pushes playlist content downward while visible and can be dismissed or hidden when resolved, restoring the standard spacing between the header band and the top of the playlist content.

name: Videos Index
title: Videos
description: Displays all published band videos with sorting and optional filtering by type and release year, and allows navigation to individual video detail pages.

core_features: Load and display a list of published videos using the listVideos interface, with thumbnails, titles, release dates, and optional metadata such as duration.
Apply sorting to the video list based on a URL-driven sort parameter (e.g., defaulting to Release Date: Newest First when no sort is provided).
Filter the video list by video type (using the Video.videoType field) and release year (derived from Video.releaseDate) via URL parameters.
Allow users to change sort and filter controls, which update the URL parameters and trigger a fresh listVideos call without using localStorage.
Enable navigation to an individual video detail page (video.html) when a user clicks on a video thumbnail or title, passing the video ID via URL.
Provide feedback on the active filters and total results, and allow resetting filters to return to the full video list.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View latest videos
1. User opens videos.html without any URL parameters.
2. The page controller reads the URL, finds no sort, videoType, or year parameters, and applies a default sort value (e.g., 'release_desc').
3. The page controller calls listVideos(sort='release_desc', filters={}) to retrieve published videos.
4. The video grid displays the returned videos in the default sort order.
5. The results summary shows the total number of videos and indicates that no filters are active.

Workflow 2: Filter by video type and year
1. User opens videos.html or lands here from another page (e.g., index.html) with or without a sort parameter.
2. The page controller reads sort, videoType, and year from the URL (if present) and constructs filters from videoType and year.
3. The page controller calls listVideos(sort, filters={ videoType, year }).
4. The video grid displays only the videos that match the specified type and release year.
5. The filter bar shows the currently selected type and year as active values.
6. The user changes the video type or year in the filter bar; the filter bar updates the URL (keeping other parameters) and triggers the controller to re-read the URL and call listVideos again with the new filters.
7. The user can click a reset/clear button in the filter bar to remove videoType and year from the URL, which triggers a new listVideos call with filters={}.

Workflow 3: Open a video detail page
1. User is viewing the videos list with any combination of sort and filters applied.
2. The user clicks on a video thumbnail or title in the video grid.
3. The video grid component reads the clicked video's id and navigates to video.html?id=<videoId>.
4. The browser loads video.html, which can use the id parameter to display the selected video's details.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads URL parameters sort, videoType, and year, constructs a filters object, and calls listVideos(sort, filters) to fetch the list of Video entities.

When the user changes the sort option in the sort controls, the sort-controls component updates the sort parameter in the query string (preserving existing videoType and year if present) and triggers a URL change that causes the controller to re-fetch videos via listVideos with the updated sort.

When the user selects or changes a video type in the filter bar, the filter-bar component updates the videoType parameter in the query string, removes it if cleared, and triggers a URL change that causes the controller to re-fetch videos with the updated filters.

When the user selects or changes a year in the filter bar, the filter-bar component updates the year parameter in the query string, removes it if cleared, and triggers a URL change that causes the controller to re-fetch videos with the updated filters.

When the user clicks a 'Reset filters' control in the filter bar, the filter-bar component removes videoType and year from the query string (leaving sort unchanged) and triggers a URL change; the controller then calls listVideos with filters = {}.
When the user clicks on a video item (thumbnail or title) in the video grid, the video-grid component navigates to video.html?id=<id of the clicked Video>.

If the listVideos call returns no videos for the current filters, the empty-state component shows a message and may provide a 'Clear filters' action; clicking this action clears videoType and year from the URL and triggers reloading the unfiltered list.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from URL query parameters and the listVideos interface response; no localStorage or hardcoded data is used. On every initial load and on any subsequent URL change (e.g., via pushState/popstate or full reload), the page controller reads three key parameters: sort (string), videoType (string), and year (string).
