If sort is absent, the controller applies an internal default value for sort and does not write it to the URL unless the user explicitly changes it. The controller constructs a filters object for the listVideos interface such that filters.videoType is set only if the videoType URL parameter is present and non-empty, and filters.year is set only if the year URL parameter is present and non-empty; otherwise, those fields are omitted from the filters object. The controller then calls listVideos(sort, filters) to obtain an array of Video entities that represent the current state. All UI components (sort controls, filter bar, results summary, and video grid) derive their displayed state directly from the URL parameters and the latest listVideos result. When a user interacts with sort or filter controls, those components update the URL query parameters (adding, changing, or removing sort, videoType, and year as needed), merge them with any unaffected parameters, and trigger a controlled navigation or history update. The controller listens for URL changes and re-invokes listVideos with parameters derived from the new URL, keeping the entire page state shareable and bookmarkable. Navigation from this page to video.html transfers only the selected video's ID via the id parameter, leaving all other state (sort, videoType, year) behind in the current page URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates the Videos page behavior: reads URL parameters, calls listVideos with the appropriate sort and filter arguments, distributes the resulting Video list and current state to child components, and reacts to URL changes.

Displays the current sort mode for videos and allows the user to change the sort order. When changed, it updates the sort parameter in the URL and triggers reloading of the video list through the controller and listVideos.

Provides controls to filter videos by video type and release year. It reads the active videoType and year from the URL, shows them as selected values, and updates the URL parameters when the user adjusts filters or resets them.

Shows a summary of the current result set (such as total number of videos returned) and indicates which filters are active by reflecting the current sort, videoType, and year state passed down from the controller.

Renders the list of videos returned by listVideos, including thumbnail, title, release date, and any additional metadata that should be visible in the index. Each item is clickable and navigates to that video's detail page.

Displays a no-results message when listVideos returns an empty array for the current sort and filters. Offers an action to clear filters, which updates URL parameters and re-triggers the controller to load the full list.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom sequence: a full-width dark header (global, outside this scope), followed by a full-width white page-title band labeled 'VIDEOS', then a centered main content container on a dark, textured background with a two-column layout. All video index components live in the left main column, stacked vertically in this order: video-sort-controls at the top, then video-filter-bar directly beneath it, followed by the video-results-summary, and then the video-grid. The video-empty-state replaces the grid in the same slot when there are no results. The right sidebar (existing widgets like search, recent posts, categories) remains visually secondary and does not interfere with the left column’s linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom sequence: a full-width dark header (global, outside this scope), followed by a full-width white page-title band labeled 'VIDEOS', then a centered main content container on a dark, textured background with a two-column layout. All video index components live in the left main column, stacked vertically in this order: video-sort-controls at the top, then video-filter-bar directly beneath it, followed by the video-results-summary, and then the video-grid. The video-empty-state replaces the grid in the same slot when there are no results. The right sidebar (existing widgets like search, recent posts, categories) remains visually secondary and does not interfere with the left column’s linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the header and title band, the main content area is a fixed-width, centered container using roughly 12-column grid logic. The left main column occupies about 65–70% of this container’s width; the right sidebar uses the remaining 30–35%. Vertically, the white title band consumes about 15–20% of the initial viewport height with generous padding above and below the page title. Inside the main column, the toolbar area (sort-controls + filter-bar + summary) takes a relatively shallow vertical slice, while the video-grid dominates most of the vertical space. Vertical spacing between major blocks (toolbar vs. grid) is in the 24–32px range, with tighter 12–16px spacing between closely related sub-elements (sort vs. filter, filter vs. summary).
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and page-title band are airy with substantial negative space. The main video column is denser but still breathable: controls are grouped yet not cramped, and the video-grid uses a clear grid with consistent gutters. Each row in the grid uses comfortable padding between thumbnails to avoid visual clutter, in line with a modern but slightly grunge aesthetic. Text lines are short-to-medium length within the main column, and no element stretches edge-to-edge without inner padding, preserving a structured, minimalist feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the band logo and nav in the dark header, then drops to the high-contrast 'VIDEOS' title in the white band. From there, the eye moves straight down into the left main column, first encountering the sort-controls and filter-bar as a compact, functional toolbar. After scanning these controls left-to-right, attention moves down to the results summary line, and then into the video-grid, where the eye scans thumbnails in an F-pattern: down the leftmost column of videos, then across rows. The right sidebar draws secondary attention as the user’s gaze occasionally shifts rightward after exploring the main content. When the empty-state is shown, it becomes the focal point of the main column, replacing the visual gravity of the grid.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark, slightly textured background with a full-width black header at the top and a hand-drawn band logo on the left, navigation links stretching to the right. Directly beneath, a full-width white title band spans the screen, creating strong contrast: centered within this band, slightly left-weighted, the word 'VIDEOS' appears large and bold with generous vertical padding above and below, leaving a lot of clean, empty space. Below this band, the main content snaps into a centered, fixed-width container, floating on the dark background. Inside this container, the layout splits into two columns: a wide left main column and a narrower right sidebar. The left column starts with a lean toolbar area for sorting and filtering, followed by a slim results-summary line, and then a large, multi-row video grid that visually dominates the page. Each video tile has strong contrast and sits in a neat grid with consistent gaps, reinforcing the professional but edgy vibe. To the right, the sidebar stacks its existing widgets vertically, visually aligned with the top of the main column. Overall, the composition feels structured and modern but retains a raw, band-friendly atmosphere through its dark palette, high contrast, and restrained, grid-based ordering of elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The videos-index-controller defines the main page canvas for this view, anchoring all video-related components within the existing two-column layout. It begins directly below the full-width white 'VIDEOS' title band, occupying the full width of the centered main content container. Inside, it divides space into a left main column (about 65–70% of the container width) and a right sidebar (about 30–35%), aligned to a 12-column grid. The left column runs from just beneath the title band down to the fold and beyond, stacking the video-sort-controls at the top, followed immediately by the video-filter-bar, then the video-results-summary, and finally either the video-grid or, conditionally, the video-empty-state. The right sidebar starts at approximately the same top baseline as the video-sort-controls, maintaining a clear vertical alignment between columns. Horizontal padding of roughly 16–24px is maintained between the main content and the container edges, while vertical spacing of about 32px separates the controller’s top (just under the title band) from its first child component, echoing the spaciousness seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container that scrolls with the page; responsible for maintaining the two-column layout and vertical stacking order for the main video components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-sort-controls sit at the very top of the left main column, directly under the white title band with a generous gap of approximately 32px, making them the first interactive element the user encounters on this page. They span the full width of the left column but leave comfortable inner padding (around 16–24px) on the left and right so controls never touch the column edges. Within this horizontal strip, primary sort options (e.g., a 'Sort by' label and dropdown) are aligned to the left, while any auxiliary controls (like a view toggle or ordering icon) align to the right, creating a balanced, bar-like appearance. The bar’s height is modest—just tall enough for the controls plus about 8–12px vertical padding above and below—keeping it visually compact while still clear and clickable. A small vertical gap of about 12–16px separates the sort-controls from the video-filter-bar immediately below, visually grouping them as a unified toolbar section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal strip at the top of the left column; scrolls normally with the main content. It defines the top baseline for other video-related elements in this column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-filter-bar is positioned directly beneath the video-sort-controls in the left main column, separated by a tight vertical gap of roughly 12–16px to visually connect it as part of the same control cluster. It also spans the full width of the left column with matching inner horizontal padding, creating a continuous, aligned edge with the sort-controls above. The filter-bar appears as a long horizontal band populated with pill-shaped filter chips, toggle buttons, or dropdowns, arranged left-to-right. On wider screens, filters are laid out in a single row with a modest horizontal gap between each; if the number of filters exceeds available width, they can wrap onto a second line, increasing the bar’s height while still feeling compact. There is about 8–12px of vertical padding inside the bar so individual filter pills have breathing room. A slightly stronger or outlined treatment (compared to the summary line below) gives it clear importance without overpowering the grid. The filter-bar sits about 8–12px above the video-results-summary, keeping the control cluster tight and cohesive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a horizontal control band that scrolls with the page; may be optionally configured as sticky within the left column (sticking to the top of the viewport when the user scrolls past it) to keep filters accessible, but it never overlaps the header or title band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-results-summary is placed directly below the video-filter-bar in the left main column, with a small vertical gap of approximately 8–12px. It is a slim horizontal line of text, aligned to the left edge of the column’s content area, that might read something like '12 videos • Filter: Live, 2023'. It uses lighter visual weight (smaller font size or lower contrast) compared to the filter and sort controls, allowing it to be seen at a glance without competing for attention. The summary spans the full column width but remains low in height—just its text plus minimal vertical padding—so that it acts as a subtle delimiter between the toolbar area and the heavier video-grid below. A slightly larger gap of around 16–24px is maintained between this summary and the top of the video-grid, signaling the transition from controls to content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text strip that scrolls with the main column, anchoring directly above the video-grid or the video-empty-state. No sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-grid occupies the main bulk of the left column, starting immediately below the video-results-summary with a comfortable vertical gap of about 16–24px. It spans the full interior width of the column, minus consistent left and right padding (16–24px) that align with the controls above. Within this area, videos are presented as a responsive grid of cards or thumbnails: on larger screens, typically three columns across (each card roughly 30–32% of the column width) with equal horizontal gutters of about 16–24px and matching vertical spacing between rows. Each video tile is vertically oriented, showing a prominent thumbnail, title, and minimal metadata, giving each item enough space to feel distinct but still part of a dense media grid. The grid stretches downward, becoming the visually dominant area of the page and creating a strong column of imagery that mirrors the large post artwork behavior from the mockup. As the user scrolls, the grid extends further, always maintaining its alignment and spacing relative to the sidebar, which continues independently on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Expanding, scrollable grid area that grows vertically with the number of videos. It remains confined to the left main column and scrolls naturally with the page, without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-empty-state occupies the same spatial slot as the video-grid and is only rendered when there are no matching videos. It begins at the exact position where the top of the video-grid would normally appear—directly beneath the video-results-summary with a gap of about 16–24px—ensuring layout consistency. Instead of multiple tiles, this area presents a single, centered message block. Horizontally, the block is centered within the left column, not the whole page, respecting the two-column layout; it may use a max-width of around 50–60% of the column to keep the message readable and focused. Vertically, generous padding (32–48px above and below the message) creates a calm, spacious feel, with optional iconography or a simple illustration above the text to echo the band’s edgy style. This space is noticeably more open than the dense grid, signaling the absence of content while still fitting the minimalist, high-contrast aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the video-grid: when active, it replaces the grid and occupies its vertical space. It scrolls with the page as a normal block-level element within the left column.

name: Video Detail
title: Video
description: Page for viewing a single video and adding it to one of the user’s playlists, including Favorites.

core_features: Read the video ID from the URL parameter and load video details using getVideoDetail(videoId).
Render a video player for the loaded video using the videoUrl and basic playback controls.
Display video metadata including title, description, release date, duration, video type, and related album title when available.
Provide an Add to playlist action that opens a playlist selection dialog.
Within the selection dialog, load all available playlists using listPlaylists() so the user can choose a target playlist.
Allow the user to create a new playlist via a form that calls createPlaylist(name, description, visibility) directly from the dialog.
When a playlist is selected (existing or newly created), add the currently viewed video to that playlist using addVideoToPlaylist(videoId, playlistId).
Show a clear success confirmation after adding a video to a playlist, including an optional navigation link to the affected playlist page.
Handle and display error states for failed video loading, playlist loading, playlist creation, or add-to-playlist operations.
user_workflows: View a video from another page: 1) User clicks a video on videos.html, index.html, or playlist.html; 2) Browser navigates to video.html?id={videoId}; 3) On load, the page reads the id parameter; 4) The page calls getVideoDetail(id); 5) While loading, a loading state is shown; 6) On success, the video player and metadata are displayed; 7) On failure (missing or invalid id, or API error), an error state is shown instead of the player.

Add the current video to an existing playlist: 1) User is on video.html?id={videoId} and the video is successfully loaded; 2) User clicks the Add to playlist control; 3) The playlist selection dialog opens and triggers listPlaylists(); 4) On success, the available playlists are listed with selection controls; 5) User chooses one playlist and submits; 6) The page calls addVideoToPlaylist(currentVideoId, selectedPlaylistId); 7) On success, the dialog closes and a confirmation banner or inline message appears; 8) The confirmation includes a link to playlist.html?id={selectedPlaylistId} to view that playlist.

Create a new playlist and add the current video to it: 1) User opens the playlist selection dialog from the Add to playlist control; 2) In the dialog, user switches to the Create new playlist option; 3) User fills in the playlist name and optional description and selects a visibility value; 4) User submits the create playlist form; 5) The page calls createPlaylist(name, description, visibility); 6) On success, the new playlistId is captured; 7) Immediately after creation, the page calls addVideoToPlaylist(currentVideoId, newPlaylistId); 8) On success, the dialog closes and a success confirmation is displayed, with a link to playlist.html?id={newPlaylistId}.

Add to the Favorites playlist and view it: 1) User opens the playlist selection dialog for the current video; 2) The dialog loads playlists via listPlaylists(); 3) Among the results, the system playlist with systemType equal to 'favorites' (if present) is shown; 4) User selects that playlist and submits; 5) The page calls addVideoToPlaylist(currentVideoId, favoritesPlaylistId); 6) On success, a confirmation message indicates that the video was added to Favorites and includes a View Favorites link; 7) Clicking the link navigates to playlist.html?id={favoritesPlaylistId}.

Handle errors during playlist operations: 1) User attempts to add the video to a playlist or create a new playlist; 2) The relevant interface call (listPlaylists, createPlaylist, addVideoToPlaylist) fails; 3) The dialog or page shows an error message explaining the failure in generic terms (without hardcoded content); 4) The user can retry the action, select a different playlist, or close the dialog and continue watching the video.
interactions: On page load, the page reads the id URL parameter; if present, it triggers getVideoDetail with that value; if missing, it sets an error state instead of calling the interface.
User clicks the main play button on the video player to start playback; subsequent clicks on pause/resume update playback state locally but do not call external interfaces.
User clicks an Add to playlist button or link near the video player/metadata; this sets an internal state flag to open the playlist selection dialog.
When the playlist selection dialog opens for the first time in a session, it triggers listPlaylists(); while the playlists are loading, a loading state is shown in the dialog.

In the playlist selection dialog, the user can: select one of the loaded playlists; switch to or enable a Create new playlist form; input a playlist name; optionally input a description; select a visibility option; and submit the form.

Submitting the existing playlist selection triggers addVideoToPlaylist with the current video ID and the chosen playlist ID; the UI shows a processing state for that action until the interface responds.

Submitting the create playlist form triggers createPlaylist; on success, the dialog immediately triggers addVideoToPlaylist for the newly created playlist; if createPlaylist fails, the dialog shows an error and allows the user to correct inputs or retry.

On successful addVideoToPlaylist, the dialog closes (or switches to a success state), and a confirmation component becomes visible on the page, presenting a message and a link to view the affected playlist.
Clicking the View playlist or View Favorites link navigates to playlist.html with the playlistId set in the id URL parameter.

If any interface call returns an error, the respective component (video detail area, playlist dialog, or confirmation area) shows an error message and provides a way to dismiss the error or retry the action, without reloading the entire page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by the id URL parameter, which is interpreted as the videoId. On initial load and whenever the URL changes to a different id, the page calls getVideoDetail(id) and sets internal loading and error flags based on the response. If id is missing or empty, the page enters an 'invalid-parameter' state and does not call getVideoDetail. The currently selected videoId is passed to playlist-related operations. Opening the playlist selection dialog sets an internal 'playlistDialogOpen' flag. When that flag transitions from false to true, the dialog checks whether playlist data has been loaded; if not, it calls listPlaylists(). The playlists returned are stored in in-memory state for the dialog session only. Selection of an existing playlist sets 'selectedPlaylistId' in the dialog state. Switching to the create-new flow clears 'selectedPlaylistId' and activates form fields for name, description, and visibility; these fields exist only in client state until createPlaylist is called. On successful createPlaylist, the returned playlistId becomes the effective selectedPlaylistId and is used immediately in addVideoToPlaylist. Success of addVideoToPlaylist sets 'lastAddedPlaylistId' and 'lastAddedPlaylistName' in page state, drives the display of the confirmation component, and may conditionally set a flag like 'lastAddedIsFavorites' if the playlist is recognized (via listPlaylists data) as having systemType equal to 'favorites'. All transient states such as loading indicators, dialog open/close status, selected playlist, and in-progress flags are maintained in memory for the current page instance only and are not persisted to URL parameters or localStorage. The only URL-driven state is the current videoId for getVideoDetail and, when navigating away to playlist.html, the target playlist's id.

components.functionality: Reads the id parameter from the URL, orchestrates loading of the video details via getVideoDetail, holds top-level loading and error states, and passes the loaded Video data and current videoId to child components such as the player, metadata, and playlist controls.

Renders the video playback element using the videoUrl from the loaded Video entity and exposes play/pause/seek controls. It may also display the thumbnail as a poster image before playback starts and show basic time information derived from durationSeconds, but it does not call any external interfaces.

Displays non-interactive details for the current video, including title, releaseDate, durationSeconds, videoType, description, and optionally albumTitle when present. It reacts to changes in the loaded Video data from the page controller and shows placeholder or loading states while data is being fetched.

Provides an Add to playlist control (such as a button or link) that the user can click to start the process of adding the current video to a playlist. When activated, it signals the playlist-selection-dialog component to open and passes along the current videoId.

When opened, loads available playlists using listPlaylists() and allows the user to either select an existing playlist or switch to a create-new playlist mode. It maintains internal state for playlists, loading and error flags, selectedPlaylistId, and create-playlist form values (name, description, visibility). On confirming an existing playlist selection, it calls addVideoToPlaylist(videoId, selectedPlaylistId). On switching to create mode and submitting, it calls createPlaylist(name, description, visibility), and upon success it immediately calls addVideoToPlaylist with the returned playlistId. It reports success or errors back to the page controller so that confirmations or error messages can be shown.

Embedded within the playlist-selection-dialog, this form captures the new playlist’s name, optional description, and required visibility selection. On submit, it validates the fields and invokes createPlaylist(name, description, visibility) via the dialog controller, then waits for the result so that the dialog can proceed to addVideoToPlaylist for the newly created playlist.

Shows a success message after addVideoToPlaylist completes, indicating that the current video has been added to a specific playlist. It receives the lastAddedPlaylistId and name from the page controller and, when available, a flag indicating whether this playlist is recognized as a Favorites playlist based on listPlaylists metadata. It includes a link to navigate to playlist.html?id={lastAddedPlaylistId}.

Displays an error message when getVideoDetail fails or when the id URL parameter is missing or invalid. It can offer a retry control that re-triggers getVideoDetail with the current id, as long as an id is present.

Used inside the playlist-selection-dialog to show errors from listPlaylists, createPlaylist, or addVideoToPlaylist calls. It informs the user that the action could not be completed and allows them to retry the operation or close the dialog without discarding the main video view.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s vertical structure: a full-width dark header (site-level), followed by a full-width white title band labeled for the Video Detail context, then a centered main content container on a dark, slightly textured background. Inside this container, use a two-column layout: the left (wider) column contains the video player at the top with its metadata and actions below (including the add-to-playlist trigger). The right (narrower) column is reserved for existing site sidebar widgets (search, recent posts, categories, etc.), visually echoing the mockup. Overlays (playlist-selection dialog, playlist-create form) sit on top of everything as modals, while notification and error states snap into predefined positions within this structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s vertical structure: a full-width dark header (site-level), followed by a full-width white title band labeled for the Video Detail context, then a centered main content container on a dark, slightly textured background. Inside this container, use a two-column layout: the left (wider) column contains the video player at the top with its metadata and actions below (including the add-to-playlist trigger). The right (narrower) column is reserved for existing site sidebar widgets (search, recent posts, categories, etc.), visually echoing the mockup. Overlays (playlist-selection dialog, playlist-create form) sit on top of everything as modals, while notification and error states snap into predefined positions within this structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Use a 12-column centered grid with the main content constrained to a fixed width (roughly 960–1140px). Allocate about 65–70% of this width (≈8 columns) to the left main column (video player + metadata + playlist trigger) and about 30–35% (≈4 columns) to the right sidebar. Vertically, keep the header compact, the white title band taller and airy (about 15–20% of initial viewport height), and let the main content area occupy the majority of the remaining height. The video player should visually dominate the left column width, with the metadata panel and add-to-playlist trigger sitting comfortably beneath. Modals are centered and use roughly 40–50% of viewport width, with ample internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Match the mockup’s moderately spacious feel: generous whitespace in the header and white title band, slightly tighter but still breathable spacing in the main content. Within the left column, the video player and metadata panel use 16–24px internal padding and 24–32px vertical gaps to feel solid but not crowded. Sidebar widgets remain compact with 16px vertical separation. The playlist-selection dialog follows the same density: clear grouping, 16–24px padding, and 16px gaps between form elements and list items. Error messages and notification banners are slim but clearly separated bands with 8–16px padding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Respect the F-pattern: user attention starts top-left on the band logo, scans right along the navigation, then drops into the white title band where the page title (e.g., “VIDEO DETAIL” or the video’s name) anchors them. From there, the eye falls straight down to the prominent video player in the left column. After the video player, attention moves down into the metadata panel and immediately to the right-aligned add-to-playlist trigger. Once the user is in an ‘add’ flow, the overlay dialog becomes the focal point in the center of the screen, with the confirmation banner appearing just under the title band as a follow-up cue. Sidebar widgets remain secondary and are picked up mainly by secondary horizontal scans to the right.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark, subtly textured background with a full-width, low-profile black header bar at the top: the band’s hand-drawn logo anchors the left, and a simple navigation runs along the right. Immediately beneath, a stark white horizontal band stretches full width, with the video page title (for example, “VIDEO DETAIL” and possibly the video’s name as a subtitle) centered or left-aligned, surrounded by generous vertical padding that creates a clean, high-contrast break from the dark chrome above and below. Below this title band, the main content container is centered on the page as a fixed-width block and adopts a two-column layout. The left column, taking roughly two-thirds of the width, starts with a large, 16:9 video player that almost fills the column horizontally and establishes the visual anchor of the page. Beneath the player, the video metadata panel runs the full width of the column, presenting title, stats, and description with a right-aligned, high-contrast ‘Add to Playlist’ button integrated into its top row. To the right, a narrower sidebar column echoes the mockup’s structure, stacking site widgets like search, recent posts, and categories with compact spacing and clear headings. When the user initiates an add-to-playlist action, a dim, semi-transparent black overlay washes over the entire viewport, and a centered dialog appears above the two-column content—dark, crisp, and boxy—containing playlist selection and creation controls. Any playlist-related errors surface inside this dialog near its top, while a successful addition triggers a slim notification banner that appears just under the white title band, aligned with the central content width. Video errors replace the player area with an error state card in the same position, ensuring the layout remains stable while communicating issues clearly. Overall, the page feels dark, edgy, and structured, with a strong, deliberate vertical flow punctuated by clear horizontal bands and a dominant left-column focus on the video.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-detail-container spans the full width of the viewport and the full height between the global header and the footer. It inherits the dark, slightly grungy textured background used in the mockup. Within it, below the existing header and the full-width white title band, a centered fixed-width content block (approximately 960–1140px wide) is placed. This block is horizontally divided into a left main column (about 65–70% of the block width, roughly 8 out of 12 grid columns) and a right sidebar column (about 30–35%, roughly 4 out of 12 grid columns). The main column runs down the left side of the container, beginning a comfortable 32px below the bottom edge of the white title band. The video player sits at the very top of this main column, followed by the video metadata panel and add-to-playlist trigger. The right sidebar column begins at the same vertical starting line as the video player, stacking other site widgets vertically. Within the video-detail-container, vertical spacing between stacked sections (player → metadata → any further content) is approximately 24–32px, echoing the mockup’s moderate spacing rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary layout wrapper for the page body, participating in normal document flow and constraining the two-column grid; it is not fixed or sticky but defines the positional context for inner components and overlays.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-player is positioned at the top of the left main column within the centered content block, approximately 32px below the white page-title band. It spans nearly the full width of the left column (about 95–100% of the 8 grid columns), with 16–24px of internal padding or margin from any card frame, so it feels expansive and dominant. Its aspect ratio is 16:9, so on a desktop viewport, it occupies a visually significant horizontal band across the left side of the page, roughly 40–50% of the viewport height above the fold. The player’s outer frame or background is a slightly lighter black/charcoal than the page background, possibly with a thin, subtle border or glow to emphasize it as the focal element. Beneath the player, a vertical gap of around 24px separates it from the video-metadata-panel, keeping the hierarchy clear while maintaining the moderately dense content feel of the main area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal flow within the left column, expanding responsively with the container width while maintaining its 16:9 aspect ratio; it is not fixed or sticky, but remains the anchoring element of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-metadata-panel sits directly below the video-player in the left main column, separated by about 24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the left column and is rendered as a dark card or text block with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. At the top of this panel, the video title and key metadata (such as views, duration, and date) are aligned starting from the left, with the add-to-playlist-trigger sharing the same horizontal line and aligned to the right edge of the panel. Below this top row, more detailed metadata (description, credits, tags) stacks vertically with 12–16px spacing between text sections. The panel’s height is flexible, expanding based on content, but the overall presentation remains compact enough that the entire player plus the initial metadata and the add-to-playlist button are likely visible above the fold on a standard desktop viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in normal document flow, following the video player and pushing subsequent content down; it doesn’t fix or float, but its top row acts as a key interaction hub by housing the add-to-playlist trigger.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-playlist-trigger is integrated into the top row of the video-metadata-panel, visually aligned with the video title and key meta information. It appears as a high-contrast button on the far right side of the panel’s top line, horizontally flush with the panel’s right padding edge. The button’s size is medium, roughly 120–160px wide and 36–44px tall, with 8–12px internal padding, and its styling contrasts sharply against the dark card (for example, a solid accent color or a light-outline button on a dark background). There is approximately 8–12px horizontal spacing between this button and any neighboring elements in the top row, ensuring it stands out without feeling cramped. Vertically, it is aligned centrally with the title text line, making it immediately discoverable when the user moves from the video player down to the metadata. On narrower screens, the button can break onto a new line directly beneath the title but still right-aligned within the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal flow within the metadata panel. It does not float independently; instead, it is anchored to the top-right of the panel and, when activated, triggers the overlay playlist-selection-dialog without changing its own position on the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-selection-dialog appears as a modal overlay centered in the viewport, sitting above the entire two-column layout. When active, it is accompanied by a semi-transparent black backdrop that covers the full viewport width and height, slightly dimming the header, title band, and main content beneath. The dialog itself is a rectangular card, roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (for example, 480–560px on desktop) and about 50–60% of the viewport height, with a dark background slightly lighter than the page’s base black, creating a clear but subtle separation. It is horizontally centered and vertically placed slightly above true center (to feel more connected to the content rather than floating too low), with 24px of internal padding on all sides. At the top of the dialog, a bold heading like “Add to Playlist” is left-aligned, with a close icon on the top-right. Directly beneath the heading, there is a 16px vertical gap leading into the content area where existing playlists are presented as a vertically scrollable list with each item taking a full row width inside the dialog, separated by 8–12px. At the bottom of the content area, a clear affordance to create a new playlist is placed, which, when engaged, reveals the playlist-create-form within the same dialog, pushing the list area upwards. The dialog has rounded or subtly beveled corners consistent with the site’s aesthetic, and it always remains fully visible without requiring the user to scroll the page beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay relative to the viewport, centered horizontally and slightly above vertical center. It remains in place when the underlying page scrolls (page scrolling should typically be locked while the dialog is open), ensuring focus stays on the playlist selection task.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-create-form is embedded inside the lower portion of the playlist-selection-dialog, occupying the full dialog width minus its horizontal padding. When revealed, it appears directly beneath the list of existing playlists, separated by about 16px of vertical space or a subtle divider line. The form consists of a text input for the playlist name stretching nearly the full width of the dialog interior, a smaller secondary control (such as a privacy toggle or dropdown) placed either on a second row below the name input or aligned to the right on the same row if space allows, and a primary action button (e.g., “Create & Add”) placed on the right side beneath the inputs. Each input field has 8–12px internal padding and is separated by about 8–12px vertically. When the form appears, the playlist list area above shrinks in height, potentially becoming scrollable if there are many playlists, so that the form and its button remain fully visible without requiring dialog-level scrolling. This keeps the form anchored near the bottom half of the dialog, visually connected to the “Add to Playlist” context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the playlist-selection-dialog content area; it is revealed or hidden without moving the dialog’s outer frame, pushing the playlist list area upward as needed. It does not scroll independently from the dialog but may cause the playlist list area above to become scrollable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-playlist-confirmation appears as a slim notification banner just beneath the full-width white title band, aligned with the centered main content container rather than spanning edge-to-edge across the entire viewport. It is positioned at the top of the video-detail-container’s main content area, so visually it sits above the two-column arrangement of video and sidebar but below the title band, forming a distinct horizontal stripe of feedback. The banner’s width matches the fixed-width content container (approximately 960–1140px), and its height is around 40–56px, with 8–16px of vertical padding inside. Its background color subtly stands out from the dark page (for example, a muted green or slightly brighter gray with a colored accent line at the top), and the success message text is left-aligned. A small close/dismiss icon is placed on the far right of the banner, aligned vertically with the text. A vertical gap of about 16–24px separates this banner from the content directly below (the top of the video player and sidebar), preserving the layout’s breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal flow at the top of the main content area, pushing the content beneath (video player and sidebar widgets) downward when visible. It remains fixed to this position and does not scroll independently; as the user scrolls the page, the banner scrolls out of view with the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The video-error-state occupies exactly the same footprint as the video-player within the left main column, ensuring layout stability when an error occurs. It is placed at the top of the left column, about 32px below the white page-title band, spanning nearly the full width of the column and maintaining a similar 16:9 rectangular shape. Instead of the player controls, the area shows a dark error card with a slightly contrasting border or subtle red accent. The error message text is centered both horizontally and vertically within this area, with a concise headline (e.g., “Video unavailable”) and a smaller explanatory line beneath. Below the text, a retry or alternative action button is centered, with 16px of vertical spacing between the lines. This error card preserves the strong visual anchor at the top of the main column while clearly communicating the failure, and the video-metadata-panel remains directly below it, separated by the usual 24px gap, so the user still has context about the video but immediately understands that playback failed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an inline replacement for the video-player, appearing in its place in normal document flow when an error state is active; it does not overlay other content, and it scrolls with the page like any other content block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playlist-error-display is shown inside the playlist-selection-dialog, directly beneath the dialog’s title row and above the playlist list and playlist-create-form. It spans the full interior width of the dialog (minus the 24px horizontal padding) and appears as a compact horizontal band or alert card, about 40–56px tall, with 8–12px of internal padding. Its background is a dark red-tinted tone that contrasts with the dialog’s base dark background while still fitting the muted, edgy palette. Error text is left-aligned and concise, and a small close or dismiss icon sits on the right edge, aligned vertically with the text. There is approximately 12–16px of vertical space separating this error band from the heading above and the playlist list below, so it feels inserted into the flow but not cramped. When visible, it pushes the playlist list and any playlist-create-form content downward inside the dialog, keeping all elements within a single scrollable visual frame if needed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives within the fixed-position playlist-selection-dialog and participates in its internal flow. It does not affect the underlying page layout, only the vertical space inside the dialog, and scrolls with any content inside the dialog if the content exceeds the dialog height.

name: Tour Dates
title: Tour Dates
description: Page for viewing, filtering, and navigating upcoming tour events, using URL parameters to control filters and linking to individual event detail pages.

core_features: Load upcoming tour events from the listTourEvents interface using optional city, startDate, endDate, and onlyOnSale filters derived from the URL.
Display a structured list of tour events with key details such as event name, city, venue, country (if available), date and time, and ticket sale status.
Provide a filter form that allows users to refine results by city, date range, and whether tickets are currently on sale; submitting the form updates URL parameters and reloads results.
Allow users to click any event row/card to navigate to the event detail page (event.html) with the event ID passed as a URL parameter.
Highlight selected events (for example, the nearest upcoming shows or events with tickets currently on sale) in a dedicated section derived from the same Event list.
Handle empty and error states functionally (e.g., no events matching filters) and offer quick actions such as clearing filters.
user_workflows: Browse all upcoming tour dates: 1) User opens tour.html with no query parameters. 2) The page controller reads the empty filter state and calls listTourEvents with city = undefined, startDate = undefined, endDate = undefined, onlyOnSale = undefined. 3) The events list displays all returned upcoming events ordered by event date/time (client-side). 4) User scans the list; highlighted events are computed from the same result set and shown in the highlighted section.

Filter shows by city and date range: 1) User types a city into the City input and optionally selects a start and end date in the filter form. 2) User submits the filter form. 3) The filter component updates the browser URL to include city, startDate, and endDate parameters for tour.html, preserving or updating onlyOnSale as needed. 4) The page controller detects the URL change, calls listTourEvents with the new parameters, and updates the events list and highlighted events accordingly.

Show only events with tickets currently on sale: 1) User toggles the "Only events with tickets on sale" control in the filter form. 2) On submit, the filter form updates the URL with onlyOnSale set to true or false. 3) The page controller reads onlyOnSale from the URL and passes it to listTourEvents; results now include only events whose ticketsOnSale flag matches the filter when true. 4) The list and highlighted section refresh to show only currently on-sale events.

Open an event from another page with pre-applied city filter: 1) On index.html or another page, the user clicks a navigation link that points to tour.html with a city query parameter set (e.g., tour.html?city=<selectedCity>). 2) The user lands on tour.html; the page controller reads city from the URL and calls listTourEvents with that city. 3) The filter form pre-fills the City field with the URL city value so the user can see and adjust it. 4) The events list shows only events in that city; the user can further refine dates or the on-sale toggle.

View details for a specific event: 1) After filters are applied and the list is shown, the user clicks on an event entry in the events list or highlighted section. 2) The event list component reads the event ID from the clicked item and navigates the browser to event.html?id=<eventId>. 3) event.html then uses its own logic (outside this page) to show full details and ticket purchasing options.
interactions: On page load or when the URL query string changes, the page controller parses city, startDate, endDate, and onlyOnSale from the URL, normalizes them (e.g., boolean parsing for onlyOnSale), and calls listTourEvents with these values.

On city input change in the filter form, the component updates its internal form state but does not immediately call listTourEvents; the API is only called when the user submits the form or when URL parameters change.
On date range input changes (startDate and endDate), the filter form updates its internal state and, on submit, encodes these dates in ISO format into the URL query parameters.
On toggling the "onlyOnSale" checkbox or switch, the filter form updates its internal boolean value and on submit writes onlyOnSale=true or onlyOnSale=false (as strings) into the URL.

On filter form submission, the filter component prevents default browser submission behavior, constructs a new query string from non-empty filter values (city, startDate, endDate, onlyOnSale), pushes/updates the browser URL for tour.html, and triggers the page controller to re-run listTourEvents.

On clicking a "Clear filters" action, the filter form removes city, startDate, endDate, and onlyOnSale from the URL query (navigating to bare tour.html or a minimal URL), causing the page controller to reload unfiltered events.
On event list item click (row/card), the events list component reads the event ID and performs a navigation to event.html?id=<eventId> without altering other state.

On receiving new Event[] data from listTourEvents, the events list and highlight components re-render their content, performing any client-side ordering or selection (e.g., ordering by eventDateTime ascending, selecting a subset to highlight) based solely on the Event model fields.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is represented in the URL query parameters: city, startDate, endDate, and onlyOnSale. When tour.html is loaded, the page controller parses these parameters from the window.location search string. If city is present, it is used as a string filter; if absent or empty, no city constraint is passed to listTourEvents. startDate and endDate, when present, are interpreted as ISO date strings and passed as-is to listTourEvents; if one or both are missing, that part of the range is left undefined so the API can interpret it accordingly. onlyOnSale, when present, is parsed as a boolean where 'true' yields true, 'false' yields false; if onlyOnSale is absent, it is treated as undefined and not used to constrain results. The page controller calls listTourEvents(city, startDate, endDate, onlyOnSale) every time these parameters change. The filter form initializes its inputs from the current URL values so the UI stays in sync with state. Derived UI states such as client-side sorting order and which events are highlighted are maintained as in-memory component state based on the latest Event[] data and are not stored in the URL. All navigation to event.html passes only the event ID as a query parameter (event.html?id=<eventId>) and does not modify the filter-related parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and state for tour.html. On initialization and whenever the URL query parameters change, it parses city, startDate, endDate, and onlyOnSale from the URL, calls the listTourEvents interface with those values, and distributes the resulting Event[] data and loading/error states to child components (filters, highlighted events, and events list). It also exposes callbacks that child components can use to request URL updates (e.g., applying filters).

Provides controls for the user to refine the tour events list by city, date range, and ticket sale status. On mount, it reads the current URL parameters (via the page controller) to pre-fill its fields so that the visible filter state matches the applied filters. Upon submission, it constructs an updated query string reflecting the current filter values and requests the page controller to update the browser URL, which triggers a new call to listTourEvents. It also offers a clear/reset action that removes all filter-related parameters from the URL.

Receives the full Event[] dataset from the page controller and computes a subset of events to treat as highlighted (for example, the nearest upcoming events based on eventDateTime, or events with ticketsOnSale = true). It displays these events in a compact list or section above the main events list to draw attention to key shows or upcoming legs of the tour. Clicking any highlighted event triggers navigation to event.html?id=<eventId> via the same handler used by the main events list.

Displays the main list of events returned by listTourEvents. It accepts the Event[] array plus loading and error flags from the page controller. It applies client-side ordering (for example, by eventDateTime ascending) and optional secondary grouping or ordering (such as grouping by city) using only Event fields. For each event, it shows essential information (event name, city, venueName, country if provided, eventDateTime, status, and ticketsOnSale). When there are no events, it shows an empty-state message and offers a callback to clear filters. When the user clicks any event list item, it navigates the browser to event.html?id=<eventId>.

Provides user controls to change the client-side sort order of the events list (for example, sort by event date/time ascending or descending, or by city alphabetically). Changes in these controls update internal sort state that is passed to the events list component but do not trigger new listTourEvents calls and are not encoded in the URL. The events list reacts to these changes to re-render the Event[] in the chosen order.

Handles the case where listTourEvents returns an empty Event[] for the current filters. It is activated by the page controller or the events list when events.length === 0 and no loading/error is present. Functionally, it informs the user that no events match the current filters and provides actions such as clearing filters or adjusting them (implemented via callbacks to the filter form and page controller). It does not itself call listTourEvents; instead, it triggers URL updates or filter resets that cause the controller to reload data.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear vertical flow: global dark full-width header at the very top (logo + navigation), immediately followed by a full-width, high-contrast white title band dedicated to the 'TOUR DATES' page title. Below that sits a centered main content container on a dark, textured background. Inside this container, content is split into a two-column layout: a wider left column for primary tour content (highlighted events, sorting controls, full event list or empty state) and a narrower right column acting as a sidebar for tour filtering (filter form). The arrangement mirrors the mockup’s pattern of a dominant main column and a functional sidebar with vertically stacked modules.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear vertical flow: global dark full-width header at the very top (logo + navigation), immediately followed by a full-width, high-contrast white title band dedicated to the 'TOUR DATES' page title. Below that sits a centered main content container on a dark, textured background. Inside this container, content is split into a two-column layout: a wider left column for primary tour content (highlighted events, sorting controls, full event list or empty state) and a narrower right column acting as a sidebar for tour filtering (filter form). The arrangement mirrors the mockup’s pattern of a dominant main column and a functional sidebar with vertically stacked modules.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is relatively shallow but spans 100% width. The white title band beneath it occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height with generous vertical padding to give the page title room to breathe. The main content container is centered and constrained to a fixed max-width (e.g., ~1120–1200px) on a 12-column grid. Within this container, the left main column occupies about 65–70% of the width; the right sidebar occupies about 30–35%. Vertical spacing between major bands (header → title band → main container) is around 32–48px, while spacing between components within the main area (highlighted events, sorting, list, filter form) is around 16–24px. Internal padding for each module follows the 16–24px range on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and title band remain visually spacious with minimal clutter. The title band has a large, bold 'TOUR DATES' title and maybe a short line of supporting text, both surrounded by ample negative space. The main content is denser but still breathable: the highlighted events component dominates visually but is isolated with sufficient margin. The event list presents multiple rows in a tighter vertical rhythm (16–24px between items) to efficiently show multiple dates without feeling crowded. The sidebar filter form is compact but not cramped: fields are stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing and clear group separation. Overall, the density increases as you move down into the main content, yet primary elements are never jammed together.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention follows an F-pattern echoing the mockup: first, the eye lands on the top-left band logo in the dark header, then scans right along the navigation. It then drops down to the high-contrast white title band where 'TOUR DATES' anchors attention near the left-center. From there, the gaze moves down into the main content container, immediately hitting the large, visually prominent highlighted events block in the left column. After absorbing those key dates, attention glides to the right to the top of the sidebar, where the filter form invites users to refine results. The eye then continues down the left column through the sorting controls and event list (or empty state), occasionally glancing horizontally to the sidebar if filter adjustments are needed, maintaining the classic F-shaped reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a dark, slightly textured background that gives it an underground, band-poster feel, while the structure remains clean and grid-based. Across the very top, a slim, full-width dark header holds the hand-drawn band logo on the left and navigation links aligned to the right, all with tight, 8–12px internal padding to keep it lean. Immediately below, a stark white title band spans the full width of the viewport, rising to about a fifth of the screen height. 'TOUR DATES' appears boldly toward the left side of this band with generous top and bottom padding, and any supporting tagline or date range sits smaller beneath it, surrounded by empty white space for impact. Below this, the main content is contained in a centered, fixed-width two-column layout on the dark background. The left column, occupying roughly two-thirds of the content width, opens with a large highlighted events section that nearly fills the column and visually echoes a feature artwork block. Beneath that, slim horizontal sorting controls stretch across the column, followed by a vertical stack of tour events in a list, each row clearly separated but not overly padded. If no events match the filters, a well-spaced empty state message replaces the list in this same left column. On the right, the narrower sidebar column hosts the filter form at the very top, aligned with the top of the left column’s highlighted events. The filter form feels like a compact white or mid-dark card floating against the black background, with neatly stacked controls and clear headings. Vertical spacing of 16–24px between modules in both columns maintains a rhythm that feels organized and modern, while the high contrast and dark aesthetic keep the page in line with a professional yet gritty music band brand.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour page controller defines the overarching structure between the global header and the footer. Directly under the site's full-width dark header, it introduces a full-width white title band that stretches edge-to-edge, with left-aligned 'TOUR DATES' text positioned slightly left of center within the content grid. This title band has generous vertical padding (roughly 32–48px top and bottom) to maintain the airy feel from the mockup. Below this band, the controller centers the main content within a fixed-width container (approximately 1120–1200px) on a 12-column grid. Inside this container, it divides space into a left main column and a right sidebar: the left column spans about 8 of 12 columns (around 65–70% width), and the right sidebar spans about 4 of 12 columns (around 30–35%). The top edges of these two columns align horizontally. The controller places the 'tour-highlighted-events' component at the top of the left column, followed by 'tour-list-sorting-controls' and then either 'tour-events-list' or 'tour-empty-state' directly beneath, all in a linear vertical stack with 16–24px spacing between each. In the right column, the controller positions 'tour-filter-form' at the top, aligned with the top baseline of the highlighted events on the left. Vertical spacing below this module is left available for future sidebar content while keeping visual balance with the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the structural container for the page content; static in the normal document flow, stretching full-width for the title band and then constraining the main content to a centered fixed-width two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour filter form sits in the right sidebar at the very top, aligned horizontally with the top of the highlighted events block in the left column. It occupies the full width of the sidebar column (approximately 30–35% of the main content width), appearing as a distinct module with 16–24px internal padding on all sides and clear separation from the dark background via either a slightly lighter dark panel or a subtle bordered card treatment. A small, uppercase heading like 'FILTER TOUR DATES' is placed at the top-left inside the module, with 8–12px spacing beneath before the first form control. Form elements (location dropdown, date range, country, etc.) are stacked vertically with 8–16px spacing between them, aligning left and stretching to nearly the full interior width of the card, while leaving 8–12px on the left and right as breathing room. Any primary filter action button sits at the bottom of the form, full-width within the module, with extra top padding (16–24px) to clearly separate it from the last input. The overall height of the filter form is compact relative to the column, but the internal spacing follows the generous, modern feel, keeping it visually balanced against the denser left-column event list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls naturally with the page as part of the sidebar column; it remains fixed in relation to the top of the sidebar content but does not stick to the viewport on scroll by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted events component is the first and most prominent element in the left main column, sitting just below the white title band with a vertical gap of about 32px. It spans the entire width of the left column (about 65–70% of the main content width), mirroring the large artwork area seen in the mockup. Visually, it behaves like a hero block for key shows: either a single large featured tour date or a tight cluster of 2–3 key events laid out horizontally or stacked with strong typography and possibly imagery. The component has generous internal padding (24–32px) and a slightly elevated visual treatment (e.g., a lighter dark panel or subtle border) to distinguish it from the background. The primary highlight, such as the next major city or tour leg, sits near the top-left of this block, anchoring the user’s eye with bold date and city text. Secondary details (venue, country, CTA link) are arranged below or to the right, maintaining a clear hierarchy. Below this block, there is a consistent vertical gap of 24px before the sorting controls, ensuring that the highlighted events feel distinct and important before transitioning into the more utilitarian list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; it remains at the top of the left column and scrolls naturally off-screen as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sorting controls are positioned directly beneath the highlighted events in the left column, separated by around 24px of vertical space. They span the full width of the left column but are visually lighter and more compact than the highlighted events block. The controls form a horizontal strip, about one to two lines tall, aligning with the left content edge. Common elements include a left-aligned label (e.g., 'Sort by') followed by a dropdown or segmented control, with any secondary options (like toggles for upcoming vs. past dates) aligned to the right edge of this strip. Internal vertical padding is tighter (around 8–12px), matching the more functional density of navigation-like elements in the mockup. A subtle bottom border or fine divider line may run across the width of the left column just below the controls, visually separating them from the event list that follows. This helps the sorting bar read as a persistent, lightweight control area. The vertical gap between the bottom of the sorting controls and the first event row in the list is about 16px, creating a clear yet compact transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored within the left column just above the events list. It scrolls with the content but may optionally be configured to stick to the top edge of the left column once the user scrolls past its initial position, remaining visible while the list scrolls underneath, as long as this does not conflict with the page’s minimalist aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The events list sits in the left main column directly beneath the sorting controls, separated by about 16px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the left column, forming a vertical stack of individual event rows. Each event row is a horizontally oriented tile with consistent internal padding (16–24px) and a clear separation from the background via either a subtle divider line or 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. Key information such as date, city, and venue is aligned to the left, using bold or slightly larger text for the date/city, while secondary details like country and ticket status are aligned either below or to the right, following the grid within the row. A 'Tickets' or 'Details' link/button appears consistently, typically aligned to the right side of each row. The list can extend downwards indefinitely, maintaining the same rhythm. To preserve the moderately dense layout, there is limited extra padding around the outer edges of the list area (16–24px from the left/right column edges), so the focus stays on the content. The bottom of the list has a slightly larger margin (24–32px) to separate it from any page footer or subsequent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main scrolling area of the left column; it expands vertically as more events are loaded. It remains within the grid defined by the page controller and scrolls normally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears in the exact space where the 'tour-events-list' would usually render, within the left main column, immediately below the sorting controls. When active, it replaces the list entirely. It spans the full width of the left column but uses a single, well-centered block of content rather than multiple rows. This block has generous padding (24–32px) and is aligned slightly above the vertical center of the visible area of the left column to keep it quickly noticeable. A concise headline like 'No tour dates match your filters' is left-aligned or center-aligned within the column, with a short explanatory line beneath it and possibly a subtle, line-art style icon that matches the band’s gritty aesthetic. Additional guidance, such as a 'Clear filters' action, is placed just below the message with 16px spacing, maintaining the clean, minimalist feel. The empty state preserves the same left/right margins as the event list, so the overall grid and alignment remain consistent even when no events are visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in place of the events list; it occupies the same structural slot in the layout. It scrolls with the page like any other content, but typically the message will appear near the top of the left column due to the short content height.

name: Event / Show Detail
title: Event Details
description: Displays details for a single event and lets users select ticket types, set quantities, and add tickets to their cart or proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load full event details (name, tour name, date/time, venue, city, country, status, description, ticket sale status) using getEventDetail based on the event ID in the URL.
Load and display all TicketType records for the event using getEventDetail, including price, currency, availability, and descriptions.
Allow users to change ticket sorting (e.g., by lowest price first) by updating the ticketSort URL parameter and reloading ticketTypes via getEventDetail.
Allow users to select a single TicketType, choose a quantity, and submit this selection to addTicketsToCart.
After a successful addTicketsToCart call, provide clear options to either continue to checkout (checkout.html) or view the cart (cart.html).
Provide navigation back to the tour dates list (tour.html), preserving the ability to return from a specific event.
Handle edge cases such as events not found, tickets not on sale, unavailable ticket types, or failed cart additions with API-driven messaging.
user_workflows:
View event details and browse tickets:
1. User opens event.html?id={eventId} (optionally with ticketSort={rule}).
2. Page reads id and ticketSort from the URL.
3. Page calls getEventDetail(eventId=id, ticketSort=ticketSort or default).
4. Event data is displayed: event name, tour name (if provided), venue, city, country, event date/time, status, and description.
5. Ticket types returned by getEventDetail are shown in a list, ordered according to the ticketSort parameter or a default ordering from the API.

Change ticket sorting:
1. User opens the ticket sort control (e.g., dropdown) above the ticket list.
2. User selects a sort rule (for example, a rule that corresponds to ordering by price ascending).
3. The ticketSort URL parameter is updated (e.g., event.html?id={eventId}&ticketSort={selectedRule}).
4. The page reloads or re-initializes the data loader component.
5. getEventDetail is called again with the new ticketSort value, and the ticket list is re-rendered in the new order.

Select a ticket type and quantity:
1. User reviews the ticketTypes list.
2. User selects one TicketType (e.g., via a radio button bound to the ticket type ID).
3. User adjusts the quantity control (minimum 1, maximum constrained by any remainingQuantity value returned by getEventDetail if provided).
4. The currently selected ticketTypeId and quantity are stored in page state (not in the URL).

Add tickets to cart and stay on the event page:
1. With a ticketType selected and a valid quantity set, user clicks an 'Add to cart' style button.
2. The page calls addTicketsToCart(ticketTypeId={selectedTicketTypeId}, quantity={selectedQuantity}).
3. If addTicketsToCart returns success=true, a success message is shown using the message from the API, and the user is shown a 'View Cart' link (cart.html) and a 'Continue to checkout' button (checkout.html).
4. If addTicketsToCart returns success=false, an error message is displayed based on the message returned from the API, and no navigation occurs.

Add tickets and immediately proceed to checkout:
1. With a ticketType selected and quantity set, user clicks a primary 'Continue to checkout' button.
2. The page calls addTicketsToCart(ticketTypeId={selectedTicketTypeId}, quantity={selectedQuantity}).
3. If the API response has success=true, the page navigates to checkout.html.
4. If success=false, the page does not navigate, and the error message is displayed; user can adjust selection or quantity and retry.

Navigate back to tour dates:
1. User clicks the 'Back to tour dates' link.
2. The page navigates to tour.html without additional parameters (or with parameters defined elsewhere in the application, but not required by this page).

View cart after adding tickets:
1. After a successful addTicketsToCart call, user clicks a 'View Cart' link.
2. The page navigates to cart.html where the user can review all cart items including the newly added ticket CartItem.
interactions: On page load, read id and ticketSort from the URL and trigger getEventDetail.
On ticket sort select change, update the ticketSort URL parameter and reload data via getEventDetail.
On ticket type row selection (e.g., clicking a radio button), update the selectedTicketTypeId in page state.

On quantity input change (e.g., plus/minus buttons or numeric input), validate and update the selectedQuantity in page state; enforce quantity >= 1 and, when available, quantity <= TicketType.remainingQuantity.
On 'Add to cart' button click, validate that a ticketTypeId and quantity are selected; if valid, call addTicketsToCart and show feedback from the response.
On primary 'Continue to checkout' button click, validate selection and quantity, call addTicketsToCart, and if success=true navigate to checkout.html.
On 'View Cart' link click, navigate to cart.html.
On 'Back to tour dates' link click, navigate to tour.html.
On getEventDetail error or when no event is returned, display an error state indicating that the event cannot be loaded, and keep navigation links available so the user can go back to tour.html.

When event.status indicates cancellation or postponement or ticketsOnSale is false, disable ticket selection and purchase CTAs while still displaying event details and explanatory text derived from the event data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses. The 'id' URL parameter is required and is used as eventId when calling getEventDetail. If 'id' is missing, the page does not call getEventDetail and instead shows an error state with a link back to tour.html. The 'ticketSort' URL parameter is optional and is passed directly to getEventDetail as ticketSort; if 'ticketSort' is not present, the page either omits the parameter or passes a default rule determined at runtime (but not hardcoded with event data). When the user updates the sort option in the UI, the ticketSort parameter in the URL is updated (e.g., using event.html?id={eventId}&ticketSort={newRule}) and the page reloads or re-fetches data so ticketTypes are returned in the new order. Selected ticketTypeId and quantity are kept in in-memory page state only (not reflected in the URL and not stored using localStorage). The outcome of addTicketsToCart (success, cartItemId, message) is stored in page state and used to control feedback messages and whether to show post-add CTAs like 'View Cart' and 'Continue to checkout'. Navigation to checkout.html, cart.html, or tour.html is performed via standard link navigation without additional parameters defined by this page.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the 'id' and 'ticketSort' URL parameters, calling getEventDetail with these values, and distributing the resulting event and ticketTypes data to dependent components. Handles loading, success, and error states.
event_handlers: onInit: Read URL parameters 'id' and 'ticketSort'; if 'id' is present, invoke getEventDetail({ eventId: id, ticketSort: ticketSort }); if 'id' is missing, set a 'missingEventId' error state.

onTicketSortChange: When notified by the ticket-sort-control that ticketSort has changed, update the URL to include the new ticketSort value and trigger a new getEventDetail call with the same eventId and new ticketSort.
onGetEventDetailSuccess: Store event and ticketTypes in shared page state; clear any previous error states.
onGetEventDetailError: Store an error state and make event and ticket-specific UI components render in an error mode with no purchase actions enabled.

components.functionality: Displays key information about the event returned from getEventDetail, including event name, optional tourName, date/time, venue, city, country, event status, and a textual description. Indicates whether tickets are on sale.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: When Event data from event-data-loader changes, re-render the summary information including status and ticketsOnSale flags.

components.functionality: Shows a contextual notice about the event status (scheduled, cancelled, or postponed) and whether ticketsOnSale is true. If the event is not scheduled or ticketsOnSale is false, this component informs other components to disable ticket selection and purchase actions.
event_handlers: onEventDataChange: Interpret event.status and event.ticketsOnSale values to compute a derived 'canPurchaseTickets' boolean flag exposed to other components.
onPurchaseActionRequested: If canPurchaseTickets is false, prevent downstream components from invoking addTicketsToCart and instead surface an explanatory message.

components.functionality: Provides a selection control for sorting ticketTypes, bound to the 'ticketSort' URL parameter. When the user changes the selection, it updates the URL and triggers reloading of ticketTypes via getEventDetail.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the current 'ticketSort' value from the URL and initialize the selected sort option in the UI.

onChangeSortRule: When the user selects a different sort rule, construct a new URL including the current 'id' and the chosen ticketSort value; update the browser URL (e.g., via navigation or history API) and notify event-data-loader to call getEventDetail with the new ticketSort.

components.functionality: Displays all ticketTypes for the event in the current sort order. Each ticket row shows ticket name, description, price with currency, availability indicators (using remainingQuantity and isAvailable if provided), and a selection control to choose that ticket type as the active selection.
event_handlers: onTicketTypesChange: When ticketTypes from event-data-loader change, re-render the list according to the order provided by the API.
onSelectTicketType: When the user selects a ticket row (e.g., radio button), set selectedTicketTypeId in shared page state and notify quantity-selector and ticket-purchase-cta components.
