onUnavailableTicketClick: If a ticketType has isAvailable=false or remainingQuantity equals 0, disable its selection control and, on user interaction, show an availability message instead of updating the selection.

components.functionality: Manages the numeric quantity of tickets for the currently selected TicketType. Ensures values are valid and compatible with any availability constraints from the selected TicketType.
event_handlers: onSelectedTicketTypeChange: Reset quantity to a default valid value (for example, 1) whenever the selectedTicketTypeId changes.
onIncreaseQuantity: Increase quantity by 1 while ensuring the result does not exceed the selected TicketType.remainingQuantity, if that value is provided.
onDecreaseQuantity: Decrease quantity by 1 but not below 1.

onManualQuantityInput: Validate user-entered quantity, enforce numeric constraints, and clamp it within allowed bounds; if invalid, display an inline validation error and do not update the confirmed quantity in shared state.

components.functionality: Provides primary actions to add the selected TicketType and quantity to the cart, and optionally proceed directly to checkout after adding. Coordinates with availability rules and event status before invoking addTicketsToCart.
event_handlers: onInit: Subscribe to selectedTicketTypeId and selectedQuantity from shared page state so that button enable/disable states reflect whether a valid selection exists.

onClickAddToCart: If event-status-notice indicates tickets can be purchased and a valid selectedTicketTypeId and quantity exist, call addTicketsToCart({ ticketTypeId: selectedTicketTypeId, quantity: selectedQuantity }); on success, store cartItemId and message in page state and notify cart-feedback-banner; on failure, store the error message and show it via cart-feedback-banner.

onClickContinueToCheckout: Perform the same validation and addTicketsToCart call as onClickAddToCart; if the response has success=true, immediately navigate to checkout.html; if success=false, do not navigate and instead surface the error via cart-feedback-banner.

onSelectionOrQuantityInvalid: If the user clicks an action while no ticket type is selected or quantity is invalid, prevent the API call and show validation feedback (e.g., a message indicating a ticket type and valid quantity are required).

components.functionality: Shows success or error feedback after addTicketsToCart calls. On success, informs the user that tickets have been added and exposes navigation options to view the cart or proceed to checkout.
event_handlers: onAddTicketsToCartResponse: When ticket-purchase-cta receives the addTicketsToCart response, display the message field from the API; if success=true, render a success state, otherwise render an error state.
onClickViewCart: When the user interacts with a 'View Cart' link inside the banner, navigate to cart.html.
onClickProceedToCheckout: When the user interacts with a 'Continue to checkout' button inside the banner, navigate to checkout.html.

components.functionality: Provides navigation links for returning to the tour dates list and, when applicable, viewing the cart independently of the feedback banner.

Displays error messages when getEventDetail fails, when 'id' is missing from the URL, or when the event cannot be used for ticket purchase (for example, no ticketTypes returned). Encourages navigation back to tour dates.
event_handlers: onMissingEventId: When event-data-loader detects that the 'id' URL parameter is absent, show an error message and the 'Back to tour dates' link from event-navigation-links.
onGetEventDetailError: Show a generic load error message derived from the failure condition, and disable ticket-selection-related components.

onNoTicketTypesAvailable: If getEventDetail returns an Event but ticketTypes is empty or all ticketTypes are unavailable, display a message that no tickets can be purchased, while still showing event details.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width site header (logo + navigation, assumed global), then a full-width white page-title band that contains the event header. Below this band sits a centered main content container on a dark, textured background. Inside that container, content is split into a two-column layout: a wide left column (about 65–70% of the content width) for the event status, ticket sorting, and the ticket selection list, and a narrower right sidebar (about 30–35%) for transactional and utility modules such as the ticket purchase CTA and event navigation links. Global or cross-cutting states (loader, cart feedback, error state) sit above or overlay this two-column area rather than being embedded inside a single column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width site header (logo + navigation, assumed global), then a full-width white page-title band that contains the event header. Below this band sits a centered main content container on a dark, textured background. Inside that container, content is split into a two-column layout: a wide left column (about 65–70% of the content width) for the event status, ticket sorting, and the ticket selection list, and a narrower right sidebar (about 30–35%) for transactional and utility modules such as the ticket purchase CTA and event navigation links. Global or cross-cutting states (loader, cart feedback, error state) sit above or overlay this two-column area rather than being embedded inside a single column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertical space mirrors the mockup’s generous rhythm. The white title band is tall, using roughly 15–20% of the viewport height with ample padding around the event title. The main content container below has a top margin of about 32–48px from the title band. Inside the container, the left and right columns align to a shared top baseline. Major blocks (status notice, ticket list, CTA module, event navigation module) are separated by 24–32px vertically. Internal component padding uses 16–24px, with compact controls (sort dropdown, quantity selector) using 8–16px padding. The overall content width is constrained to a fixed, centered container (e.g., ~1100–1200px) to preserve the band’s tight, grid-based feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and white title band remain visually airy with minimal elements. Below, the main content area is denser but still ordered: the left column’s ticket list forms the primary content stack, while the right sidebar’s modules are compact and clearly separated. Tickets are listed in vertically stacked rows with enough spacing (16–24px gaps) to avoid clutter. Sidebar modules use clear headings and boxed sections with breathing room. Controls like sort and quantity selectors are visually compact but not cramped, maintaining the dark, minimalist style without overwhelming the user.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a clear F-pattern. Users first scan the global header (logo on the left, navigation on the right), then drop to the white title band where the event name dominates. From there, the eye moves down into the main content: it hits any cart feedback banner first (full-width in the container), then flows to the upper-left of the left column where the event status and ticket sort control sit, and then down through the ticket list. The right sidebar draws secondary attention, with the ticket purchase CTA module at the top of the sidebar as a strong focal point; event navigation links sit below it as a tertiary focus. Error and loading states either replace the main content center or overlay it, ensuring that critical system feedback is unmissable without disrupting the overall F-pattern when things are working normally.

layout.overall_description: The event detail page sits on a dark, subtly textured background that stretches full-width across the browser, echoing a gritty, underground music venue wall. At the very top sits the site-wide black header with the band’s hand-drawn logo on the left and navigation links aligned to the right, all in a tight, shallow bar. Immediately beneath this, the page opens up into a tall, full-width white title band that slices across the dark backdrop. Centered within a fixed-width grid, the event title and key summary sit aligned to the left with generous padding above and below, leaving plenty of white space that spotlights the event name. Below the white band, the background returns to dark and the main content snaps into a centered two-column container. A slim cart feedback banner, when present, spans the full width of this container at the top, just under the title band. Beneath it, the left column—occupying roughly two-thirds of the content width—starts with a compact event status notice, followed by a subtle ticket sorting row and then a vertically stacked list of ticket types stretching down the page. Each ticket row is a horizontal bar: details on the left, a quantity selector block on the right, all aligned to the grid and spaced for clarity. The right column forms a sidebar about one-third the width: at its top is a boxed, high-contrast ticket purchase CTA module summarizing selected tickets and offering a prominent button, remaining visible as the user scrolls. Below that, secondary modules such as event navigation links are stacked with clear labels and spacing. If loading or errors occur, they visually dominate the central content area, either overlaying or replacing the two-column layout. The result feels like a polished yet raw gig poster translated into a structured, modern web layout: bold at the top, functional and transactional in the main body, always anchored by a strong grid and hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event data loader appears above the main two-column content area, but below the global header and white title band. It covers the entire fixed-width main content container—from the left edge of the left column to the right edge of the sidebar—using a semi-transparent dark scrim that subtly mutes the underlying layout. Vertically, it spans from just beneath the bottom of the white title band down to at least the initially visible portion of the ticket list, effectively blocking interaction with tickets and sidebar CTAs while data loads. At the center of this overlay (horizontally centered in the container, slightly above the vertical midpoint of the visible content), a loading indicator and short message appear, sized to occupy roughly 25–30% of the container width. The rest of the page (header and title band) remains visible above, reinforcing context while clearly communicating a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute-positioned overlay anchored to the main content container, covering both columns while visible; non-scrolling relative to the container, so it stays in place until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event header occupies the core of the full-width white title band, which stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport just below the global site header. Inside this band, the content is constrained to the centered fixed-width grid. The event title is aligned flush to the left within this container, starting roughly 24–32px below the top of the white band and extending no more than about 70% of the container width, allowing generous white space to its right. Below the main title, secondary details like date, time, and venue appear in smaller, subdued text in a tight vertical stack, maintaining a left alignment with an 8–16px vertical gap between lines. The right side of the white band remains mostly empty or subtly textured, reinforcing the minimalist, high-contrast look and making the event name the dominant visual element before users scroll into the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, forming a full-width horizontal band between the global header and the dark main content background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event status notice sits at the very top of the left column within the main content container, just below the white title band (or cart feedback banner if present). It is aligned to the left edge of the left column, with a top margin of approximately 32px from the bottom of the title band (or 16–24px from the feedback banner). The notice appears as a compact horizontal strip—about 50–60% of the left column’s width—featuring a high-contrast label such as "ON SALE", "SOLD OUT", or a date-based message. It sits inside a slightly raised or bordered capsule with 8–12px of internal padding on all sides, hugging the content but leaving breathing room. There is at least 16–24px of vertical space between this notice and the ticket sort control below, giving it its own clear visual moment without overwhelming the rest of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the left column; scrolls naturally with the main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket sort control is positioned directly beneath the event status notice in the left column, aligned flush with the left column’s content edge. It occupies a single horizontal row across roughly 70–80% of the left column width, with a top margin of about 16–24px from the status notice. On the left, a small label (e.g., "Sort tickets") is aligned to the baseline of the control, while to the right of that label—separated by 8–16px—a dropdown or segmented control allows the user to choose the sort order. The control itself is compact: about 25–30% of the column width, with 8–12px internal padding and a subtle border that matches the dark, edgy aesthetic. There is a clear 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of this sort row and the top of the ticket-selection list, visually connecting it to the list but keeping the interface uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element within the left column’s flow; it scrolls away as the user moves down the ticket list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket selection list dominates the left column, beginning directly below the ticket sort control with a generous 24–32px gap. It spans the full width of the left column from left to right, creating a vertical stack of ticket rows that extend down the page. Each ticket row is a low-profile horizontal card with a dark background and subtle edging, aligned to the column grid. Within each row, the left portion (approximately 65–70% of the row’s width) is dedicated to ticket information: ticket name, description, and price are arranged in a vertical micro-stack with tight 4–8px spacing. The right portion (about 30–35% of the row’s width) houses the quantity selector area, right-aligned and vertically centered relative to the ticket text. Vertical spacing between rows is around 16–24px to keep the list readable and scannable without feeling crowded. The list maintains consistent left and right padding (16–24px) inside the column boundaries, aligning visually with the sidebar modules on the right. As users scroll, this list forms the main vertical reading path of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block flowing down the left column; its height grows with the number of ticket types and it scrolls behind any sticky sidebar elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity selector lives inside each ticket row, occupying the rightmost portion of that row. It is horizontally oriented, typically comprising a minus button, a numeric value, and a plus button, or a compact dropdown. This control block is about 60–70% of the width of the ticket row’s right-side segment and is right-aligned so its outer edge lines up vertically across all rows. It has 8–12px of internal padding and a subtle border or background treatment that differentiates it from the darker card behind, while still fitting the minimalist, grunge-inspired palette. Vertically, it is centered in the ticket row so that it aligns with the main ticket title text, creating a clean horizontal axis across the list. There is a small 8–12px gap between this control and any per-ticket labels or help text, ensuring the interaction target remains clear and unfussy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each ticket row, maintaining its relative position as the row resizes; scrolls naturally with the ticket list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The ticket purchase CTA module anchors the top of the right sidebar and is the most visually assertive element in that column. It sits aligned to the right edge of the main container, starting at the same vertical baseline as the top of the left column’s event status notice. The module takes up the full width of the sidebar, with a card-like appearance: a slightly lighter dark background, clear border, and generous 24px internal padding. Inside, a summary of selected tickets and subtotal appears at the top, using a compact vertical hierarchy, while the primary action button (e.g., "Add to cart" or "Proceed to checkout") spans nearly the full width of the module below, with 16–24px margin above it to set it apart. The module’s top edge is about 32px below the bottom of the white title band (or aligned with any cart feedback banner), and there is a 24–32px gap between this CTA card and the next sidebar module below. This placement makes the CTA highly visible in peripheral vision while users scan the ticket list on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky sidebar module on larger screens: once the user scrolls past its initial position, it remains fixed within the viewport’s right side, bounded vertically so it doesn’t overlap the footer; on smaller screens it reverts to a normal static block flowing beneath the ticket list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart feedback banner sits at the very top of the main content container, directly below the white title band. It spans the full width of the centered container, bridging across both the left content area and the right sidebar. This banner is relatively low in height—roughly 48–64px—with a high-contrast background color (e.g., a muted yet vivid accent) that stands out against the dark main background. The message text is aligned to the left within the container, starting about 16–24px from the left edge, while any optional icon or close button is placed at the far right, aligned to the same vertical center. There is a 16–24px vertical gap between the bottom of this banner and the top of the left-column event status notice and the right-column CTA module, so that it reads as a distinct, transient notification rather than a permanent part of either column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow, appearing and disappearing above both columns; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event navigation links form a compact, utility-style module in the right sidebar, positioned directly beneath the ticket-purchase CTA module. It aligns to the same left and right edges as the CTA, maintaining the sidebar’s column rhythm. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the CTA module and the top of this navigation block. Inside, a small heading such as "MORE SHOWS" appears at the top left, with 8–12px of space below it, followed by a vertical list of navigation links (e.g., "Back to all events", "Previous show", "Next show"). Each link is on its own line with 8–12px of vertical spacing, creating a relaxed, scan-friendly stack. The entire module is relatively low-profile compared to the CTA, using lighter typography and no heavy background box, or a very subtle card with minimal padding (16–20px) to keep it clearly secondary. It stays within the sidebar column height; when the CTA becomes sticky, this module will scroll underneath it as the user moves down the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static module in the right sidebar that scrolls normally; on larger screens it appears below the sticky CTA and moves out of view as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The event error state replaces the normal content experience within the main content container when an error occurs. In this state, instead of the two-column layout, a single, centered error block appears within the same fixed-width container, aligned beneath the white title band. This block is horizontally centered and occupies roughly 50–60% of the container width, leaving wide margins of dark background on either side. It is placed with a generous top margin (48–64px) from the bottom of the white title band to create a calm, intentional pause. The error icon or symbol appears at the top of the block, centered, followed by a short, bold error message and a more detailed explanation in smaller text beneath, all stacked with 8–16px spacing. A secondary action (e.g., "Back to events" link) is placed below, centered but clearly actionable. The left ticket column, right sidebar modules, and interactive controls are either hidden or visually suppressed behind this state so that the user’s focus is drawn entirely to resolving or understanding the error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the two-column content within the main container when active, occupying the central area; scroll behavior is normal, but content is typically short enough to remain in the upper viewport without needing to scroll.

name: Blog / News Index
title: Blog & News
description: Lists blog and news articles, supports filtering by tag, year, and category via URL parameters, and lets users save articles to a reading list.

core_features: Load and display a list of articles using the listArticles interface, with tag, year, category, and sort parameters taken from the current URL.
Provide filter controls for tag, year, category, and sort order that update the URL parameters and refresh the article list.
Allow users to open full article detail pages by clicking article titles or a dedicated "Read more" interaction, navigating to article.html?id={articleId}.
Enable users to save articles to a reading list using the saveArticleToReadingList interface from each article item.
Provide navigation to the reading_list.html page (Reading List / Saved articles) from the blog page.
user_workflows: Browsing latest posts: 1) User navigates to blog.html (with or without URL parameters). 2) On page load, the page reads tag, year, category, and sort from the URL. 3) The page calls listArticles with these values. 4) The returned Article list is rendered as article items, each showing title, date, excerpt, and tags. 5) User clicks a post title or "Read more" to open article.html?id={articleId}.

Filtering by tag and year: 1) User adjusts the tag input (e.g., to a specific tour diary tag) and/or year input in the filter bar. 2) On change or submit, the filter bar updates the URL query string (blog.html?tag={value}&year={value}&category={value}&sort={value}) while preserving unchanged parameters. 3) The page detects the URL change and re-calls listArticles with the updated parameters. 4) The article list updates to show only articles matching the selected filters. 5) Optionally, user clicks on a tag displayed on an article; this sets the tag URL parameter to that tag value and triggers the same reload and filter behavior.

Filtering by category and sort: 1) User chooses a category value and/or sort option in the filter bar. 2) The filter bar writes the chosen values into the category and sort URL parameters (adding or updating them). 3) The page re-runs listArticles with the new category and sort parameters. 4) The article list re-renders in the new order and with the selected category filter applied.

Saving an article to the reading list: 1) User finds an interesting article in the list. 2) User clicks the article’s Save/Bookmark control. 3) The bookmark component calls saveArticleToReadingList with the article’s id and an optional note (empty or user-entered). 4) On success, the component receives readingListItemId and readingListId and updates its local state to reflect that the article is saved (e.g., disabling the control or switching to a saved state). 5) If the call fails, the component shows an error state and allows the user to retry.

Going to the Reading List page: 1) User clicks the "Reading List" or "Saved articles" navigation link in the blog sidebar or footer. 2) The browser navigates to reading_list.html without additional parameters. 3) The reading list page (implemented separately) uses its own interfaces to load saved items.
interactions: Filter controls: Interacting with the tag, year, category, or sort controls updates the respective URL parameters and triggers a fresh call to listArticles; the article list updates accordingly.
Article title click: Clicking an article title navigates to article.html?id={articleId}.
"Read more" click: Clicking a dedicated "Read more" interaction on an article navigates to article.html?id={articleId}.

Tag chip click on an article: Clicking a tag value on any article sets the tag URL parameter to that tag and reloads the list using listArticles, preserving the current year, category, and sort parameters unless explicitly reset.

Bookmark/Save click: Clicking the Save/Bookmark control on an article triggers saveArticleToReadingList with the article’s id and optional note; on success, the control switches to a saved state for that article within the current page session.
Reading List navigation click: Clicking the Reading List / Saved articles link navigates to reading_list.html.

URL parameter parsing: On initial page load and whenever the URL changes (e.g., via navigation or browser back/forward), the page parses tag, year, category, and sort from the URL and re-runs listArticles with those values.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully driven by URL query parameters and the results of the listArticles and saveArticleToReadingList interfaces. On load, the page reads the following parameters from the URL's query string: tag, year, category, sort. Any parameter that is absent or empty is treated as unset for the purposes of calling listArticles and is passed as undefined or omitted. The listArticles interface is called with these four parameters: listArticles(tag, year, category, sort). The returned Article array defines the current content state of the article list.

User actions that change filters or sorting do not mutate in-memory state directly; instead, they update the URL query string. Changing a filter (tag/year/category) or sort option causes the browser to navigate to blog.html with a new query string reflecting the desired state. For example, if a user sets tag and year but leaves category unchanged, the resulting URL might be blog.html?tag={tagValue}&year={yearValue}&category={currentCategory}&sort={currentSort}. On each such navigation (including browser back/forward), the page re-parses the URL parameters and calls listArticles again, ensuring the visible articles always match the URL.

Clicking a tag on an article sets only the tag parameter to the tag value from that article, while preserving other parameters (year, category, sort) unless the implementation chooses to reset them. For example, if the current URL has year and sort set, clicking a tag updates tag while keeping year and sort unchanged.

Saving an article via saveArticleToReadingList is not encoded in the URL; it is a side-effect that returns readingListItemId and readingListId. The bookmark component maintains transient in-memory state for which items have been saved during the current page session, used solely to prevent duplicate saves and to alter the control state (e.g., disabling the button) after a successful save. Because there is no interface to list current reading list items on this page, previously saved items from prior sessions are not reflected in the initial UI state; only actions taken during the current visit are tracked. All permanent state about saved articles is held server-side in the ReadingList and ReadingListItem models, via saveArticleToReadingList.

Navigation to article.html and reading_list.html uses URL parameters as follows: article.html?id={articleId} for article detail pages, and reading_list.html (without additional query parameters) for the reading list overview. No localStorage or equivalent client-side persistence is used for this page’s core logic; all durable state flows either through URL parameters or through the two assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Manages the article filter and sorting controls at the top of the page. On initialization, it reads tag, year, category, and sort from the current URL and populates the corresponding input controls (e.g., text fields, selects). When the user changes any control and submits (or when controls auto-apply), it constructs a new URL query string reflecting the chosen tag, year, category, and sort values, preserving any parameters the user has not changed, and navigates the browser to blog.html with these updated parameters. This triggers the article-list component to reload articles via listArticles.
event_handlers: onLoad: Read tag, year, category, and sort from URL and initialize control values accordingly.
onTagChange: Update the internal tag value and, on apply/submit, navigate to blog.html with tag set to the new value and other parameters preserved.
onYearChange: Update the internal year value and navigate to blog.html with year updated and other parameters preserved.
onCategoryChange: Update the internal category value and navigate to blog.html with category updated and other parameters preserved.
onSortChange: Update the internal sort value and navigate to blog.html with sort updated and other parameters preserved.
onResetFilters: Clear selected filter values (for example, tag and year, or all filters) and navigate to blog.html with only the remaining or default parameters.

components.functionality: Central component responsible for fetching and rendering the list of articles. On page load and whenever the URL query parameters change, it extracts tag, year, category, and sort from the URL, calls listArticles with these parameters, and renders each returned Article as an article-list-item. It handles loading and error states, and passes relevant props (such as whether an article was just saved) down to child components.
event_handlers: onLoad: Parse tag, year, category, and sort from URL; invoke listArticles(tag, year, category, sort); handle and display results.
onUrlParamsChange: When the browser URL changes (via navigation or filter-bar actions), re-parse parameters and re-invoke listArticles to refresh the article list.
onFetchSuccess: Receive the array of Article objects from listArticles and render them as article-list-item components.
onFetchError: Display an error state and optionally provide a manual retry that re-invokes listArticles with the current URL parameters.

components.functionality: Represents a single article within the list. It displays the article title, published date, excerpt, and tag list using the Article data. The title and a dedicated "Read more" interaction both navigate to the article detail page. Each tag, when clicked, behaves as a filter shortcut: it updates the tag URL parameter to that tag value and reloads the article list. It also contains the article-bookmark-control subcomponent for saving the article to the reading list.
event_handlers: onTitleClick: Navigate the browser to article.html?id={article.id}.
onReadMoreClick: Navigate the browser to article.html?id={article.id}.

onTagClick: For the clicked tag value, construct a new URL that sets tag to that value while preserving year, category, and sort (unless intentionally reset); navigate to blog.html with these parameters so article-list-container reloads via listArticles.
onBookmarkClick: Delegate to the article-bookmark-control component to trigger saveArticleToReadingList for this article.

components.functionality: Provides a Save/Bookmark action for an article. When activated, it calls saveArticleToReadingList with the current article's id and an optional note. On success, it stores the returned readingListItemId and readingListId in its own transient state and updates its appearance and behavior (e.g., indicating that the article is saved and preventing repeated saves during the same session). On failure, it exposes an error state and allows retrying the save operation.
event_handlers: onClickSave: Triggered when the user clicks the Save/Bookmark control; calls saveArticleToReadingList(articleId, note) where articleId is from the bound Article and note is either empty or collected from an optional note input.

onSaveSuccess: Receive {readingListItemId, readingListId} from saveArticleToReadingList; update component state to mark this article as saved and disable or alter the Save action to reflect this state.
onSaveError: Handle any error returned from saveArticleToReadingList; show an error indication and keep or re-enable the Save action for retry.
onOptionalNoteChange: If the UI offers a text field for notes, update the internally stored note value used in the next saveArticleToReadingList call.

components.functionality: Provides a direct navigation entry point from the blog page to the Reading List / Saved articles page. It does not call any interface; it simply navigates to reading_list.html when clicked.
event_handlers: onClick: Navigate the browser to reading_list.html.

components.functionality: Utility component (or logic module) responsible for reading and writing URL query parameters related to blog filtering and sorting. It exposes helper methods to get the current tag, year, category, and sort values from the URL and to create updated URLs when filters change. Other components (blog-filter-bar, article-list-container, article-list-item) rely on this component for consistent URL manipulation and state interpretation.
event_handlers: onReadUrl: Parse the current window.location.search string into tag, year, category, and sort values for consumers.
onWriteUrl: Given a partial set of new parameter values, merge them with existing parameters to build a new query string; update window.location to navigate to blog.html with these parameters.
onPopState: Listen to browser history changes (back/forward) and notify interested components (such as article-list-container) to re-read parameters and refresh data by calling listArticles.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom stack: a full-width dark header with logo on the left and navigation (including the reading list link) on the right; directly beneath that, a full-width white (or very light) page-title band labeled something like “BLOG / NEWS” with generous vertical padding; below the title band sits a centered, fixed-width main content container on a 12-column grid. Inside this container, the layout splits into a two-column structure: a wider left column (about 65–70% of the content width) for the blog filter bar and the article list, and a narrower right column (about 30–35%) for sidebar widgets like search, recent posts, and categories. The filter bar is positioned at the top of the left column, immediately under the title band, with the article list stacked directly beneath it. URL state management is non-visual and overlays the entire page logic without occupying space.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom stack: a full-width dark header with logo on the left and navigation (including the reading list link) on the right; directly beneath that, a full-width white (or very light) page-title band labeled something like “BLOG / NEWS” with generous vertical padding; below the title band sits a centered, fixed-width main content container on a 12-column grid. Inside this container, the layout splits into a two-column structure: a wider left column (about 65–70% of the content width) for the blog filter bar and the article list, and a narrower right column (about 30–35%) for sidebar widgets like search, recent posts, and categories. The filter bar is positioned at the top of the left column, immediately under the title band, with the article list stacked directly beneath it. URL state management is non-visual and overlays the entire page logic without occupying space.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Vertically, the header is relatively short (roughly 64–80px tall) but spans the full browser width. The white title band below it is visually prominent, occupying around 15–20% of the initial viewport height with 48px–64px of vertical padding above and below the page title, creating a calm, spacious hero-like area. The main content container below occupies the remainder of the viewport, with a top margin of about 32px from the title band. Within this container, the left main column occupies roughly 65–70% of the width (e.g., 8 out of 12 grid columns), while the right sidebar takes the remaining 30–35% (4 out of 12 columns). A 24–32px vertical gap separates stacked elements (filter bar above article list, article cards from each other, sidebar modules from each other), and a 32px horizontal gutter separates the main content column from the sidebar. Inside components, padding is generally 16–24px, with tighter 8–12px padding for compact controls like nav links and the bookmark button.
layout_strategies.content_density: The header and the white title band are intentionally sparse and airy: few elements, large padding, and plenty of negative space that lets the edgy band aesthetic breathe. The main content area is more content-dense but still controlled: the left column holds a functional filter bar followed by stacked article cards that nearly fill the column width, while maintaining a clear 24–32px vertical rhythm between cards. Each article card feels substantial, with a dark panel, strong typography, and enough internal padding to keep the artwork, title, metadata, excerpt, and bookmark control from feeling cramped. The right sidebar is the most compact zone, with small text modules (search, recent posts, categories) stacked tightly yet separated by 16–24px vertical gaps and clear headings to avoid clutter. Overall, the density ramps up as you move downward into the content, but consistent spacing and a clear grid prevent visual noise.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s F-pattern. The eye enters at the top-left with the band’s logo, then sweeps right along the dark header navigation where the reading list link sits with other nav items. From there, attention drops straight down to the high-contrast white title band where “BLOG / NEWS” or a similar heading is centered or left-aligned with ample breathing room. Next, the eye falls into the main container, landing first on the blog filter bar at the top-left of the content area (the functional next step after understanding where you are). From the filter bar, the viewer scans down the left column through the article list, reading each card in sequence; at the same time, the sidebar modules on the right form the secondary vertical stroke of the F, inviting sideways glances for search, recent posts, and categories. Bookmark icons appear consistently in the top-right of each article card, becoming quick, recognizable targets without disrupting the left-to-right reading of titles and metadata. The URL state management is invisible but ensures the filter and reading list behavior feels cohesive and predictable as users navigate and share URLs.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a textured, nearly black background with a faint grunge feel. At the very top, a full-width dark header bar anchors the design: the band’s hand-drawn logo is pinned to the top-left, while a tight row of navigation links, including the reading list, aligns along the right, each with compact horizontal padding and subtle hover states. Directly beneath, a white (or very light) horizontal title band stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport, acting as a visual break from the dark chrome; in its center or left side, a bold, uppercase “BLOG / NEWS” heading floats with 48–64px of vertical padding above and below, leaving plenty of open space around it. Below this, the page snaps back to dark: a centered, fixed-width main container appears, constrained to something like 1100–1200px, and is divided into two columns. The left column, roughly two-thirds of the width, begins with a low-profile, horizontally oriented filter bar that lets users adjust tags, years, and categories; beneath that, a vertical stack of article cards runs down the page, each card filling the width of the column with a dark panel, strong typography, and a small bookmark control in the top-right. The right column, about one-third of the width, functions as a sidebar: at its top sits a search module, followed by a list of recent posts, then categories and any other widgets, each clearly separated by 16–24px vertical gaps and headings. A 32px gutter keeps the main column and sidebar comfortably apart. As you scroll, the article list is the dominant reading path on the left, while the sidebar acts as a tight vertical spine of utilities, all within the same edgy, minimalist, high-contrast aesthetic. The URL state layer tracks filter and bookmarking behavior behind the scenes, so the interface looks clean and focused while supporting rich interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog filter bar is positioned inside the main content container at the top of the left column, immediately below the white page-title band. It aligns with the left edge of the main article content, spanning nearly the full width of the left column (about 65–70% of the total content width) and leaving a consistent 32px horizontal gutter to the sidebar on its right. Vertically, there is a 32px gap between the bottom of the title band and the top of the filter bar, matching the mockup’s generous section separation. The filter bar has a low-profile, horizontal layout: filter controls (e.g., dropdowns or pill toggles for tag, year, category) are aligned from left to right in a single row, with 16px spacing between controls. A small “Clear filters” text link or icon sits on the far right side of the bar, right-aligned within the column. The bar itself sits on a slightly lighter dark panel than the page background, with 16–20px padding on all sides and subtly rounded corners, visually separating it from the surrounding content while keeping it understated. Below the filter bar, a 24–32px vertical gap opens before the first article card begins, preserving a clear hierarchy between controls and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the main left column; scrolls with the page. Width locked to the left content column and aligned to the same grid as article cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-list-container occupies the full vertical stack of the left main column beneath the filter bar. It is horizontally constrained to the left column’s 65–70% share of the centered main container, with its left edge aligned exactly to the shared text and image alignment line used by the filter bar and its right edge ending 32px before the sidebar column. Inside, articles are laid out as a clean vertical list of cards. The top of the first card starts approximately 24–32px below the bottom of the filter bar, and each subsequent card is separated by a consistent 24–32px vertical gap. The container itself does not have a visible border or panel; instead, the article cards define the visual rhythm, floating on the dark background but aligned to a strict grid. As the user scrolls, this container extends downward, providing the main reading flow. If pagination or infinite scroll is used, any load-more controls appear at the bottom of this container, centered along the same left column width.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-height list within the left column; scrolls naturally with the page. It serves as the primary vertical flow anchor for main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each article-list-item is presented as a distinct dark card within the article-list-container, emphasizing the band’s edgy aesthetic while maintaining readability. The card spans almost the full width of the left column, with small horizontal insets (e.g., 8–12px) from the column’s edges to create a subtle framing effect. It sits on a slightly elevated dark panel (e.g., a near-black with a faint textured or noise effect) and may have a thin, low-contrast border or soft outer glow to separate it from the background. Internal padding is generous—around 20–24px on all sides—to give breathing room to the content. The internal layout is vertically stacked with a left-to-right emphasis: at the top, the article title sits flush left, using large, bold typography; the bookmark control is placed in the top-right corner inside the card, forming a clear focal point for saving the article. Directly beneath the title, aligned to the left, a compact metadata row (date, category, tags) runs horizontally in a smaller, lighter type. Below that, a short excerpt or lead-in paragraph spans the full content width, ending with a “Read more” link aligned either bottom-right or immediately following the text. If a thumbnail or artwork is used, it appears either as a full-width image at the top of the card with 0 bottom margin followed by title and text, or as a left-aligned image with text wrapping to its right—either way, the image never breaks the card’s padding. Vertical spacing between elements inside the card follows the 8–16px scale (e.g., 8px between title and metadata, 12–16px between metadata and excerpt). The bottom of the card leaves a clear visual break before the next card begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card element within the article list; each card maintains consistent width and internal layout. The layout does not float or overlap; cards simply stack vertically.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-bookmark-control is a compact interactive icon (e.g., a stylized bookmark or heart) placed inside each article-list-item, anchored to the top-right corner of the card’s content area. It sits within the card’s padding, positioned approximately 8–12px from the top edge and 8–12px from the right edge of the content box, ensuring alignment across all cards. The control is visually subtle but clearly interactive: in its idle state, it might appear as a thin-lined icon in a muted light gray; when hovered, it brightens slightly or gains a soft glow; when active (saved), it fills with a solid accent color (such as a deep red or electric accent used elsewhere in the band’s branding). The control is small enough not to compete with the title but large enough to be easily targetable, around 24–32px in touch area. It visually floats above the content block of title and text, often aligning horizontally with the first line of the title, making it easy to recognize and quickly tap or click without disrupting the left-to-right reading of text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each card, anchored to the card’s top-right inside padding. It does not affect card sizing beyond its presence and scrolls with the article list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-nav-link resides in the main site header, positioned along the right side of the top navigation bar. Horizontally, it sits inline with the other nav items, typically as the last or near-last item in the sequence, creating a clear endpoint for the top horizontal scan. Vertically, it is centered within the header bar’s height (around 64–80px tall), sharing the same baseline, font size, and padding as the other navigation links. The link text might be labeled “Reading List” or similar, possibly accompanied by a small badge to the top-right of the label indicating the number of saved articles; this badge would be a small pill or circle, around 16–20px in height, adding a subtle focal point without overwhelming the minimalist header. The link sits on the dark header background, with 8–12px horizontal padding on either side, and a hover state that might include an underline or a slight color shift to a brighter accent. It visually balances the logo on the left, reinforcing the F-pattern’s initial top-horizontal stroke.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the header navigation; it moves with the page as the user scrolls, unless the header is globally configured to be sticky. It does not occupy a separate block-level area, simply participates in the inline nav layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-url-state-manager is a non-visual, structural component that does not render any interface elements or occupy any physical space on the page. Conceptually, it overlays the full blog index experience, connecting the filter bar, article list, bookmark controls, and reading list link. It listens to changes in the blog-filter-bar (e.g., selected tags, year, category) and updates the browser’s URL parameters accordingly; likewise, it reads those parameters on initial load or navigation to restore the current filter state and active page of results. It also tracks bookmarked articles, ensuring that when a user clicks the article-bookmark-control, the saved state is reflected both in the list and in any reading list badge on the reading-list-nav-link, and that appropriate URL or local state is updated so the reading list can be revisited. From a layout perspective, it is invisible and does not affect spacing, alignment, or the visual grid—it purely orchestrates behavior behind the scenes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and global; it has no direct spatial footprint and effectively overlays the entire page logic layer, synchronizing UI components with the URL and state.

name: Article Detail
title: Article Detail
description: Displays the full content of a single article, lets the user save it to their reading list, and provides navigation to the blog index, the reading list, and related posts.

core_features: Fetch and display a single article based on the URL parameter id by calling getArticleDetail with articleId derived from the URL.
Show full article content including title, content body, hero image, publish date, category, tags, and author using the Article data returned from getArticleDetail.
Indicate whether the article is already saved to the default reading list using the isSavedToReadingList flag returned by getArticleDetail.
Provide a control to save the article to the default reading list by calling saveArticleToReadingList with the current articleId and an optional note.
After saving, expose a direct navigation link to reading_list.html so the user can view/manage their saved articles (including unsaving).
Provide a persistent navigation control back to blog.html so the user can return to the blog index.
Encourage further reading by generating links to blog.html filtered by the current article’s tags or category (e.g., blog.html?tag=... or blog.html?category=...).
user_workflows:
1) Open an article from the blog index
   - User clicks an article entry on blog.html.
   - Browser navigates to article.html?id=<selected-article-id>.
   - Article Detail page reads the id parameter from the URL.
   - Page calls getArticleDetail with articleId equal to the URL id.
   - While loading, the page shows a loading state (no static content).
   - On success, the page displays the article header, metadata, and content.
   - The Save/Reading List control reflects isSavedToReadingList from the response.

2) Open an article from the reading list
   - User clicks an article entry on reading_list.html.
   - Browser navigates to article.html?id=<saved-article-id>.
   - Article Detail page reads the id parameter and calls getArticleDetail with that articleId.
   - Article content is displayed.
   - Because the article came from the reading list, isSavedToReadingList is expected to be true; the Save control shows that the article is already saved and offers navigation to reading_list.html for management.

3) Save an article to the reading list
   - User is viewing article.html?id=<article-id>.
   - getArticleDetail has completed and isSavedToReadingList is false.
   - User enters an optional note in the note input field of the Reading List control.
   - User clicks the Save button.
   - Page calls saveArticleToReadingList with:
     • articleId = current URL id
     • note = the text entered by the user (or an empty string if omitted)
   - While waiting for the response, the Save button shows a pending/disabled state to prevent duplicate submissions.
   - On success, the component receives readingListItemId and readingListId from the response.
   - The local saved state for this article is updated to reflect that it is now saved.
   - A confirmation message is shown along with a link to reading_list.html so the user can open the reading list.

4) Manage or unsave an article via the reading list
   - User is viewing article.html?id=<article-id> where isSavedToReadingList is true (either from getArticleDetail or after a successful saveArticleToReadingList call).
   - The Reading List control exposes a "View Reading List" / "Manage in Reading List" link.
   - User clicks this link.
   - Browser navigates to reading_list.html (optionally with a query parameter such as reading_list.html?articleId=<article-id> so that page can highlight or scroll to this article).
   - On reading_list.html, the user uses that page’s functionality to unsave/remove the article from the list.
   - If the user later returns to article.html?id=<article-id>, getArticleDetail will reflect the updated saved state via isSavedToReadingList.

5) Navigate back to the blog index
   - User is done reading an article on article.html.
   - User clicks the "Back to Blog" navigation control.
   - Browser navigates to blog.html without additional parameters, or retains any filters the blog page itself may embed in its own links.

6) Discover related posts (Tour Diary, news, etc.)
   - User is viewing article.html and wants more content.
   - The Related Reading component checks the current article’s tags and category from getArticleDetail.
   - For each tag, it renders a link to blog.html with a tag query parameter constructed dynamically from that tag value (for example, blog.html?tag=<tag-value>).
   - If the article has a category, it renders a link to blog.html?category=<category-value>.
   - User clicks one of these links.
   - Browser navigates to blog.html with the given filter parameter; that page is responsible for filtering and displaying relevant posts.
interactions: On initial page load or when the id URL parameter changes, the Article Loader component triggers getArticleDetail with articleId set to the id parameter value.
If getArticleDetail succeeds, the article data and isSavedToReadingList flag are propagated to dependent components (header, content, reading list control, related reading).
If getArticleDetail fails or returns no article for the given id, the page displays an error/not-found state and suppresses other components that depend on article content.
Clicking the "Back to Blog" navigation control triggers a client-side navigation to blog.html.
Typing in the note input within the Reading List control updates an internal note state that is later passed to saveArticleToReadingList.
Clicking the "Save to Reading List" button when the article is not yet saved triggers a call to saveArticleToReadingList with the current articleId and the note state.
While saveArticleToReadingList is in progress, the "Save" button is disabled and may show a loading state; additional clicks are ignored until the request completes.

If saveArticleToReadingList succeeds, the Reading List control updates its local saved state, reflects that the article is saved (e.g., button label and status), and shows a success message and "View Reading List" link.
If saveArticleToReadingList fails, an error message is shown and the save button returns to its normal state so the user can retry.
Clicking the "View Reading List" / "Manage in Reading List" link navigates to reading_list.html (optionally with an articleId parameter) where unsaving/removal can be performed.
Clicking a tag chip or link in the Related Reading section navigates to blog.html with a tag query parameter constructed from the clicked tag.
Clicking a category-based link in the Related Reading section navigates to blog.html with a category query parameter constructed from the article’s category.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state driver for this page is the id URL parameter. On every page load, the Article Detail page reads the id parameter from the query string of article.html. If id is present and non-empty, the page calls getArticleDetail with articleId = id. If id is missing, empty, or invalid, the page enters an error/not-found state and does not attempt to render article-specific components.

The article data returned by getArticleDetail (Article fields plus isSavedToReadingList) is stored in in-memory page/component state. When the browser’s back/forward navigation changes the id parameter (for example, if the user navigates between different article.html?id=... entries), the page detects the changed id and re-triggers getArticleDetail with the new articleId. No data is persisted via localStorage; every visit or navigation that changes id triggers a fresh API call.

The isSavedToReadingList value from getArticleDetail initializes the saved state for the Reading List control. When the user successfully calls saveArticleToReadingList, the page updates its local saved state to indicate that the article is saved without re-calling getArticleDetail. If the user navigates away (e.g., to reading_list.html to unsave the article) and later returns to article.html?id=<same-id>, the saved state will be re-synchronized by the new getArticleDetail response.

Additional derived navigation URLs are constructed dynamically based on the current article’s data: for example, links to blog.html?tag=<tag> or blog.html?category=<category>. These links do not alter state on the Article Detail page itself but define how navigation to other pages will behave. Any optional parameter appended to reading_list.html (such as articleId to highlight an item) is also generated purely from current article data and reflected only in the target page.

components.functionality: Reads the id URL parameter, calls getArticleDetail with articleId set to that id, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting article data and isSavedToReadingList flag to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read id from the URL and, if present, call getArticleDetail({ articleId: id }).
onUrlParamChange: When the id parameter changes (e.g., browser navigation), re-call getArticleDetail with the new articleId.
onGetArticleDetailSuccess: Store the returned article object and isSavedToReadingList boolean in component state and notify dependent components.
onGetArticleDetailError: Store error state and notify dependent components to render a not-found or error message instead of article content.

components.functionality: Displays the article’s title and core metadata (publish date, author, category) and exposes a back navigation control to return to the blog index.
event_handlers: onClickBackToBlog: Navigate the browser to blog.html.
onArticleDataChange: When new article data arrives from article-loader, update the displayed title and metadata.

components.functionality: Renders the main article body including hero image (if available) and full content returned by getArticleDetail.
event_handlers: onArticleDataChange: When article content changes, re-render the body and hero image.
onContentRenderError: If the content cannot be rendered (e.g., malformed markup), display a fallback error message while keeping the rest of the page functional.

components.functionality: Allows the user to save the current article to the default reading list with an optional note, reflects whether the article is already saved, and provides navigation to the reading list for management or unsaving.
event_handlers: onInitFromArticle: Receive the current article id and initial isSavedToReadingList value from article-loader and set the initial saved state.
onNoteInputChange: Update internal note state whenever the user types or edits the note field.
onClickSave: If the article is not currently marked as saved in local state, call saveArticleToReadingList({ articleId: currentArticleId, note: currentNoteValue }).
onSaveRequestStart: Set a loading flag and disable the save button to prevent duplicate submissions.
onSaveRequestSuccess: Receive readingListItemId and readingListId, update local saved state to true, clear loading flag, and display confirmation and a link to reading_list.html.
onSaveRequestError: Clear loading flag, keep saved state as false, and display an error message so the user can retry.
onClickViewReadingList: Navigate the browser to reading_list.html, optionally appending ?articleId=<currentArticleId> so the Reading List page can highlight this item.

components.functionality: Generates links to the blog index filtered by the current article’s tags and/or category to encourage further reading, including Tour Diary or news posts where relevant based on the article data.
event_handlers: onArticleDataChange: When article tags or category change, rebuild the set of related reading links (e.g., blog.html?tag=<tag> or blog.html?category=<category>).
onClickTagLink: Navigate the browser to blog.html with a tag query parameter derived from the clicked tag.
onClickCategoryLink: Navigate the browser to blog.html with a category query parameter derived from the article’s category.

components.functionality: Provides footer-level navigation options including returning to the blog index and opening the reading list after reading an article.
event_handlers: onClickBackToBlogFooter: Navigate the browser to blog.html.
onClickViewReadingListFooter: Navigate the browser to reading_list.html, optionally including articleId from the current article to assist the target page in focusing on this item.

components.functionality: Displays a clear error or not-found state when getArticleDetail cannot retrieve an article for the current id, and still offers navigation back to the blog index.
event_handlers: onGetArticleDetailError: When the loader reports an error, render an error message instead of article content.
onClickBackToBlogFromError: Navigate the browser to blog.html so the user can select another article.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom structure: a full-width global header (logo + primary navigation) sits at the very top, followed by a full-width white title band that labels the section (e.g., “ARTICLE” or “BLOG”). Below this, a centered, fixed-width main container is split into two columns on a 12-column grid: a dominant left column (about 8/12) for the article header and full article content, and a narrower right column (about 4/12) acting as a sidebar for auxiliary navigation and discovery (including the related-reading-links module alongside other blog widgets like search, recent posts, and categories). The article-footer-nav sits at the bottom of this main container, spanning the full container width beneath both columns. The article-loader and article-error-state occupy the space of the main content region below the title band, either overlaying or replacing the left column content as needed.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom structure: a full-width global header (logo + primary navigation) sits at the very top, followed by a full-width white title band that labels the section (e.g., “ARTICLE” or “BLOG”). Below this, a centered, fixed-width main container is split into two columns on a 12-column grid: a dominant left column (about 8/12) for the article header and full article content, and a narrower right column (about 4/12) acting as a sidebar for auxiliary navigation and discovery (including the related-reading-links module alongside other blog widgets like search, recent posts, and categories). The article-footer-nav sits at the bottom of this main container, spanning the full container width beneath both columns. The article-loader and article-error-state occupy the space of the main content region below the title band, either overlaying or replacing the left column content as needed.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The full-width header is relatively shallow but stretches edge to edge over the textured dark background. The white title band below it is visually tall, occupying roughly 15–20% of the viewport height with generous vertical padding (around 32–48px) and ample whitespace around the title text. The central main container beneath the band spans roughly 70–80% of the page width, centered on the screen. Within this container, the left content column uses about 65–70% of the container width, while the right sidebar uses about 30–35%. Inside the left column, the article-header and above-the-fold portion of article-content (including hero artwork) are given large vertical breathing room (24–32px between major blocks). Sidebar modules, including the related-reading-links, are more compact but still separated by 16–24px vertical gaps. The article-footer-nav has extra spacing above it (about 32–48px from the end of the article/content) so it feels like a footer band, not another dense content block.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is intentionally mixed: the header and white title band are very airy, with a lot of negative space and minimal elements, reflecting a minimalist aesthetic. The main article area is more content-dense but still controlled: text lines are reasonably short, margins are consistent, and large visuals such as the hero artwork nearly fill the left column but are cushioned by 16–24px margins. Sidebar modules are compact, stack vertically, and rely on clear headings and consistent padding (16–24px internal padding with 16–24px gaps between modules) to avoid visual clutter. Functional elements like the reading-list-control are compact and integrated into existing blocks (e.g., within the article-header area) without adding heavy visual noise. Loader and error states are visually simple and centered, preserving the minimalist, high-contrast feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow mirrors an F-pattern. The user’s eye starts at the top-left with the hand-drawn logo in the header, skims right across the main navigation, then drops down to the high-contrast, centered page title in the white band. From there, attention falls into the left content column: first to the article-header (title and metadata), then to the prominent hero artwork and body of the article-content. After the main content is registered, the eye naturally shifts to the right sidebar column, starting at its topmost module and scanning downward through stacked blocks (including the related-reading-links). Finally, as the user scrolls to the bottom, the eye lands on the article-footer-nav, which provides a clear navigational end-cap to the reading experience. When loading or error states appear, they occupy the same left-column focal area beneath the title band, ensuring the user immediately understands the page’s state without breaking the established flow.

layout.overall_description: The article detail page sits on a textured, almost matte-black background with a hand-drawn band logo in a slim full-width header at the top. Directly beneath, a broad, full-width white band stretches across the page, with the section title (like “ARTICLE”) set in bold, high-contrast type and surrounded by generous vertical whitespace, giving a striking, poster-like feel. Below this band, the main content snaps into a centered, fixed-width container that rides on top of the dark background. Inside that container, an 8/12–4/12 two-column grid forms: the left column carries the article’s title, metadata, hero artwork, and full text in a clean, linear stack; the right column is a sidebar of compact, dark modules, each with sharp headings, subtle separators, and enough padding to feel distinct but cohesive. The article’s save-to-reading-list control is integrated near the title area, keeping the key action in the first sweep of the eye. As users scroll down through the article, the sidebar ends earlier, leaving a clean dark margin on the right while the article continues in the left column. At the very bottom of the main container, a low-profile navigation bar spans the full width, offering links back to the blog index, the reading list, and neighboring posts, acting as a grounded, band-like footer. When the article is loading, a centered loader overlays the content columns below the white band; if an error occurs, a bold but minimal error panel occupies the left column’s prime space. Overall, the layout feels dark, edgy, and spacious, with a strong F-shaped reading pattern and a balance of underground grit and professional structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-loader appears within the main content region, directly beneath the white page-title band and above the article-footer-nav. When active, it overlays both the left article column and the right sidebar column inside the centered main container. It is positioned absolutely relative to this container, stretching nearly the full width of the container (about 70–80% of the viewport width) and sufficient height to cover the visible portion of the article area. At its center—both horizontally and vertically within this overlay—a compact, high-contrast loader indicator (such as a circular spinner or edgy, band-style progress glyph) is placed. The surrounding overlay is a semi-transparent dark veil that slightly mutes the underlying content area while keeping the global header and white title band fully visible above. Internally, the loader’s content (icon and short status text) is padded by roughly 24px on all sides, ensuring it feels intentional and not cramped. As the user scrolls, the loader overlay moves with the main content (it is tied to the content container, not fixed to the viewport), so it always covers the article area until loading completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an absolute overlay within the centered main content container, covering both left and right columns beneath the title band. It scrolls with the page (not fixed to the viewport) and temporarily sits on top of the article-header, article-content, sidebar modules, and related-reading-links until loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header is the first piece of content in the left column of the main container, sitting directly below the white title band with a generous vertical gap of about 32px. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the content container) and aligns flush with the container’s left margin on desktop. The header block itself has internal padding of around 24px top and bottom and 16–24px left and right, maintaining the moderately spacious feel. The article title is left-aligned, occupying most of the left column’s width, with a large, bold type size that visually anchors this section. Directly below the title, a single line or compact stack of metadata (e.g., date, author, category) appears, with slightly reduced font size and tighter spacing (about 8–12px below the title). On wide screens, the reading-list-control is docked to the right edge of this header block, aligned horizontally with either the top of the title or the metadata row, giving the header a subtle two-column feel within the left column: title and meta on the left, action control on the right. On narrower screens, the header collapses into a single column, with the reading-list-control stacking beneath the title and meta but still within the padded header block. A subtle bottom spacing of around 24px separates the article-header from the start of the article-content, with no heavy borders, keeping the look minimal but clearly segmented.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block at the top of the left content column, scrolling normally with the article. It anchors the reading-list-control in its upper-right region on larger viewports and reflows to a stacked layout on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content sits immediately beneath the article-header in the left column, separated by roughly 24px of vertical whitespace. It spans the full width of the left column and forms the main vertical reading spine of the page. At the very top of this block, a large hero artwork image nearly fills the left column width, maintaining a consistent margin of 0–8px from the column edges (depending on overall gutter strategy) and an aspect ratio around 16:9 or slightly taller to create a strong visual hit. About 16–24px of space separates the hero image from the first paragraph of text. The body text is set with comfortable line length, constrained by the left column width, and generous line spacing, so each paragraph breathes against the dark background. Between major sub-sections (e.g., subheadings, blockquotes, embedded media), vertical spacing of 16–24px is used; between paragraphs, spacing is tighter, around 8–12px. If there are inline media elements (additional images, pull quotes), they align with the text column, never exceeding the left column width, and have 16–24px of top and bottom padding to avoid crowding. As the user scrolls, the article-content extends downward, often surpassing the height of the right sidebar; once the sidebar ends, the left column continues alone, leaving a clean, empty dark area on the right. At the bottom of the article-content, there is a clear 32–48px gap before any lower-level components (such as related-reading-links if placed below in mobile layouts, or the article-footer-nav) to preserve hierarchy and avoid visual merging.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically flowing content within the left column that scrolls naturally. It respects the column boundaries and grid, flexing in height according to article length while maintaining consistent horizontal alignment with the sidebar modules to its right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-control is a compact, high-contrast form element tightly associated with the article-header. On desktop and larger tablet widths, it is positioned inside the article-header block, anchored near the top-right corner of the left column. It forms a small horizontal pill or button group, roughly 25–30% of the header block’s width, aligning to the right edge while leaving at least 16px of internal right padding from the header’s border. Vertically, it is aligned with either the baseline of the article title or the center of the metadata row, providing a strong visual tie between the article identity and the save action. There is at least 8–12px of internal spacing around its label and icon to keep it usable and touch-friendly. On smaller screens, where width becomes constrained, this control reflows to sit directly underneath the title and metadata within the same header block, spanning about 50–70% of the left column width and centered or left-aligned, with around 16px of vertical spacing above and below. On all viewports, it avoids touching the hero artwork or any text from article-content, maintaining a clear gap (approximately 24px) between the bottom of the header block and the start of the content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Attached to and positioned within the article-header. It is not sticky; it scrolls away with the header as the user moves down the page. It shifts from a right-aligned inline element (on wide screens) to a stacked, full-width or partial-width control (on smaller screens) to preserve readability and tap targets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-reading-links component is presented as a dedicated module within the right sidebar column, reinforcing the F-pattern’s secondary vertical scan. It is positioned below the core sidebar widgets (such as search, recent posts, and categories) and separated from the module above by a vertical gap of roughly 24px. The module itself aligns to the right column’s grid, spanning the full sidebar width (about 30–35% of the main container) and uses internal padding of around 16–24px on all sides. At the top of the module, a compact, all-caps heading like “RELATED READING” appears, left-aligned, with 8–12px of space beneath it. Below the heading, individual related links are stacked vertically, each taking the full module width with 8–12px of vertical spacing between them. Each link line is concise, with optional small metadata (e.g., date or category) in a lighter tone or smaller size, aligned beneath or beside the title. To maintain the edgy, minimalist aesthetic, the module might have a subtle top border or tone-on-tone background panel that is slightly lighter than the overall black, clearly delineating it from the main background without feeling boxy. The bottom of the module has a 16–24px margin before the next sidebar component (if any) or the end of the sidebar, ensuring it doesn’t feel cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static sidebar module within the right column, scrolls with the page. It maintains alignment with other sidebar modules and does not stick or float; on narrow screens where the layout collapses to a single column, it should move below the main article-content, becoming a full-width related section with the same internal structure and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-footer-nav sits at the very bottom of the main content container, beneath both the article-content and the right sidebar modules. It is separated from the final content element above (usually the end of the article, possibly followed by related-reading-links in mobile layouts) by a generous 32–48px gap, which makes it feel like a distinct footer band rather than an extension of the article text. Horizontally, it spans the full width of the centered main container, aligning with both the left and right columns, and sits on the dark textured background with a subtle top border or a slightly lighter strip of color to visually differentiate it from the content above. Inside, navigation links (such as “Back to Blog”, “Reading List”, and “Previous/Next Article”) are arranged in a low-profile horizontal row on desktop: key links like “Back to Blog” are aligned left, “Reading List” may sit center, and previous/next controls align right, creating a balanced distribution. On smaller screens, these links stack vertically or form two rows, with 8–12px vertical spacing. The internal padding of this band is around 16–24px top and bottom, mirroring the spacing used in the header for visual symmetry.
