The typography here is slightly smaller than the main body text, emphasizing its navigational, tertiary nature while remaining legible against the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static footer-like navigation bar that anchors the end of the main content. It is not sticky; it appears only when the user reaches the bottom of the article and related content. It maintains alignment with the main container grid and adjusts its internal layout from horizontal (desktop) to stacked or wrapped (mobile).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-error-state occupies the same spatial territory as the normal article content within the left column, ensuring that error feedback appears exactly where users expect the article to be. When active, it replaces both the article-header and article-content blocks in the left column, starting at the same vertical offset beneath the white title band (approximately 32px down). It spans the full width of the left column and is centered horizontally within the main container. Internally, it resembles a clean, compact panel: a bold error icon or glyph sits near the top left or center, followed by an error title (e.g., “Article Could Not Load”) and a short explanatory text. The panel uses generous internal padding (24px on all sides) and vertical spacing of around 12–16px between the icon, title, and message. To keep the minimalist, edgy aesthetic, the panel may use a slightly lighter dark tone or a thin border to distinguish it from the underlying background. If recovery actions are available (e.g., a “Back to Blog” link or “Retry” button), they appear beneath the message, left-aligned or center-aligned, with about 16px of space above them. The right sidebar may remain visible to the right, providing alternative navigation, or be simplified depending on implementation; in either case, the error module remains the dominant element in the left column. A comfortable 32–48px gap separates the bottom of the error panel from the article-footer-nav, if the footer-nav is shown in the error state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement of the normal article content in the left column. It scrolls with the page and does not overlay the header or title band. When active, it pushes the article-footer-nav down, maintaining the overall vertical flow of the layout.

name: Reading List / Saved Articles
title: Reading List
description: Page where users can view, filter, and manage their saved articles, and open any saved article in its detail page.

core_features: Load the default reading list and all associated saved articles using getReadingListArticles on page load.
Display all saved articles with key metadata (title, publication date, tags, and date saved).
Allow users to open any saved article by clicking its title, navigating to article.html?id={article.id}.
Allow users to remove individual articles from the reading list using removeArticleFromReadingList.
Provide client-side filtering of the loaded items by publication year (derived from article.publishedAt).
Provide client-side filtering of the loaded items by tag (derived from article.tags), with filter state stored in URL parameters.
Optionally provide client-side sorting (e.g., by saved date or publication date) based on a URL parameter.
Optionally provide client-side pagination of the loaded items, driven purely by URL parameters and without additional API calls.
Display contextual empty states (no saved articles at all, or no results for current filters) with actions such as clearing filters.
user_workflows: Viewing the reading list and opening an article: 1) User navigates to reading_list.html via the footer link or a direct link. 2) On page load, the page calls getReadingListArticles and receives the readingList metadata and items. 3) The list component renders all saved articles based on current URL parameters (default is unfiltered, first page, default sort). 4) The user scans the list and clicks an article title. 5) The browser navigates to article.html?id={selectedArticle.id}, where the article detail page displays the full content.

Filtering the reading list by year and tag: 1) User opens reading_list.html; getReadingListArticles returns all saved items. 2) The filter component derives available years from article.publishedAt and tags from article.tags across the loaded items. 3) The user selects a year and/or tag filter. 4) The filter component updates the URL query parameters (e.g., ?year={YYYY}&tag={encodedTag}) and resets the page parameter to 1. 5) The list component re-evaluates items in memory, including only those whose article.publishedAt year and tags match the URL parameters, and re-renders the filtered list. 6) The user can click "Clear filters", which removes year and tag parameters from the URL and re-renders the full list.

Finding and opening the first saved Tour Diary entry for a specific year (e.g., 2024): 1) User opens reading_list.html. 2) In the filters, the user selects the tag corresponding to the desired series (e.g., the tag that represents 'Tour Diary') and selects the year (e.g., 2024). 3) Optionally, the user chooses a sort option that orders items by savedAt ascending. 4) The page updates the URL (e.g., ?tag={tourDiaryTag}&year=2024&sort=saved_asc&page=1) and re-renders the filtered, sorted list. 5) The first item in the list is now the first article saved that matches the chosen tag and year; the user clicks the title to navigate to article.html?id={article.id}.

Removing a single article from the reading list: 1) User is on reading_list.html with items loaded via getReadingListArticles. 2) Next to each article entry, the user sees a remove control (such as a button). 3) The user clicks the remove control for a specific entry. 4) The item-actions component calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the corresponding readingListItemId. 5) On success, the component updates the in-memory collection (or triggers a reload via getReadingListArticles) to remove the item, and the list re-renders respecting current filters and pagination. 6) If the current page becomes empty due to removal, the pagination logic adjusts the page parameter in the URL to the highest valid page and re-renders.

Navigating through pages of saved articles (if pagination is enabled): 1) User opens reading_list.html; getReadingListArticles loads all items. 2) Based on a configured page size and the total number of (filtered) items, the pagination component computes how many pages are available. 3) The URL parameter page (default 1) determines which slice of the filtered list is rendered. 4) When the user clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or a specific page number), the pagination component updates the URL parameter page accordingly. 5) The list component re-renders to show only the items for the selected page, without any new API calls.

Handling empty and error states: 1) If getReadingListArticles returns successfully but with no items, the empty-state component displays a message indicating there are no saved articles and can provide a navigation link (e.g., back to the main articles section). 2) If filters are applied and the filtered result is empty, the empty-state component indicates that no articles match the current filters and provides a control to clear filters (which removes filter parameters from the URL and re-renders). 3) If getReadingListArticles or removeArticleFromReadingList returns an error, the feedback component displays an error message and, for getReadingListArticles, a 'Try again' control that triggers a retry of the API call.
interactions: On page load of reading_list.html, the data-loader component invokes getReadingListArticles with no parameters, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting readingList and items to other components.

When the user changes the year filter, the filter-controls component updates the URL parameter year and resets page to 1; the list component re-filters the in-memory items by the selected publication year.

When the user changes the tag filter, the filter-controls component updates the URL parameter tag and resets page to 1; the list component re-filters the items to those whose article.tags include the specified tag.

When the user changes the sort selection, the filter-controls (or a sort sub-component) updates the URL parameter sort; the list component re-sorts the filtered items in memory according to the specified sort mode.
When the user clears filters, the filter-controls component removes year and tag (and optionally sort) parameters from the URL; the list component reverts to using all items with the default sort.
When the user clicks an article title, the list component navigates the browser to article.html?id={article.id} using the article.id from the bound data.

When the user clicks the remove control for an item, the item-actions component calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the associated readingListItemId, then on success updates the central items collection (or triggers a reload via getReadingListArticles) and informs the pagination component to recompute page counts.

When the user changes page via pagination controls, the pagination component updates the page parameter in the URL; the list component calculates which subset of the filtered items to render based on the page value and the configured page size.

When API operations succeed or fail (load or removal), the feedback component displays transient success or error messages and allows the user to dismiss them; for load errors it also provides a retry action that re-invokes getReadingListArticles.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely derived from the combination of (1) the API response from getReadingListArticles and (2) URL query parameters. The interface getReadingListArticles always returns all items for the default reading list without accepting parameters, so filtering, sorting, and pagination are handled exclusively on the client side. URL parameters drive state as follows: 'year' (optional) filters items to those whose article.publishedAt year matches the value; it is parsed as an integer and compared against the year extracted from the ISO timestamp. 'tag' (optional) filters items to those whose article.tags array contains the exact tag string (after URL decoding). 'sort' (optional) determines the ordering of filtered items in memory. Supported values can include modes such as 'saved_desc' (default, sort by savedAt descending), 'saved_asc', 'published_desc', 'published_asc', or 'title_asc'. Any unsupported value falls back to the default sort. 'page' (optional) controls which page of the filtered, sorted items is displayed. It is parsed as an integer; values less than 1 are normalized to 1, and values greater than the maximum page count are normalized down to the maximum. The page size is a configurable constant in the implementation and is used only for slicing the in-memory collection. When year or tag (or potentially sort) changes, page is reset to 1 to avoid empty pages. All components read URLSearchParams on initialization and when the URL changes (e.g., via history.replaceState or history.pushState triggered by filter and pagination interactions). No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; refreshing the page re-reads the API and the current URL parameters to reconstruct the state. Navigation to article.html passes only the article id in the query string (article.html?id={article.id}) as specified.

components.functionality: On initial page load and on explicit reload requests, this component invokes getReadingListArticles to fetch the default reading list and its items. It manages loading and error states and provides the resulting readingList metadata and items array to dependent components via a shared state or context mechanism.

Displays basic information about the current reading list (name and optional description from the API), along with the count of currently visible items after filters are applied. It may also host a clear-filters control that delegates to the filter-controls component.

Provides controls for filtering and sorting the reading list based on the already-loaded items. It derives available years from article.publishedAt and available tags from article.tags across all items returned by getReadingListArticles. When the user selects or changes filters or sort order, it updates the URL query parameters (year, tag, sort, page) and notifies listeners so that the list and pagination components re-evaluate the in-memory collection.

Computes pagination metadata based on the number of items that remain after filtering and the implementation's configured page size. Renders controls for navigating between pages. When a page control is activated, it updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers a re-render of the list component to show the correct slice of items.

Renders the list of saved articles according to the current filters, sort order, and page derived from URL parameters. For each item, it displays the article title, publication date, tags, and savedAt information. The title is a link that navigates to article.html?id={article.id}. It also integrates the item-actions component for removal controls.

Attached to each list item, this component provides the control to remove the article from the reading list. When activated, it calls removeArticleFromReadingList with the item's readingListItemId, handles loading and error states for that action, and on success removes the item from the shared items collection or requests a reload from the data-loader component.

Displays appropriate messaging and actions when there are no items to show. If there are no saved items at all from getReadingListArticles, it shows a baseline empty state. If there are items overall but none match the current filters, it indicates that filters caused the empty result and offers a clear-filters action that removes filter-related URL parameters.

Centralizes display of transient messages for operations on this page. It listens for events from the data-loader (success or failure of getReadingListArticles) and from item-actions (success or failure of removeArticleFromReadingList) and shows success or error notifications accordingly. It also provides controls for dismissing notifications and, in case of load errors, for retrying the data-loader.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width header (existing globally), followed by a full-width white/light title band for the 'Reading List' page title. Below that, use a centered fixed-width main content container with a two-column layout. The left main column (approx. 65–70% width) holds the reading list and its core controls (toolbar, list, pagination, empty state, data-loader overlay). The right sidebar (approx. 30–35% width) holds secondary/utility elements, mapping to the mockup’s search and widgets—here this becomes the filtering controls and any persistent feedback/notification center hook.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width header (existing globally), followed by a full-width white/light title band for the 'Reading List' page title. Below that, use a centered fixed-width main content container with a two-column layout. The left main column (approx. 65–70% width) holds the reading list and its core controls (toolbar, list, pagination, empty state, data-loader overlay). The right sidebar (approx. 30–35% width) holds secondary/utility elements, mapping to the mockup’s search and widgets—here this becomes the filtering controls and any persistent feedback/notification center hook.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Keep the header relatively shallow but spanning full width. The title band below it is taller and airy, with generous vertical padding (around 48–64px top and bottom) and minimal content besides the page title and an optional subtitle. The main content container below occupies the bulk of the viewport height, with a vertical gap of about 32–40px from the title band. Inside the container, the left main column takes about 8 of 12 grid columns (roughly two-thirds of the width), and the right sidebar takes about 4 of 12 columns (one-third). Within the main column, the list and pagination fill the width edge-to-edge within their padding, while sidebar modules use compact blocks with consistent inner padding (16–24px).
layout_strategies.content_density: Honor the mockup’s mix of spacious framing and denser content: the header and title band remain visually light with lots of negative space. The main content area is more information-dense but still breathable: list items are stacked with clear separation, item actions are visible but not cramped, and filters in the sidebar are organized into concise, well-padded modules. Vertical spacing between major blocks (toolbar, list, pagination, sidebar modules) is around 24–32px; spacing between elements inside those blocks is tighter, around 8–16px.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-pattern. The eye first catches the logo and navigation in the global header (top-left to top-right), then drops to the high-contrast page title in the white band, aligned left within the band. From there, attention moves down into the left main column where the reading list itself lives. Users scan down the list of saved articles vertically, then their gaze shifts horizontally right to the sidebar to refine/filter what they see. Notification/feedback elements appear in the upper-right area of the content container, echoing the mockup’s secondary attention zone. Overall, the layout leads users left-to-right across the top and then down the primary content column, with the sidebar and notifications as secondary attention anchors.

layout.overall_description: On a dark, slightly textured background, the page starts with a full-width, low-height header featuring the band’s hand-drawn logo on the left and navigation on the right. Beneath it, a full-width white (or very light) title band stretches across the screen, framed by generous vertical padding so that the 'Reading List' title has room to breathe and stand out in stark contrast against the dark surroundings. Below this band, a centered main content container snaps into place, fixed to a roughly 1200px width and aligned on a 12-column grid. Inside this container, the layout splits into two columns: a dominant left column holding the reading list itself and its primary controls, and a narrower right sidebar that houses filtering tools and utility modules. The left column presents a clear vertical stack: a compact toolbar at the top, the list of saved articles below it, and pagination controls at the bottom. When there are no saved items, a visually prominent empty state takes over this column, centered both horizontally and vertically within the available space. A semi-transparent loader can overlay this main area when data is being fetched. On the right, sidebar modules—starting with filter controls—are stacked vertically with dark panels, subtle borders or dividers, and consistent padding, echoing the 'Recent Posts' and 'Category' widgets from the mockup. Notification toasts appear near the upper-right corner of the main content area, slightly overlapping both columns without disturbing the grid, reinforcing the edgy, layered feel. The result is a dark, modern, band-appropriate reading list: structured and professional, but with enough contrast, spacing, and hierarchy to feel both underground and highly usable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as an overlay centered over the main content container, respecting the two-column layout beneath it. When active, a semi-transparent dark veil (e.g., 60–70% black) covers both the left main column and the right sidebar, starting from just below the white title band down to slightly above the bottom of the viewport. In the visual center of the container—roughly aligned with the mid-point of the left column—a loading indicator (spinner or minimal icon with a short label like 'Loading…') is displayed. This indicator occupies a compact area, about 15–20% of the container’s width, and is vertically offset slightly above the true center to keep it closer to the user’s typical reading zone. The overlay does not extend into the header or title band; it feels like a layer floating over the content area rather than the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-cover overlay relative to the main content container (positioned absolute or fixed within that container), blocking interaction with both columns while visible but not shifting any underlying layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list toolbar sits at the very top of the left main column, directly below the main content container’s top padding, aligning horizontally with the top edge of the right sidebar modules. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the container width) with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. Vertically, it is a low-profile bar: about 48–56px tall, echoing the compact yet clear nav feel from the header. Inside, key controls (e.g., a small 'Saved Articles' label, total count, and sorting options) align from left to right, respecting the F-pattern: primary label and count on the left, secondary actions like sort or view toggles on the right. The toolbar has a slightly lighter dark tone than the background, with perhaps a subtle top or bottom border line, giving it a clear yet understated separation from the list below. A vertical gap of around 24px separates it from the list component beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable main column. On taller screens with long lists, it scrolls up and out of view with the content (not sticky by default), keeping behavior simple and consistent with the mockup’s non-sticky header sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter controls occupy the top-most module inside the right sidebar, mirroring the position of the search widget in the mockup. The sidebar starts at the same vertical level as the toolbar in the left column, so the top edges of the toolbar and filter module align horizontally. The filter controls module spans the full width of the sidebar, roughly 30–35% of the main content container. It appears as a dark, card-like block with 16–24px internal padding and a subtle outer separation from the background (e.g., a faint border or elevated shadow). At the top of this module, a small, uppercase label like 'FILTER' or 'REFINE' is placed left-aligned, with 8–12px space below it. Below that, the primary filter input—most likely a search field—runs nearly the full width of the sidebar with tight vertical margins. Additional filter elements such as dropdowns (e.g., 'Sort by') or tag chips stack vertically below the main search field with 8–16px spacing between them. The overall height is compact but not cramped, approximately 160–240px depending on the number of controls. A margin of about 24px separates this module from any subsequent sidebar widgets or the bottom of the sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the sidebar. It scrolls vertically with the page; it does not stick to the top when scrolling, maintaining a simple stacked layout like the mockup’s sidebar widgets.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the left main column, directly beneath the reading list. They sit on their own horizontal strip with generous top margin (around 32px) from the last list item or empty-state content, acting as a clear footer to the main content feed. The pagination bar spans the full width of the left column but centers its controls horizontally: page numbers and next/previous arrows form a compact row centered in the column, leaving equal negative space on the left and right. Vertical space above and below the controls is moderate (about 16–24px), mirroring the airy feel of the title band but scaled down for this smaller component. Buttons or links use high-contrast text on dark buttons or borders, fitting the edgy aesthetic. There is no major interaction with the sidebar beyond aligning visually so that the bottom of the pagination roughly aligns with the end of the sidebar modules if their content length is similar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main column. It moves down as more list content is loaded or as the window is resized, and scrolls off-screen as the user scrolls past it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list component occupies the core of the left main column, sitting directly below the toolbar with about 24px vertical separation. It spans 100% of the left column’s width, with consistent inner padding (16–24px) on its left and right edges so that list items align visually with elements in the toolbar and do not touch the column edges. List items are stacked vertically, following the downward stroke of the F-pattern. Each item takes the full module width and has a moderate height, perhaps 96–144px, depending on content. Within each item, the article title is left-aligned and runs horizontally across the majority of the width, with metadata (e.g., publication name, date, tags) underneath in smaller text, leaving plenty of breathing room (8–12px between lines). If thumbnails or artwork are used, they appear on the far left of each item, occupying about 20–25% of the row width, with text content to the right. The visual rhythm is created by consistent vertical gaps between items (16–24px) and thin dividers or subtle background shifts, echoing the way the mockup’s main image and text blocks feel distinct but related. The list extends downwards and scrolls naturally, with the sidebar visible to its right as long as the viewport allows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical stack within the main column, forming the primary scrollable content. It expands vertically with more items and defines the overall page scroll height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Item actions are integrated within each row of the reading list, positioned towards the right side of each list item to align with the F-pattern’s secondary horizontal attention. In a typical list item, the article title and metadata occupy the left and center, while the actions—such as 'Open', 'Remove', or 'Move to Playlist'—are grouped into a tight horizontal cluster on the far right of the item’s content area. This action cluster sits vertically centered relative to the item’s height, with 8–16px padding around it so it doesn’t hug the item edges. On narrower screens or when content wraps, these actions can stack into a compact vertical group beneath the metadata but remain right-aligned. They do not define their own separate row; instead, they sit within the item’s background panel, using either icon-only buttons or small, high-contrast text buttons that stand out against the dark backdrop without overpowering the title. The action area visually aligns with the right boundary of the list column and, indirectly, with the right edge of the toolbar above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within each list item, flowing with the item’s layout. They do not affect page-level layout; they simply occupy their reserved region within each list row and move with the list as it scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state takes over the left main column whenever there are no saved articles to display. It occupies the same footprint as the list would: directly beneath the toolbar, spanning the full width of the left column with consistent horizontal padding (16–24px). Vertically, the empty state is more expansive and centered, using the spaciousness of the mockup’s title band as inspiration. The main empty-state message (e.g., 'No saved articles yet') sits near the vertical center of the column, centered horizontally. A supporting line of text and a primary call-to-action button (such as 'Browse Articles') appear below, with 12–16px spacing between each line. Above and below this block, generous empty space ensures it feels deliberate and calm, not just a blank area. The right sidebar remains visible and unchanged, reinforcing the two-column layout even in the absence of data. Overall, it feels like a bold, minimal statement block on the dark background, mirroring the high-impact, low-clutter aesthetic of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, replacing the list content area when the list is empty. It scrolls with the page but will often sit centered in the viewport on typical screen heights due to surrounding vertical padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification center manifests as small, stacked toast-style messages appearing near the upper-right corner of the main content area, overlapping both the left and right columns slightly but anchored visually to the sidebar zone. The first toast appears just below the bottom edge of the white title band and inset from the right edge of the main content container by about 16–24px, aligning its right edge with the sidebar’s outer edge. Additional toasts stack downward with 8–12px vertical gaps. Each toast is a compact dark rectangle with 12–16px padding, subtle border or glow, and high-contrast text that can be read at a glance. They do not push content out of the way; instead, they float above it like layered stickers, reinforcing the edgy, layered aesthetic. When no notifications are present, there is no reserved space in the layout; the area remains clean, with only the sidebar visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed relative to the viewport while within the bounds of the main content area (e.g., positioned fixed to the top-right of the viewport, but visually aligned with the main container’s right edge). Toasts overlay the two-column layout without shifting any underlying content and automatically stack and disappear after a short duration.

name: Newsletter Signup
title: Newsletter Signup
description: Page for visitors to subscribe to the band’s newsletter, select their interests, region, and preferred email frequency, and receive feedback after submitting.

core_features: Display a newsletter signup form that collects name, email, interests, region, and email frequency.
Validate required fields (at minimum email and frequency) before allowing submission.
Submit the subscription data via the submitNewsletterSubscription interface and handle the returned response.
Show success or error status after a submission attempt, including any message returned by the interface.
Reflect key page states (initial, submitting, success, error) in the URL using query parameters instead of localStorage.
Provide a "Back to Home" navigation action that takes the user to index.html after successful subscription.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: First-time visitor subscribing
1. User navigates to newsletter.html, possibly with a source parameter (e.g., newsletter.html?source=from_footer).
2. The page controller reads the source parameter and stores it in in-memory state for analytics/logging (it does not change form defaults).
3. The newsletter signup form is shown in its initial state with empty fields and no status message.
4. User enters their name (optional) and email (required).
5. User selects one or more interests from the available interest options derived from the NewsletterSubscription.interests field definition.
6. User selects their region from options derived from the NewsletterSubscription.region enum.
7. User selects their preferred email frequency from options derived from the NewsletterSubscription.frequency enum.
8. User submits the form.
9. The form component performs client-side validation (e.g., email present and syntactically valid, frequency selected; any additional rules defined centrally).
10. If validation fails, the validation summary and inline field errors are shown; no interface call is made.
11. If validation passes, the page enters a "submitting" state (e.g., disabling the submit button) and calls submitNewsletterSubscription with the current form values.
12. When the interface responds successfully, the page controller updates internal state with the returned subscriptionId, confirmed flag, and message.
13. The URL is updated using the History API to add or replace a status parameter indicating success (e.g., newsletter.html?source=from_footer&status=subscribed). No page reload is required.
14. The success status panel is displayed, including the message returned by submitNewsletterSubscription and any confirmation information (such as whether the subscription is already confirmed or will require further confirmation).
15. A "Back to Home" navigation control is shown, allowing the user to navigate to index.html.
16. If the user clicks "Back to Home", the browser navigates to index.html (without additional parameters, as defined in outgoing navigation).

Workflow 2: Handling submission errors
1. User fills out the form and submits.
2. Client-side validation passes; submitNewsletterSubscription is called.
3. The interface returns an error condition (e.g., via a message field indicating a failure or by not providing a valid subscriptionId). The exact error handling logic is based on the returned object structure and any error channel defined in implementation.
4. The page controller interprets the response as an error state and sets an internal error flag and error message.
5. The URL is updated to reflect an error status (e.g., newsletter.html?status=error&source=from_footer) using the History API without reloading the page.
6. The error status panel is displayed, showing a user-facing error message (using the message from the interface when available).
7. The form remains visible with previously entered values so that the user can adjust inputs.
8. The user corrects the data (e.g., fixes the email or changes frequency) and resubmits.
9. Steps 2–8 of Workflow 1 repeat until success or the user abandons the process.

Workflow 3: Returning user arriving on a URL with status parameter
1. User lands on newsletter.html with a status parameter already present in the URL (e.g., newsletter.html?status=subscribed or newsletter.html?status=error), either from bookmarking, sharing the URL, or a redirect.
2. The page controller parses both the source and status parameters during initialization.
3. If status=subscribed, the page assumes a success state: it displays the success status panel and may hide or collapse the form by default while still allowing the user to reveal it again if they want to change their preferences.
4. If status=error, the page displays an error status panel prompting the user to fill in or correct the form.
5. If no recognized status is provided, the page displays the default initial form state with no status panel.
6. Any changes the user makes and subsequent submission again call submitNewsletterSubscription and update both UI state and URL parameters as in Workflows 1–2.

Workflow 4: User changes preferences after seeing success
1. After a successful subscription, the success status panel is visible and the URL contains status=subscribed.
2. The user decides to change their interests, region, or frequency and clicks a control such as "Update preferences" that reveals the form if it is collapsed.
3. The form fields may be prefilled with the last submitted values held in in-memory state (no persisted data is required or assumed in this design).
4. The user edits their preferences and submits again.
5. The page validates the new inputs and calls submitNewsletterSubscription with the updated values.
6. After the response, the success panel updates to reflect the latest message and confirmation status.
7. The URL remains or is reset to status=subscribed to reflect the current successful state.
8. The user can again choose to navigate back to index.html using the "Back to Home" control.
interactions: Typing into text fields (name, email): updates the local form state in the newsletter-signup-form component via onChange handlers.

Selecting or deselecting interest options: toggles entries in the interests array in local state via onToggleInterest handlers; the allowed interest codes are taken from the NewsletterSubscription.interests field definition.
Choosing a region from a dropdown: sets the region value in form state; options are derived from the NewsletterSubscription.region enum; handled by onChangeRegion.

Choosing an email frequency (e.g., via radio buttons or a select control): sets the frequency value in form state; options are derived from the NewsletterSubscription.frequency enum; handled by onChangeFrequency.
Clicking the primary submit button: triggers onSubmit, which runs client-side validation, and if valid, calls submitNewsletterSubscription with the current form data and manages the submitting state.
Viewing validation errors: when validation fails, a validation summary is displayed and focus may move to the first invalid field; the user can click into fields to correct them.

Handling API success: when submitNewsletterSubscription resolves successfully, onSubmitSuccess updates the status panel state, tracks subscriptionId and confirmed, updates the URL (e.g., status=subscribed), and optionally collapses the form.
Handling API failure: when submitNewsletterSubscription indicates failure, onSubmitError sets an error message, updates the URL (e.g., status=error), and leaves the form filled for corrections.
Clicking "Back to Home": in the success state, the back-to-home-link component handles onClickBackToHome and navigates to index.html.
Clicking a control to dismiss a status message: onDismissStatus clears the status/error panel and may remove the status parameter from the URL, returning the page to its neutral form-focused state.
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page controller reads URL query parameters, primarily source and status. The source parameter is treated as an opaque string that represents where the user navigated from; it is stored in transient page state for analytics or logging purposes but does not alter form defaults or interface behavior directly. The status parameter is used to drive the UI state machine: if status is 'subscribed' (or an implementation-defined success token), the page enters a success state where the success status panel is shown, and the form may be collapsed by default. If status is 'error' (or an implementation-defined error token), the page initializes in an error state where an error status panel is visible and the form is ready for correction. If status is absent or unrecognized, the page shows the initial blank form with no status panel. After each submission attempt, the page updates the URL using the History API (e.g., pushState or replaceState) to reflect the new status without reloading the page, ensuring that the current state can be restored on refresh or if the user shares/bookmarks the URL. The source parameter is preserved when updating the URL so that origin information is not lost. No state is stored in localStorage or any other persistent client-side storage; all session state beyond what is encoded in the URL lives in memory only for the current page view.

components.functionality: Coordinates page-level behavior: parses URL parameters (source, status), initializes child components with appropriate state (e.g., whether to show the success or error status panel), manages transitions between states (initial, submitting, success, error), and updates the URL when state changes after submissions.

Collects user input for the newsletter subscription, including name, email, interests, region, and frequency. Performs client-side validation before allowing submission. On successful validation, it delegates to the page controller to call submitNewsletterSubscription. It can be rendered in an editable state or collapsed (read-only view) depending on the page controller's instructions (for example, after a successful subscription).

Encapsulates the preferences fields inside the form: interest selection, region selection, and frequency selection. It reads available options from the NewsletterSubscription field definitions (interests, region enum, frequency enum) and binds current selections to the parent form state. It reports changes upward via event handlers so the main form component can maintain a single source of truth.

Displays a list of validation errors for the newsletter signup form after the user attempts submission with invalid or incomplete data. It indicates which fields are invalid and can provide anchors or focus management to move the user directly to the problematic fields.

Shows the outcome of the most recent submission. In success mode, it displays the message returned by submitNewsletterSubscription along with any additional confirmation indication based on the confirmed flag. In error mode, it presents an error message derived from the interface response or a generic fallback. It also exposes controls such as dismissing the message or revealing the form again if it was collapsed after success.

Provides a navigation control to return to the homepage (index.html) after a successful subscription or whenever the page controller determines it should be shown. It is typically rendered within or near the submission status panel in the success state.

Abstracts the interaction with the submitNewsletterSubscription interface. It receives sanitized and validated form data from the page controller or form component and invokes submitNewsletterSubscription(name, email, interests, region, frequency). It returns a standardized result object to the page controller containing subscriptionId, confirmed, and message, and also an error indicator when applicable.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a full-width, dark header (with band logo and main navigation, owned structurally by the page controller), followed by a full-width white page-title band reading “NEWSLETTER” with generous vertical padding. Below this, a centered, fixed-width main content container sits on the dark, textured background. Inside that container, use a two-column layout: the left, wider column holds the newsletter-signup-form (including the validation-summary, preferences-section, and submission-status-panel), while the right, narrower column is reserved as a sidebar zone for potential secondary content. The back-to-home-link sits at the top of the main content container, aligned to the left column, just under the title band.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s top-to-bottom structure: a full-width, dark header (with band logo and main navigation, owned structurally by the page controller), followed by a full-width white page-title band reading “NEWSLETTER” with generous vertical padding. Below this, a centered, fixed-width main content container sits on the dark, textured background. Inside that container, use a two-column layout: the left, wider column holds the newsletter-signup-form (including the validation-summary, preferences-section, and submission-status-panel), while the right, narrower column is reserved as a sidebar zone for potential secondary content. The back-to-home-link sits at the top of the main content container, aligned to the left column, just under the title band.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a 12-column grid with the main content container centered and spanning roughly 70–80% of the viewport width (e.g., max-width around 1040–1200px). Allocate about 65–70% of this container to the left column (form + related panels) and 30–35% to the right sidebar. Vertically, the header is relatively shallow, the white title band is tall and airy (roughly 15–20% of viewport height with 32–48px padding above and below the title), and the main content area occupies the remaining space with 32–48px spacing between the title band and the top of the form/back link. Within the form, apply 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical gaps between field groups and sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Follow the mockup’s moderate density: the header and title band are very spacious and minimal, giving a strong, clean first impression. The main content area is denser but still breathable: the left column form is clearly segmented (intro, validation summary, preferences section, submit area) with consistent vertical spacing and padding; the sidebar is more compact and modular if used, with 16–24px separation between modules. No element feels cramped; each block has enough negative space to stand alone against the dark background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the F-pattern. The eye first catches the band logo and navigation in the dark header (top-left to top-right), then drops to the high-contrast white page-title band with the large “NEWSLETTER” heading, centered. From there, attention moves down to the left column where the back-to-home-link and the form’s heading/intro appear, followed by either the validation-summary or submission-status-panel if present, and then the main form fields and preferences section. After scanning the left column, the viewer’s gaze naturally shifts rightward to the sidebar zone. The validation-summary and status-panel are positioned high within the left column so that feedback is noticed immediately after the title band.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page sits on a dark, textured background with a full-width, low-profile header at the very top featuring the band’s hand-drawn logo on the left and navigation spreading toward the right, all in a clean, minimalist style. Directly beneath, a bold, white page-title band stretches edge to edge: the word “NEWSLETTER” appears centered with generous vertical padding, creating a strong contrast against the dark page and echoing the mockup’s archive title area. Below this band, the main content container is centered on the page, a fixed-width block divided into a wider left column and a narrower right sidebar. In the left column, a small back-to-home text link sits at the top-left, followed by the newsletter signup form that nearly fills the column width, with clear sections for basic info, preferences, validation messages, and submission feedback. The validation summary and submission status panel stack near the top of the form area so they are immediately visible after the user interacts. The right sidebar column aligns with the top of the form and remains visually secondary, giving room for future widgets while currently functioning as quiet negative space that reinforces the two-column structure. Overall, the layout feels like a very focused, band-branded signup screen that marries the grunge/indie aesthetic with a rigorous, grid-based, modern structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-page-controller defines the entire page frame following the mockup’s structure. At the very top of the viewport, it reserves a full-width, dark header strip (approx. 64–80px tall) spanning 100% width; the band logo is conceptually aligned left with main navigation running horizontally to the right, though these are not separate components here. Immediately below, the controller places a full-width white page-title band across the viewport, with the word “NEWSLETTER” centered horizontally and vertically, padded by roughly 32–48px above and below the text, creating a bold, open block of white space between two dark zones. Beneath this band, the controller centers a fixed-width main content container on the textured black background; this container occupies about 70–80% of the viewport width and is horizontally centered with equal left and right margins. Inside the container, the controller establishes a 12-column grid and divides it into a left main column (about 8 columns, ~65–70% width) and a right sidebar column (about 4 columns, ~30–35% width). The top edge of this container sits about 32–40px below the bottom of the title band. At the very top-left of this container, aligned with the left column, it places the back-to-home-link. Directly under that link, the controller anchors the newsletter-signup-form in the left column, allowing it to stretch almost the full width of that column with 16–24px internal padding. The right column is reserved as a sidebar zone aligned to the top of the form; even if currently empty, it maintains the two-column visual structure. The validation-summary and submission-status-panel are both arranged within the form’s vertical flow but kept high in the left column, so they appear just beneath the form’s heading area when visible. The dark background extends beneath all content, with 48–64px of vertical margin below the main container before any footer area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the static layout frame for the page in the normal document flow; header and title band are full-width, fixed-position only in the sense of occupying the top of the layout (not sticky), while the main content container remains centered and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-signup-form is the dominant content block in the main area, positioned in the left column of the centered container. Its top edge sits roughly 16px below the back-to-home-link, aligning with the top of the right sidebar column. The form spans about 95% of the left column’s width, leaving a narrow gutter on each side to reveal the dark background and maintain separation from the sidebar. Inside the form, apply a dark, slightly lighter-than-background panel or subtle border to visually group its contents. Provide 20–24px padding on all sides, so fields do not touch the edges. At the top of the form, a succinct title (e.g., “Join the Newsletter”) and a short intro paragraph run full width; directly beneath this header area, the newsletter-validation-summary and/or newsletter-submission-status-panel will appear when needed. Below that, the form fields are stacked vertically: basic inputs (e.g., name, email, region, frequency) run in a one-column layout on narrow viewports, with optional two-column alignment for shorter fields on wider desktops, always respecting the grid. The newsletter-preferences-section is placed midway down the form, separated above and below by 16–24px of vertical space, and spans the full internal width. The submit button row sits toward the bottom, aligned left within the form content area with 16–24px of spacing above it from the last field. Overall, the form height is substantial, forming a strong vertical block that anchors the left side of the page, with each field group separated by 12–16px to avoid crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the left column, expanding vertically to fit its content and pushing the rest of the page downward; it scrolls normally with the page without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-preferences-section appears as a clearly defined sub-block within the newsletter-signup-form, positioned below the basic personal details (such as email and region) and above the email frequency and submit controls. It begins roughly 24px beneath the preceding form group, spanning the full internal width of the form panel. A small, high-contrast section heading (e.g., “What do you want to hear about?”) is left-aligned at the top of this section, with 8–12px of space below it and any descriptive helper text. Below the heading, the section’s controls (e.g., checkboxes or toggles for interests like new releases, tours, behind-the-scenes) are laid out in a two-column grid on wider screens, ensuring each option has at least 8–12px vertical spacing and clear tap targets; on narrower widths, they collapse into a single column while retaining the same vertical spacing. The entire section is visually grouped either by subtle internal dividers or consistent vertical gaps, making it feel like a cohesive module but still a natural continuation of the form. The bottom of the section leaves 16–20px of space before the next form block, maintaining the moderate content density of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline part of the form’s normal vertical flow; it expands vertically as options increase but does not change the overall column layout, adapting responsively from a two-column to a single-column internal structure on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-validation-summary is embedded near the top of the newsletter-signup-form so that any errors are immediately visible in the main F-pattern path. When present, it appears directly below the form’s title/intro area and above the first form field, spanning the full internal width of the form content (matching the form’s padding and alignment). The summary is styled as a compact, high-contrast block against the dark form background—e.g., a dark panel with a subtle red border or a slightly lighter background strip with a red accent icon on the left—so it stands out without breaking the minimalist, edgy aesthetic. It has about 12–16px padding on all sides, with each error line having 4–8px vertical spacing. Vertically, it adds roughly 40–80px of height when errors are present, pushing the rest of the form content down, but leaves about 16px of margin above the first field group to avoid visual crowding. When there are no errors, the component collapses and occupies no vertical space, allowing the form fields to move up under the intro text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered block within the form’s normal flow; it appears and disappears without affecting horizontal layout, simply expanding or collapsing vertically in place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-submission-status-panel sits in the same high-visibility region as the validation summary—near the top of the left column form area—so feedback after submission is instantly noticeable. When active, it is positioned inside the newsletter-signup-form, directly below the form title/intro and logically above the main form fields, using the same left and right alignment as the validation summary. It spans the full internal width of the form and is visually more celebratory or alert-like depending on success or failure: for success, a band-branded dark panel with a subtle glow or accent color (e.g., muted neon) and a bold heading (e.g., “You’re in!”) aligned left, followed by a short confirmation message; for non-critical info (e.g., pending confirmation), use a more neutral accent. The panel has 16–20px padding and approximately 16px margin above the first remaining form content or below the intro. On successful submission, the rest of the form fields can be visually softened or partially hidden below the panel (for example, dimming or collapsing them) so the status panel becomes the main focus in the left column. It thus occupies a similar visual weight as the large artwork in the mockup’s main column, drawing the eye after the title band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally displayed, block-level component within the form’s vertical flow; it expands the form’s height when shown. It does not float or stick, but it effectively replaces the upper part of the form content post-submission by pushing other elements downward or triggering their collapse.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-home-link appears as a subtle, text-based breadcrumb-style link positioned at the top-left of the main content container, just below the white title band and aligned to the left column. Its baseline sits about 16–24px below the bottom edge of the title band, leaving a comfortable white-to-dark transition area. Horizontally, it aligns with the left edge of the newsletter-signup-form and the main content grid, reinforcing the column structure. The link is relatively small but clear—perhaps an all-caps label like “← BACK TO HOME” or a simple left-arrow icon plus text—using a muted light gray or off-white that stands out against the dark background but remains secondary to the bold “NEWSLETTER” title above. There is 8–12px of vertical space between the link and the top of the form, so the link feels associated with the page context rather than the first form field. On scroll, the link moves naturally with the main content, always preceding the form in the vertical reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element at the top of the main content container; it participates in the normal document flow, acting as a simple anchor link without sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The newsletter-submission-service operates entirely behind the scenes with no direct visual presence on the page. It is conceptually associated with the newsletter-signup-form, listening for form submissions and handling communication with the backend service. Visually, it does not occupy any space, draw borders, or alter the grid; its effects are only observable through changes in the newsletter-submission-status-panel and the newsletter-validation-summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-spatial component with no rendered footprint in the layout; it does not participate in the document flow or affect positioning, acting purely as a functional adapter invoked by form events.

name: About & Contact
title: About & Contact
description: Page for viewing band information, member details, contact options, and support/privacy information, including a simple contact form.

core_features: Load and display the band biography, members, and contact-related content using getAboutContent on page load.
Provide a structured layout with anchorable sections for biography, members, contact information, contact form, and privacy/terms/support content.
Allow users to submit inquiries via a contact form that sends data through submitContactForm and displays success or error feedback.
Show contact email and booking email values from getAboutContent and provide interactions (e.g., mailto links or copy actions).

Expose support and policy-related information (including links or summaries for privacy and terms) and allow direct navigation to that section via the section URL parameter (e.g., section=privacy_terms).
Handle internal navigation between sections by updating and reacting to the section URL parameter and scrolling to the corresponding anchors.
Provide navigation back to the home page (index.html) via a back/home/logo link.
user_workflows:
View band information:
1. User opens about.html (no section parameter).
2. The page initializes and the about-content-loader component calls getAboutContent.
3. Once data is returned, the biography section renders the bioHtml content.
4. The members list section renders each member from members, showing name, role, and photoUrl.
5. The user scrolls or uses in-page navigation to move between the biography, members, contact, and privacy/terms sections.

Open About & Contact from the footer:
1. User clicks the “About & Contact” link in the footer on any page.
2. Browser navigates to about.html without a section parameter.
3. The page loads, getAboutContent is called, and all sections become available.
4. The user can then scroll or use section links to access contact or privacy/terms content.

Open Privacy & Terms directly from the footer:
1. User clicks the “Privacy & Terms” footer link on any page.
2. Browser navigates to about.html?section=privacy_terms.
3. On initialization, the section-anchor-router reads section=privacy_terms and sets the active section state.
4. After getAboutContent finishes loading, the router scrolls to the privacy/terms/support section anchor.
5. The user reviews the privacy/terms content and can then navigate to other sections using in-page links.

Send a contact inquiry via the form:
1. User opens about.html (optionally with section=contact if linked directly) and the page loads.
2. getAboutContent is called; contact-related values (contactEmail, bookingEmail, supportInfoHtml) are populated.
3. The section-anchor-router moves the viewport to the contact section if section=contact, otherwise the user scrolls there manually.
4. In the contact-form component, the user fills in name, email, topic, and message.
5. The user submits the form, triggering submitContactForm with the current input values.
6. While waiting for the response, the form is set to a “submitting” state and inputs can be disabled.
7. On success (response.success is true), the contact-form-status component shows a confirmation using the returned messageId.
8. On failure (response.success is false or an error occurs), the contact-form-status shows an error state and allows the user to retry.

Use email contact info directly instead of the form:
1. User reaches the contact information section (by scrolling or via section=contact).
2. The contact-info-section component displays contactEmail and bookingEmail from getAboutContent.
3. User clicks an email address, which triggers a mailto link action, or uses a copy interaction to copy the address.
4. User completes the email in their own email client.

View support and store policy information:
1. User arrives on about.html, either directly or via a page such as store.html that links to about.html?section=support or about.html?section=privacy_terms.
2. The router reads the section parameter and, if it corresponds to the support/privacy area, marks that section as active.
3. getAboutContent loads and the support-and-policies-section displays supportInfoHtml, along with site policies including or linking to privacy and terms content.
4. Router scrolls to the support/privacy section anchor.
5. User can follow any internal links (for example to store or tour support details) or return to home.

Navigate back to home:
1. User clicks the logo or a “Back to Home” navigation element available on the About & Contact page.
2. The back-nav-link component intercepts the click.
3. Browser navigates to index.html without additional parameters (or with any default parameters defined globally).
interactions:
Page load:
- Event: window.onload / component onInit.
- Action: about-content-loader calls getAboutContent.
- Result: Shared page state is populated with bioHtml, members, contactEmail, bookingEmail, and supportInfoHtml; dependent components re-render.

URL section handling on load:
- Event: On initial render, the section-anchor-router reads the section query parameter from window.location.
- Action: It sets the internal activeSection state (e.g., to 'privacy_terms', 'contact', 'members', or 'bio') and queues a scroll-to-anchor once content has rendered.
- Result: The viewport scrolls to the corresponding anchor element if one matches the section value.

In-page section navigation:
- Event: User clicks an in-page navigation link for a section (e.g., band bio, members, contact, privacy/terms).
- Action: section-anchor-router updates the URL’s section query parameter via pushState/replaceState and scrolls to the linked section anchor.
- Result: The visible section changes without a full page reload, and the URL remains in sync for deep-linking or sharing.

Contact form input:
- Event: User types in the name, email, topic, and message fields.
- Action: contact-form updates its local form state on each onChange event, and may run basic client-side validations (e.g., email format, required fields) before allowing submission.
- Result: The submit button is enabled only when required fields pass validation; error messages may appear inline for invalid fields.

Contact form submission:
- Event: User clicks submit on the contact form.
- Action: contact-form validates current values; if valid, it calls submitContactForm with { name, email, topic, message } and switches to a submitting state.
- Result: On success, contact-form-status receives { success: true, messageId } and displays confirmation; the form may clear or keep values depending on implementation. On failure, contact-form-status receives { success: false } or an error flag and shows an error message, allowing the user to correct and resubmit.

Contact info interactions:
- Event: User clicks on contactEmail or bookingEmail in the contact-info-section.
- Action: Browser is directed to a mailto: link constructed from the selected email, or a copy-to-clipboard action is triggered if such behavior is implemented.
- Result: User can compose a direct email or paste the email address elsewhere.

Support/Privacy section links:
- Event: User clicks a link within the support-and-policies-section (e.g., another anchor link within the page or an external URL).
- Action: For internal anchors, section-anchor-router updates the section parameter and scrolls; for external URLs, normal browser navigation occurs.
- Result: User navigates between relevant policy/support content or leaves to another page as intended.

Back to Home navigation:
- Event: User clicks the logo or a “Back to Home” link.
- Action: back-nav-link triggers navigation to index.html (e.g., setting window.location.href = 'index.html').
- Result: Home page is loaded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary cross-session/page state is managed via the URL section query parameter, not via localStorage. On every render of about.html, the page-level router reads window.location.search and extracts section. Supported values correspond to internal anchor IDs such as 'bio', 'members', 'contact', 'privacy_terms', or other predefined sections that exist in the layout. If section is present and matches a known section ID, activeSection is initialized to that value, and after getAboutContent has loaded and dependent components have rendered, the router scrolls to the corresponding anchor element. Internal navigation between sections (e.g., clicking a link to the contact section or privacy/terms section) updates the section query parameter via history.pushState or history.replaceState, ensuring that the current section is always reflected in the URL. This allows deep-linking; for example, footer links can point to about.html?section=privacy_terms so users land directly on the privacy/terms area. No user-specific data or contact form responses are persisted in URL parameters; the result of submitContactForm is held in transient page state within contact-form and contact-form-status. Any other internal UI state (such as validation errors or the submitting flag) is maintained in component state only and reset when the page is reloaded.

components.functionality: On page initialization, call getAboutContent once and store the returned bioHtml, members, contactEmail, bookingEmail, and supportInfoHtml in a shared page-level state object that other components subscribe to. Handles loading and error states for data-dependent sections.

Manages anchor-based routing within the About & Contact page using the section URL parameter. On load, reads ?section=... and sets activeSection; after content is available, scrolls to the matching section. When users click internal section links, updates the section parameter in the URL and scrolls accordingly.
Displays the band biography and history content. Renders rich text from bioHtml and exposes an anchor with a stable ID so the router can scroll to it when section=bio is used.
Shows a list of band members with their names, roles, and photos based on the members array from getAboutContent. Provides an anchor so it can be targeted when section=members is specified.

Displays contact and booking email information retrieved from getAboutContent. Provides interactions such as clicking to open the user's email client or copying an email address, and can be anchored for section-based navigation.

Collects user inquiries to the band or management. Contains fields for name, email, topic, and message. Validates input and submits the data via submitContactForm. Manages submitting, success, and error states and coordinates with the contact-form-status component for feedback.

Presents the outcome of the contact form submission. When submitContactForm returns, it shows a success message (optionally referencing the messageId) or an error state prompting the user to retry or adjust inputs.

Shows support-related information and any site policy content that belongs on this page, including links or summaries for privacy and terms. Uses supportInfoHtml from getAboutContent for support content and hosts any additional privacy/terms copy or anchors. Can be directly navigated to via section=privacy_terms or other relevant section keys.
Provides a compact set of navigation links (e.g., Bio, Members, Contact, Privacy/Terms) that update the section URL parameter and trigger the router to scroll to the relevant section.
Allows users to return to the website’s main page. Typically attached to a logo or a text link labeled as a back/home action, and navigates to index.html when clicked.

layout.grid_system: Likely a centered, fixed-width container on a 12-column grid, with a two-column content layout (wide main column + narrower sidebar).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark header (logo + global nav, outside this component list), then a full-width white page-title band labeled “ABOUT & CONTACT”. Below this, a centered fixed-width main container is split into a two-column layout. The left column is the main content stack (band bio, members, contact info, form, support/policies), while the right column is a sidebar containing navigation utilities (back link and section navigation). Invisible functional components (loader and section-anchor router) are layered within this structure without disrupting the visual flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark header (logo + global nav, outside this component list), then a full-width white page-title band labeled “ABOUT & CONTACT”. Below this, a centered fixed-width main container is split into a two-column layout. The left column is the main content stack (band bio, members, contact info, form, support/policies), while the right column is a sidebar containing navigation utilities (back link and section navigation). Invisible functional components (loader and section-anchor router) are layered within this structure without disrupting the visual flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The white page-title band spans the full viewport width and roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, with the title centered and ample vertical padding. The main content container below is constrained to a fixed width (e.g., ~960–1140px) and centered. Inside it, the left main column takes about 65–70% of the container width, and the right sidebar takes about 30–35%, separated by a generous gutter (~32px). Within each column, modules have internal padding of around 16–24px and vertical gaps of 16–32px between them in line with the spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: The top of the page (header and title band) is very spacious with minimal elements. The main content column is moderately dense: stacked sections with clear section headings, readable line length, and solid spacing between sections (around 24–32px). The sidebar is compact but not cramped; section navigation links are grouped into a small number of tight, scan-friendly blocks with 12–16px padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between blocks, mirroring the mockup’s sidebar widgets.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Eye flow starts in the upper-left at the band’s logo (header), then moves right along the main navigation, then drops down to the centered “ABOUT & CONTACT” title in the white band. From there, attention moves down into the left main column, first encountering the band bio, then the members, contact details, contact form, and finally support/policies. The right sidebar supports an F-pattern: after taking in the main left content, users glance right to the back navigation and section nav links, which align vertically with the main column sections. The section-anchor router ensures smooth vertical jumps between sections when sidebar links are clicked, reinforcing this F-shaped scanning behavior.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a textured, nearly black background with a high-contrast, white page-title band below a slim dark header. Centered in the viewport, a fixed-width content area is split into a wide left column on a dark canvas and a narrower right sidebar. Large, bold section headings and ample breathing room give the left column a confident, editorial feel: the band bio appears first, followed by a panel-style band member list, then a concise contact info block, a prominent contact form with its status banner directly above it, and finally a more subdued support and policies section. The right sidebar aligns vertically with this main stack, starting with a subtle back link and a compact module of section navigation links that mirror the order of the main sections. Vertical spacing and consistent alignment create a clear hierarchy, while the dark, slightly grungy aesthetic and strong contrast keep the layout on-brand for a modern music band site.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader is logically anchored to the main content container that sits below the white page-title band. When active, it appears as a semi-transparent dark overlay covering the entire left main column (band bio, members, contact info, form, and support/policies) and slightly dimming the right sidebar, centered horizontally within the fixed-width container. A minimal loading indicator (e.g., a spinner or text) is centered both vertically and horizontally within this overlaid region. When inactive, it takes up no visible space and does not affect the positioning of other components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an overlay that sits above the main content and sidebar within the content container; positioned fixed or absolutely relative to the container, covering roughly 100% of its width and height while active, otherwise not rendered or visually hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-anchor router is an invisible structural layer aligned with the left main column. Conceptually, it associates anchor positions with the top edges of each major section (band bio, band members, contact info, contact form, support and policies). It does not add visible elements but ensures that when a section link in the right sidebar is clicked, the viewport scrolls so that the corresponding section sits just below the page-title band, maintaining consistent visual alignment and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and occupies no additional layout space; it hooks into the natural scroll positions of the main column sections and adjusts scroll behavior (e.g., smooth scrolling with a slight offset to account for the page-title band).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The band bio section is the first visible block in the left main column, positioned immediately below the main container’s top margin under the white title band. It spans the full width of the left column (approximately 65–70% of the main container width) with equal left and right padding (16–24px) against the dark background. A bold section heading (e.g., “Band Bio”) sits at the top-left of this block with generous top padding, followed by the bio text in a comfortable line length. There is a clear bottom margin of roughly 24–32px separating it from the band members list below, giving it a prominent, lead-story feel similar to the large artwork area in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow as the first stacked section in the left column; it expands vertically based on content while maintaining fixed horizontal alignment with the main column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The band members list sits directly beneath the band bio in the left main column, separated by a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full width of the left column with consistent horizontal padding matching the bio. A section heading (e.g., “Members”) is aligned to the top-left of this block, with the list of members arranged in a vertical stack or a tight grid (2 columns on large screens), each item aligned to the left and using 8–16px vertical spacing between members. The entire module reads as a compact, card-like block within the main column, with enough breathing room at the bottom (around 24px) before the contact info section that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows directly after the band-bio-section within the left column; it grows vertically as needed and maintains alignment within the main grid without any fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact info section is placed below the band members list in the left main column, with a vertical gap of around 24–32px. It uses the same full-width left-column footprint and padding as the previous sections, keeping the column visually unified. A clear heading (e.g., “Contact Info”) appears at the top-left, with contact details (email, booking, press, etc.) stacked in short lines or small clusters. The block is moderately compact, functioning as a bridge between the descriptive content above and the interactive contact form below. A bottom margin of 24–32px separates it from the contact form status banner.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the natural vertical stack in the left column; expands vertically based on content and remains fully responsive within that column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form status banner sits directly above the contact form in the left main column, aligned to the same left and right edges as the other sections but visually more compact. It occupies the full width of the left column and appears only when there is a status to display (e.g., success or error). When visible, it adds a slender, high-contrast strip with 12–16px internal padding and a small top margin (~8–16px) from the contact info section, immediately leading the eye into the form below. The bottom edge of this banner aligns tightly with the top of the contact form, with minimal vertical gap (8–12px) so the two are clearly associated.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the normal document flow between the contact-info-section and contact-form, pushing content down when shown; when hidden, it collapses so no extra vertical space is consumed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact form is one of the most visually commanding modules in the left main column. It sits immediately below the contact-form-status (or directly below contact-info-section when no status is present), with a vertical gap of about 8–16px. The form spans the full width of the left column and uses generous internal padding (around 24px) to give fields room to breathe against the dark background. Form fields are left-aligned and stacked vertically with 12–16px gaps, and the submit button anchors the bottom-right or bottom-left edge, depending on the overall alignment strategy. The block has a noticeable bottom margin of 32px before the support-and-policies-section, giving it focus as the main interactive element on the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline in the left column, expanding vertically as fields require. It remains fixed within the document flow without sticky or floating behavior, ensuring predictable scrolling down to the support/policies content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support and policies section is the last major block in the left main column, positioned beneath the contact form with a vertical gap of roughly 32px to subtly separate it as secondary information. It occupies the full width of the left column with the same side padding as prior sections. A heading (e.g., “Support & Policies” or separate subheadings for support, privacy, terms) appears at the top-left, with the content in shorter paragraphs or bullet lists. This block is slightly less visually heavy than the bio and form—using normal text size and moderate line spacing—so it reads as the final reference section at the bottom of the page. Generous bottom padding and margin provide a soft visual endpoint before the page footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits at the end of the main content stack, extending vertically as needed; no special positioning, simply following the natural scrollable flow of the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation menu is a compact, stacked module in the right sidebar, aligned with the top portion of the main content area. It is positioned just below the back-nav-link, with a vertical gap of around 16–24px, and occupies the full width of the sidebar (approximately 30–35% of the main container). A small label or heading (e.g., “Sections”) sits at the top-left of this module, followed by vertical text links (e.g., About, Members, Contact, Support) each taking a full row and left-aligned. Links have tight internal spacing (8–12px vertical padding) and 8–16px gaps between them, mirroring the compact feel of sidebar widgets in the mockup. The module maintains a clear right margin from the container edge and is vertically aligned so that its first link roughly lines up with the upper portion of the band-bio-section in the main column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored in the right sidebar; on larger screens it may use sticky positioning so that, after scrolling past the top padding, the menu remains visible along the right side while the user scrolls through the main sections. On smaller screens, it falls back to standard flow above or below main content as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back navigation link sits at the very top of the right sidebar, aligned to the right column’s left edge and positioned horizontally in line with the upper part of the band-bio-section. It appears as a small, understated text link or pill-style button (e.g., “← Back to Archive”) with 8–12px internal padding and minimal vertical height, visually lighter than the section nav menu below. There is a comfortable top margin (around 24–32px) between the bottom of the white page-title band and this link, preserving the spacious feel beneath the title. A gap of 16–24px separates this back link from the section-nav-links module beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right sidebar’s normal flow; it scrolls with the page unless the entire sidebar is given sticky behavior, in which case it maintains its position at the top of the sidebar stack while the user scrolls through the main content.
